The development of multilevel inverters is the viable solution for electric vehicle power systems, with lower harmonic distortions, enhanced power quality, and the ability to transfer power in both directions. The present research is a revolutionary multisource multilevel inverter (MLI) with fewer switches. Suggested multilevel inverter utilizes two asymmetric battery sources. This distinctive arrangement facilitates the production of a more refined seven-level AC voltage output quality is improved with decreased harmonic content, addressing the rigorous power quality necessity of the electric vehicle requirement. This research provides comprehensive simulation outputs derived from MATLAB-Simulink, regarding the suggested MLI’s performance under varied operational situations and load circumstances. The simulation results involve examining the output voltage waveforms and conducting a harmonic analysis.

title: AI-Powered Hypertension Prediction: Leveraging Machine Learning for Early Detection
authors: Nidal Turab, Shahad Abu Hedeel, Batool Al Zubaidi, Suhila Abuowaida, Ahmad Aburomman
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Machine learning algorithms can enhance early diagnosis, treatment, and patient outcomes for hypertension, a significant worldwide health concern. This study classifies the presence of hypertension or not (yes, no) into classes using a Kaggle dataset that includes demographic and physical features. To assess their ability for prediction, the model integrates classifiers built using machine learning techniques such as Support Vector Machine (SVM), Naive Bayes (NB), and Random Forest (RF). The effectiveness of each classifier was evaluated using performance metrics such as area Under Curve (AUC), accuracy, F1-score, and precision. The maximum Area Under the Curve (AUC) was 98.50% for the RF method, 97.40% with NB, and 91.70% for SVM. These findings show that sophisticated machine learning algorithms may be used to accurately forecast hypertension, which will help medical professionals make well-informed decisions about patient care.

title: FakeVision: A Vision Transformer Framework for Deepfake Detection
authors: Shriya Shukla, Ishan Bhalla, Pratistha Sharma, Abhishek Singhal
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: This paper proposes a family of frameworks for the detection of DeepFake and other techniques, which can generate synthetic media. These usually refer to techniques such as Face Swap, Face-to-Face, and Neural Textures. The basic idea of the work is to use a Vision Transformer, specifically Swin Transformer, along with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). The CNNs are good at capturing spatial and frequency artifacts that are present during manipulation. The Swin Transformer captures both local and global visual patterns efficiently using shifted attention windows. A bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) analyzes temporal information. This hybrid model helps in covering every inconsistency from spatial to frequency to temporal, resulting in overall generalization across all synthetic content generation techniques. The model is trained on FaceForensics++ dataset, and evaluated on that and CelebDFv2, which ensures that different types of manipulation techniques are covered. The evaluation of the model is done primarily based on Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC), which provides us with an assessment of detection capability and robustness. This work reveals that the proposed family of swin-based architecture provides decent intra-dataset and great cross-dataset performance (0.9822 and 0.8478 are the highest respective AUC values) when detecting deepfakes in contrast to existing models, that too with varying computational needs.

title: IPOS LLC Resonant Converter with Reconfigurable Output for 400V and 800V Architectures in EV Charging
authors: Yu Zuo, Yang Li, Qingcheng Sui, Diego Bernal Cobaleda, Bangli Du, Jiaze Kong, Xiaobing Shen, Wilmar Martinez
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Electric vehicle charging is transitioning from 400V to 800V architectures, enabling higher power and faster charging. Off-board DC-DC converters are required to accommodate ultra-wide voltage and power ranges to ensure compatibility with both architectures. Currently, wide output voltage ranges are typically achieved through topology modifications, two-stage conversions, or hybrid modulation, with series-parallel modular operation commonly used to increase output power. Different from existing approaches, this paper introduces an input-parallel output-serial converter with a simple on-off relay switch, enabling two distinct operating modes. This design not only extends the output voltage range without requiring auxiliary circuits or complex modulation but also preserves the advantages of modular operation. Additionally, the converter provides automatic voltage balancing for the series-connected output side, while coupled inductors facilitate current sharing, even with intentional inconsistencies in resonant parameter values on the parallel input side. Furthermore, the converter’s control is simplified through pulse frequency modulation within a narrow switching frequency range, leveraging the characteristics of LLC resonant converters to regulate the output voltage for both 400V and 800V charging systems. Moreover, soft-switching is maintained across the entire voltage range. Finally, experimental results from a 1kW prototype with a 400V input and a 200-800V output validate the proposed approach, achieving a peak efficiency of 93.7%, and above 92% overall voltage range.

title: Low DC-link Voltage Operation of Three-Phase Bidirectional AC-DC Converter with Fixed Voltage Modulation Index
authors: Cheolmin Hwang, Seongwon Lee, Jung-Ik Ha
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The operation of conventional AC-DC converters is premised on a boosted DC-link voltage to ensure an adequate voltage margin. As a result, expanding the operating range toward a lower DC-link voltage has been overlooked. A lower DC-link voltage reduces switching losses in both the AC-DC converter and the subsequent load converter, while also enhancing low-speed efficiency in variable frequency drive applications. To facilitate low DC-link voltage control, the traditional decoupled d- and q-axis current controls operate in the overmodulation (OVM) region to maximize voltage utilization. However, operating in the OVM region causes an error between the voltage reference and the converter's output voltage, which then introduces a disturbance into the current control loop. This paper proposes the one-degree-of-freedom (1-DOF) control that utilizes the voltage angle as the sole DOF, thereby improving current control performance in low DC-link voltage region. Furthermore, the paper evaluates the voltage margin in rectification and regenerative modes and proposes an appropriate DC-link voltage reference modification strategy to maximize the voltage utilization.

title: Active Power Buffer for Smoothing GPU Workload Transients in Data Center Power Delivery
authors: Lei Gu, Ahmed Nabih, Sudhir Kudva, Mostafa Mosa, Rose Abramson, Youssef Elasser, C. Thomas Gray
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: GPU workloads in data centers often produce sharp power transients, up to 1.75× the rated power, lasting several milliseconds, which strain upstream converters and grid infrastructure. To mitigate these effects, we propose an active power buffer architecture using compact bidirectional converters with high-density capacitors placed near GPUs. These buffers locally absorb or inject energy during load fluctuations, reducing stress on upstream power delivery systems. This paper presents high-frequency control schemes for the active power buffer and validates their performance in a proof-of-concept setup. Experimental results demonstrate a reduction of up to 45% in peak power drawn from the line, highlighting the potential of this approach to enhance scalability in AI-driven data center power systems.

title: Silicon Microring Resonator Gated by High Mobility Hydrogen-Doped Indium Oxide
authors: Sudipta Biswas, Saika Muntaha Bari, Alan X. Wang
abstract: We present a silicon MOSCAP microring resonator with high-mobility hydrogen-doped indium oxide, achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$175 \text{pm} / \mathrm{V}$</tex> wavelength tuning efficiency, a Q-factor <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>10000$</tex>, and sub-volt phase control with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\pi} \mathrm{L}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.09 ~\mathrm{V} \cdot ~\text{cm}$</tex>. The device shows great potential for energy-efficient optical communication.

title: DM Conducted EMI Noise Prediction for Variable-Frequency, Variable-On-Time Interleaved Integrated TCM Totem-Pole PFC Converters
authors: Zhehui Guo, Yusheng Chen, Ripun Phukan, Misha Kumar, Peter Barbosa
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents a numerical modeling method based on the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) for predicting the differential-mode (DM) conducted electromagnetic interference (EMI) noise of two-phase interleaved integrated triangular current mode (iTCM) totem-pole PFC converters. The proposed method incorporates the time-varying switching-frequency and on-time characteristics of iTCM operation, models the drain-to-source voltages as noise sources, and uses STFT to extract their instantaneous harmonic spectra over the line cycle. The dominant harmonic contributions and frequency-domain peak envelopes are identified for both single-phase and interleaved operation, providing detailed insight into the noise-generation mechanism. DM noise transfer gains (DMTGs) are analytically derived for both single-phase and interleaved circuits. The predicted DM noise spectra are validated on a 4-kW iTCM totem-pole PFC prototype. Experimental results show close agreement with the predictions, with peak DM noise measured at 100.78 dBμV at 137 kHz for single-phase operation and reduced to 91.32 dBμV at 274.7 kHz under interleaving. The proposed STFT-based modeling framework offers an accurate and efficient tool for DM EMI noise prediction and DM filter pre-design over varying input-voltage and load operating conditions.

title: Segmentation From Partial Views Via Patient-Specific Shape Priors
authors: Jared Vicory, Dženan Zukić, Balazs Vagvolgyi, Peter Kazanzides, Andinet Enquobahrie, Emad Boctor
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Precision surgical interventions rely on accurate integration of preoperative and intraoperative imaging to guide clinical decision-making and improve patient outcomes. Traditionally, most 3D imaging modalities capture the entirety of the target object, allowing for the segmentation of its entire volume. However, some medical imaging devices trade full field-of-view for other considerations, such as size and ability to access the target organ in unique ways. This is particularly true of ultrasound, where imaging deeper organs from outside the body is not viable due to attenuation, thus motivating the need for alternative imaging strategies. In scenarios such as image-guided intervention, it is often necessary to put this partial view of the object in its full anatomical context, such as alignment to a pre-operative MRI. We propose a method for jointly segmenting the portion of the object visible in the acquired image and estimating its full shape. We do this by combining a pre-trained shape prior with a patient-specific expectation of organ shape acquired via pre-operative imaging. In a simulated dataset for prostate MRI/US fusion, we show the ability to accurately estimate the prostate shape from ultrasound images capturing only a fraction of its total volume.

title: Performance Analysis of Cloud-Based Disaster Recovery for Workday Applications
authors: Monu Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: The increasing reliance on cloud-native enterprise services such as Workday has intensified the need for robust disaster recovery mechanisms to ensure business continuity and service availability. As a leading provider of cloud-based Human Capital Management (HCM) and Financial Management solutions, Workday supports mission-critical business operations that require high availability, strong data integrity, and rapid recovery in the event of system failures or large-scale outages. Traditional off-site disaster recovery approaches are often constrained by high infrastructure costs, limited scalability, and prolonged recovery times, making them unsuitable for Workday’s dynamic, multi-tenant, and data-intensive cloud environment. Cloud-based disaster recovery, commonly referred to as Disaster Recovery as a Service (DRaaS), has emerged as an effective alternative by leveraging virtualization, automation, and geographically distributed cloud infrastructure. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of cloud-based disaster recovery solutions in the context of Workday, focusing on system architecture, recovery mechanisms, and performance evaluation. A Workday-oriented disaster recovery framework is proposed that integrates automated data replication, orchestration, and continuous monitoring to achieve low Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) while adhering to enterprise security and availability requirements. Comparative analysis demonstrates that cloud-based disaster recovery outperforms traditional approaches in terms of cost efficiency, elastic scalability, high availability, and operational resilience. Key challenges, including data security, regulatory compliance, and performance optimization in multi-region cloud deployments, are also discussed, along with directions for future research aimed at enhancing disaster recovery capabilities in Workday’s evolving cloud ecosystem.

title: A Proximal Approach for Stain Separation and Normalization of Whole-Slide Histopathological Images
authors: Aymen Sadraoui, Astrid Laurent-Bellue, Mounir Kaaniche, Amel Benazza-Benyahia, Catherine Guettier, Jean-Christophe Pesquet
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Stain variability in histopathological images presents a major obstacle to developing accurate and reliable computer-aided diagnosis systems. In this paper, we introduce a novel stain normalization framework based on a stain separation proximal algorithm. Specifically, we first propose a new stain separation method that extracts individual stain components from a given stained image. Subsequently, we perform stain normalization by aligning the separated source stains with those of a target reference image. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively reduces inter-image color variations while preserving the essential structural details of the tissue.

title: An Empirical Evaluation of Open-Source LLMs for Philippine Labor Law Question Answering Using Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Cedric Isaiah D. Bautista, Alisandro N. Domingo, John Paul Benedict R. Monteon, Lysa V. Comia
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: This study evaluates the effectiveness of opensource large language models (LLMs) for answering questions about the Philippine Labor Code using a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework. Three instruction-tuned models-LLaMA 3.1 8B, Qwen2.5 7B, and Gemma 2 9B-were deployed within an identical hybrid retrieval pipeline combining BM25, dense embeddings, reciprocal rank fusion, and cross-encoder reranking. Evaluation was conducted on 30 benchmark queries (27 in-scope and 3 out-of-scope) using metrics including semantic similarity, faithfulness, answer relevancy, citation accuracy, LLM-as-a-Judge scoring, and response latency. Retrieval performance was identical across models (Recall $\approx 0.99$; Precision $\approx 0.31$), while differences emerged in generation quality. Gemma 29 B achieved the highest semantic similarity (0.859), citation accuracy (0.935), and judge score (0.733), indicating stronger legal grounding. Qwen2.5 7B showed the fastest responses (8.89 s) and highest faithfulness, while LLaMA 3.1 8B achieved slightly higher answer relevancy. All models correctly refused 100% of out-ofscope queries. The results demonstrate that RAG-enhanced open-source LLMs can provide reliable and accessible legal information systems for Philippine labor law assistance.

title: Detecting AI-Generated Text Using Multilingual DistilBERT Fine-Tuning on the Kaggle AI vs Human Dataset
authors: Ahlam Migdady, Riyad AlShalabi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: With the recent developments in the field of Generative Artificial Intelligence, it has become possible for the system to produce text that is nearly indistinguishable from the text written by humans, and this has led to several serious concerns with regards to academic integrity and the authenticity of digital content. This has, therefore, led to the growing need for the development of systems that can detect the difference between text written by humans and text written by AI. This paper proposes a solution to this problem by using an intelligent model for classifying text using the Multilingual DistilBERT model, a lean transformer model that has been designed to retain high language understanding capabilities while still maintaining efficiency and fast inference times. The approach takes a balanced dataset of 20,000 pieces of text, half of which are human-generated while the other half are AI-generated. Experimental evaluation on an unseen set of 4,000 samples showed that the model performed outstandingly. The overall accuracy of the model is 98.22%, and the AUC value obtained from the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve is 0.9980, which is outstanding in distinguishing human-written texts from AI-generated texts.

title: TopoGate: Quality-Aware Topology-Stabilized Gated Fusion for Longitudinal Low-Dose CT New-Lesion Prediction
authors: Seungik Cho
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Longitudinal low-dose CT follow-ups vary in noise, reconstruction kernels, and registration quality. These differences destabilize subtraction images and can trigger false “newlesion” alarms. We present TopoGate, a lightweight model that combines the follow-up appearance view with the subtraction view and controls their influence through a learned, quality-aware gate. The gate is driven by three case-specific signals: CT appearance quality, registration consistency, and stability of anatomical topology measured with topological metrics. On the NLST-New-Lesion-LongCT cohort comprising 152 pairs from 122 patients, TopoGate improves discrimination and calibration over single-view baselines, achieving an area under the ROC curve of 0.65 with a standard deviation of 0.05 and a Brier score of 0.14. Removing corrupted or low-quality pairs, identified by the quality scores, further increases the area under the ROC curve from 0.62 to 0.68 and reduces the Brier score from 0.14 to 0.12. The gate responds predictably to degradation, placing more weight on appearance when noise grows, which mirrors radiologist practice. The approach is simple, interpretable, and practical for reliable longitudinal LDCT triage.

title: Dual-Domain Mapping: Sensor Fusion of UAV-LiDAR and Ground Penetrating Radar for High-Fidelity 3D Utility Mapping
authors: Nurul Izzati Abd Karim, Amer Hamzah Taib, Wan Anom Wan Aris
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Accurate mapping of underground utilities is essential for safe urban development but remains challenging due to the separation between surface observations and subsurface geophysical measurements. This study presents a sensor-fusion framework integrating Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) with Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) to generate a unified threedimensional geospatial representation of surface and subsurface infrastructure. The workflow establishes a semantic linkage between surface utility indicators such as manholes and valve covers extracted from LiDAR data and subsurface pipe or cable reflections detected from radargrams. Field data were collected at Lingkaran Ilmu, Universiti Teknologi Malaysia using a RIEGL VUX-1UAV LiDAR system and an IDS GeoRadar platform. Ground Control Points (GCPs) measured using GNSS were used to georeference both datasets and establish spatial correspondence between the aerial and ground surveys. The integrated processing workflow combines LiDAR-derived terrain modelling with GPR interpretation to enable accurate spatial registration of subsurface utilities. The resulting fused model achieved a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 0.08 m, demonstrating sub-decimetre positional accuracy suitable for high-quality utility mapping. The integration also reduced interpretation ambiguity by linking radar anomalies with contextual surface features. Although the test site provided adequate GNSS coverage, the framework can be adapted for GNSS-limited environments through the integration of inertial navigation and SLAM-based positioning techniques. The results demonstrate that combining UAV-LiDAR surface modelling with GPR subsurface detection provides a reliable approach for high-fidelity three-dimensional underground mapping.

title: Integrated Planar Transformer with Embedded Zero-Sequence Inductance for Active Power Decoupling in Three-Phase Dual Active Bridge Converters
authors: Yi Han, Satyam Sa, Olivier Trescases
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The power density of battery chargers is constrained by the large dc-link capacitors required to buffer double-line-frequency (DLF) ripple power, which limit achievable volumetric power density and lifetime. Active power decoupling (APD) can reduce the capacitor volume requirement, but it typically relies on an additional inductor. The zero-sequence inductance (ZSI) of the three-phase (TP) transformer is shown to serve as the APD inductor in a TP dual-active-bridge (DAB) converter, enabling up to 40% reduction in capacitor volume when buffering half of the ripple power, thereby motivating a practical implementation. A planar TP transformer with integrated ZSI is presented for a 300-kHz GaN-based TP-DAB converter. A five-limb gapped core structure and a low-capacitance PCB winding layout were developed to achieve the required ZSI and support high-frequency switching. The flux distribution was analyzed via finite-element simulation, and the analytical ZSI model was validated using hardware measurements from 10-turn and 14-turn prototypes. Experimental results from a 2-kW dc-dc prototype demonstrate effective ripple-power buffering through the transformer neutral port, with the higher-ZSI 14-turn design halving the APD current ripple and incurring only a 3% efficiency penalty relative to the 96.4% baseline. These results verify that the transformer consolidates power-transfer and decoupling functions, enabling APD without a separate inductor.

title: Inverse Laplacian Pyramid For Image Generation With Limited Data_supp1-3694739.pdf
authors: Yikui Zhai
abstract: This paper demonstrates that a robust and data-efficient image generation model can be achieved by simulating the inverse Laplacian pyramid through a neural network. The key insight is that under appropriate parameterization, the score function of this neural network is approximately proportional to the negative high-frequency residuals in the forward degradation process. We refer to this framework as the Score-Matching Adversarial Model (SMAM). The model consists of two main components: the Laplacian Pyramid Discriminator (LPD) and the Inverse Laplacian Pyramid Generator (ILPG). The LPD includes a Laplacian pyramid decomposition module, which enables both the forward degradation process and multi-scale image discrimination for real and generated images. The ILPG, in turn, generates the prior of the forward degradation process as well as the high-frequency residuals needed for the inverse Laplacian pyramid reconstruction. This allows the Laplacian pyramid reconstruction module to recover a complete image from the generated data. SMAM is grounded in a solid theoretical framework and incorporates a strong coarse-to-fine inductive bias. The model’s inherent multi-scale nature and its ability to disentangle global color and shape features contribute to its superior performance in data-limited settings. SMAM significantly outperforms baseline model, and it performs on par with other leading methods in comparative experiments. Extensive experiments prove the effectiveness and superiority of SMAM. The code can be found at https://github.com/yikuizhai/SMAM.

title: HarmonicLIME: Frequency-Aware Local Explanations for Deep Music Genre Classification_supp1-3694788.pdf
authors: Sridharan Sankaran
abstract: Deep learning models for music genre classification achieve high accuracy but remain opaque, producing decisions that domain experts cannot interpret or audit. Existing post-hoc explainability methods such as LIME and SHAP operate on raw mel-spectrogram bins, yielding saliency maps perceptually meaningless to musicians. We propose HarmonicLIME, a music-aware local explanation framework that perturbs audio in the harmonic-percussive-residual (HPR) decomposed space, producing component-level saliency maps aligned to tonal and rhythmic musical structure. Applied to a VGGish classifier fine-tuned on GTZAN (83.0% accuracy), HarmonicLIME achieves a mean Deletion AUC of 0.1990 (excluding the Classical anomaly), outperforming Mel-LIME (0.2127) and SHAP (0.2582), with statistically significant superiority on Country and Reggae (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$p < 0.001$</tex-math></inline-formula>, Wilcoxon signed-rank). The proposed Harmonic Localization Ratio reveals genre-consistent saliency structure: Hip-Hop concentrates 81.5% of saliency in the percussive component while Country assigns 76.9% to the harmonic component. Zero-shot transfer to FMA-small confirms cross-dataset generalization, independently replicating Clever Hans diagnostic findings on a structurally distinct genre taxonomy. Code is publicly available to facilitate reproducibility.

title: Artificial Intelligence: Legal Applications of Artificial Intelligence
authors: Damien Charlotin
abstract: None

title: Recovering Reward Functions From Distributed Expert Demonstrations via Bi-Level Maximum-Likelihood Optimization_supp1-3688600.pdf
authors: Tian Lan
abstract: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) seeks to infer the latent reward function and the associated optimal policy from expert demonstrations. However, most current IRL methods assume centralized access to all trajectory data, which is impractical in real-world scenarios characterized by decentralized data sources and privacy concerns. To this end, this article proposes a novel algorithm for federated maximum-likelihood IRL (F-ML-IRL) and provides a rigorous analysis of its convergence rate. The proposed F-ML-IRL leverages dual aggregation to update the shared global model and performs bi-level local updates: an upper level learning task to optimize the parameterized reward function by maximizing the discounted likelihood of observing human expert trajectories under the current policy, and a lower level learning task to find the optimal agent policy regarding the entropy-regularized discounted cumulative reward under the current reward function. We analyze the convergence rate of the proposed F-ML-IRL algorithm and show that the global model in F-ML-IRL converges to a stationary point for both the reward and policy parameters within finite time. That is, the log-distance between the recovered policy and the optimal policy, as well as the gradient of the likelihood objective, converges to zero. Evaluating our F-ML-IRL algorithm on high-dimensional robotic control tasks in MuJoCo, we show that it ensures convergence of the recovered reward in decentralized learning and outperforms centralized baselines due to its ability to utilize distributed data—attaining better recovered rewards than all baselines in 12 out of 20 tasks.

title: Deep learning framework on physics-model data for accelerated design of nanoarray photocathode_supp1-3694453.docx
authors: Sihao Xia
abstract: Photoemission models are widely used for the structural design and physical analysis of photocathodes. However, the computational cost becomes prohibitive due to the complex equation-solving process when applied to nanostructured photocathodes, particularly for large-scale nanoarrays. In this work, we introduce a hybrid deep learning framework that employs a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on data generated by rigorous three-dimensional photoemission simulations. The network is first pretrained on photocurrent data from small-scale nanoarrays (dimensions≤100 nm), demonstrating its capability to capture the underlying photoemission physics embedded in the simulation data. A lightweight transfer learning strategy using only hundreds of larger-scale samples then generalizes the Photoemission CNN (PECNN) to broader geometries. This approach reduces the average prediction error from 36.82% to 2.91% for emitted photocurrent and from 54.80% to 3.34% for collected photocurrent. Concurrently, the framework achieves computational speedups of approximately 1.6×103 for small nanoarrays and 1.5×104 for larger systems compared to traditional numerical solvers. Post-hoc interpretability analyses, including SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), Permutation Importance (PI), and Partial Dependence Plots (PDP), identify array spacing, nanorod diameter, and substrate thickness as the dominant parameters governing emission performance, and the learned relationships align consistently with established photoemission theory. By delivering cross-scale, interpretable, and ultra-fast predictions, the PECNN framework provides a powerful tool for the rational design and optimization of advanced photoemission devices.

title: COSMO: O-RAN-Based Service Management and Orchestration for Cross-Technology Multi-Tenant Radio Access Networks
authors: Miguel Catalan-Cid, Joan Josep Aleixendri, Jorge Pueyo, Pau Tomas, Daniel Camps-Mur
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: The evolution toward 6G networks envisions a heterogeneous Radio Access Network (RAN) comprising diverse access technologies, such as private 5G, public 4G/5G, and Wi-Fi, managed by multiple stakeholders. While considerable research effort has been devoted to O-RAN-based frameworks enabling rApp and xApp implementation and validation, few works provide integrated support for cross-technology RAN orchestration, end-to-end multi-tenancy, and a unified subset of SMO functionalities, including Non-RT RIC components. This paper introduces COSMO, a novel RAN Service Management and Orchestration platform designed to support heterogeneous 3GPP (5G NR, LTE) and non-3GPP (Wi-Fi) access networks. COSMO enables cross-technology multi-tenancy, defined as the capability to allow multiple tenants to dynamically share heterogeneous RAN resources with explicit resource allocation guarantees based on Service Level Agreements (SLAs). This is achieved through management primitives that support flexible and on-demand resource allocation. Additionally, the platform includes a cross-technology Non-Real-Time RAN Intelligent Controller (Non-RT RIC) that enables the development of intelligent rApps for closed-loop control and network orchestration. Beyond its architectural design, COSMO improves resource utilization and operational flexibility through unified orchestration of heterogeneous multi-tenant RAN resources. Through prototyping and benchmarking, we demonstrate the effectiveness of COSMO in resource allocation, SLA enforcement, and scalability. In our prototype, the SLA-based rApp reduces SLA violation from approximately 21% to below 10% under dynamic traffic conditions in a heterogeneous RAN deployment including 5G, 4G, and Wi-Fi access networks. Our results confirm that COSMO offers an efficient solution for managing and orchestrating future multi-tenant cross-technology RAN environments.

title: GarmentRec: towards Individual Garment Reconstruction from a Monocular Human Image_supp1-3691682.pdf
authors: XB Gao
abstract: Reconstructing high-quality garment models from monocular images is important as it provides a practical and effective solution for human digitization and virtual try-on etc. Recent implicit function-based garment reconstruction methods recover free-form geometry but struggle to reconstruct individual garment meshes from human images and tend to produce disembodied limbs or degenerate shapes for novel views. In contrast, explicit parametric garment template models can be utilised to construct separate meshes and constrain the shape reconstruction robustly. However, this limits the reconstruction of garment details and shape variations, such as the wrinkles and pockets etc. To address this problem, in this paper, we introduce a novel explicit garment template that is designed for both closed and open garment topology. Powered by our new garment template, we further propose a detailed garment reconstruction method based on a monocular view that can process both the closed and open types for shape recovery. To capture those challenging parts with unknown geometry and topology, we predict displacement maps on the parameterization domain for the target garment from the monocular image and elaborate it to the 3D garment surface via the UV coordinates, achieving realistic details on the 3D garment shape. Extensive experiments demonstrate the accuracy and robustness of our method and show that realistic details like garment wrinkles and pockets can be faithfully recovered in an explicit way. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/worryDes/GarmentRec.

title: ISAC Beamforming for Underwater Acoustic Networks Based on Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Hengyi Lin, Tianle Zhang, Xiaowen Ye, Xianxin Song, Yi Wu
abstract: This paper proposes DeepUIB, a deep reinforcement learning-based beamforming scheme for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) in underwater acoustic (UWA) networks. The scheme aims to maximize the communication sum-rate under sensing constraints by addressing a key UWA challenge: long propagation delays that cause echoes from past transmissions to interfere with subsequent ISAC signals. Besides, a symmetric experience augmentation mechanism is developed for DeepUIB to accelerate convergence. Simulation results show that DeepUIB attains a higher sum-rate, converges faster, and exhibits stronger robustness than other schemes under various simulation setups.

title: Enhanced Flying-Capacitor Boost Multilevel Inverter (EFC-MLI) with Optimized Voltage Balancing and Current Spike Elimination
authors: Ahmed Awadelseed, Arkadiusz Lewicki, Charles Odeh
abstract: This paper proposes an Enhanced Flying-Capacitor Boost Multilevel Inverter (EFC-MLI) with optimized capacitor voltage balance technique. The proposed inverter configuration is derived from integration of H-Bridge (main cell) with new flying capacitor multilevel inverter (auxiliary cell). Moreover, the auxiliary cell generates the required voltage levels, while the main cell generates the base of the output voltage waveform. The proposed EFC-MLI utilized single dc source to achieve 9-level with dual voltage gain. The lower component count, reduced capacitor voltage rating, lowering cost, eliminates capacitor charging current spikes, high voltage gain, and capacitor voltage balance makes the proposed inverter attract+ive for renewable energy applications such as, Photovoltaic (PV) system, Fuel Cell system, and hybrid PV-FC system. Furthermore, to enhance the efficiency of the proposed EFC-MLI, a Hybrid PWM strategy with FC voltage balancing algorithm was implemented to ensure precise voltage regulation across capacitor while optimizing inverter performance. It minimizes voltage ripple, reduces switching losses, eliminates FC current spikes, and improves THD. Additionally, the proposed inverter was compared with recently proposed SC-MLIs. To support the proposed work, experimental results are provided which shows a good agreement with the analytical approach.

title: Stage-Aware Secondary Frequency Regulation for Coordinated Flywheel Energy Storage Arrays and Thermal Power Units
authors: Yubin Liu, Fang Fang, Le Wei, Zijian Li, Tong Tong
abstract: Coordinated secondary frequency regulation by flywheel energy storage arrays (FESAs) and thermal power units (TPUs) mitigates grid frequency oscillations caused by renewable integration. However, existing strategies often overlook stage-specific dynamics and multiobjective tradeoffs. To address this, a stage-aware, multiobjective, bilevel dynamic load allocation strategy is proposed based on grid assessment criteria. At the upper level, fuzzy adaptive control predicts TPU capacity, while field-based traction experiments establish precise physical power constraints for the FESA. An improved nondominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II) with a comprehensive satisfaction model and smooth weights is developed to optimize power output across distinct regulation stages. At the lower level, a bidirectional coupling interface enables adaptive group selection, followed by an improved consensus algorithm based on the relative rate of change of state of charge (SOC) for rapid energy and state alignment. Validated with operational data from a 660-MW supercritical plant in Ningxia, China, results demonstrate superior robustness against 0.6 s network delays and 0.5 s unit failures. Compared to representative benchmarks, it increases regulation net profit by 21.83% and reduces energy losses by 19.8%, effectively balancing grid requirements with the operational safety and economic sustainability of storage-integrated plants.

title: PQ-FRL: A Privacy-Preserving and Quality-Aware Federated Reinforcement Learning for UAV-Assisted Edge Computing
authors: Zifeng Dai, Hui Xie, Shengjun Wei, Changzhen Hu
abstract: Optimizing service performance in UAV-assisted Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) systems often relies on effective cooperative trajectory and resource allocation. Federated Reinforcement Learning (FRL) provides a distributed paradigm to collaboratively learn these policies without sharing raw observations. However, practical deployments face severe coupled challenges: the model quality degradation caused by Differential Privacy (DP) noise injection, heterogeneous environments (Non-IID data), and complex physical constraints. To mitigate these issues, we propose PQ-FRL, a Privacy-Preserving and Quality-Aware FRL framework. Our approach utilizes an Output Perturbation DP mechanism to provide strict per-round protection for uploaded policy updates, representing a pragmatic engineering trade-off between securing immediate operational privacy and maintaining continuous-control DRL convergence. To address the mixed-action space, we incorporate a Straight-Through Estimator (STE) for differentiable offloading decisions, guided by a conditionally shaped reward to prevent suboptimal local equilibria. Furthermore, a novel Quality-Aware (QA) aggregation mechanism dynamically assigns weights based on explicitly DP-protected local reward signals, helping to disentangle noise from inherently poor performance. Simulation results indicate that PQ-FRL can effectively balance realistic non-linear energy and latency constraints. Under the evaluated heterogeneous scenarios and strict privacy budgets, our method demonstrates robust utility preservation and exhibits graceful degradation as physical airspace congestion increases with larger swarm sizes, offering a stable and practical solution for privacy-sensitive UAV edge computing.

title: 200-Mbps/7-m LED-to-LED Underwater Wireless Optical Communication with Dynamic Topology Reconfiguration
authors: Yuxin He, Xiaoxu Ma, Yufan Zhang, Wendong Liao, Chengye Cai, Baokang Chen, Haipeng Wang, Shaoda Yuan, Wenwen Guo, Jing Xu
abstract: In this paper, a light-emitting diode (LED)-to-LED underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC) system based on a commercial 4 × 4 red LED array is proposed. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to utilize dynamic topology reconfiguration for realizing transceiver multiplexing with the same LED array. Specifically, the 16-LED array is configured in series during transmission to achieve high optical power, and reconfigured into four branches, each consisting of 4 LEDs, during reception to improve the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) via diversity reception. Equivalent circuit models are established to describe the frequency response behavior of the LED in both transmission and reception modes. Based on the proposed model, a pre-equalizer is designed to flatten the system frequency response, extending the 3-dB bandwidth from 3.29 MHz to 23.23 MHz. Experimental results in a 7-m water tank demonstrate a 200-Mbps on-off keying (OOK) transmission, achieving the highest rate-distance product among LED-to-LED wireless optical communication (WOC) systems to date. Furthermore, diversity reception based on maximal ratio combining (MRC) yields a 17.6% improvement in data rate compared to the single-channel reception. In addition, the system demonstrates robust performance under harsh link conditions. It maintains reliable operation in turbid water with an attenuation coefficient of 2.4 dB/m and bubble-induced turbulence, while accommodating a wide misalignment tolerance of 24 cm. Given its high integration, cost-effectiveness, and robustness, the proposed scheme offers a promising solution for resource-constrained nodes in distributed underwater sensor networks and the Internet of Underwater Things (IoUT).

title: Enhancing Different Hybrid Energy Systems Management and Their Optimisation Using AI Technology
authors: Ahmad Habboush, Bassam Elzaghmouri, Hussein Ahmad Al-Ofeishat, Takialddin Al Smadi
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Hybrid energy systems (HES) are necessary to make the energy supply less carbon dioxide-emitting, more flexible and cheaper. You know what? They use traditional backup and storage technologies, as well as renewable sources such as solar, wind, and biomass. This review covers the state of the art in HES optimisation, including engineering architectures, component technologies, and new approaches such as mathematical programming, metaheuristics, and surrogate models. Guess what? In agriculture, there is a strong emphasis on high-impact uses such as freezing, storage, and drying. This is because predictable heat loads facilitate the use of thermal energy storage and demand-shifting strategies to maximise the use of renewable energy. Essential key technical and economic metrics, such as Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE), Life Cycle Cost (LCC), and Net Present Value (NPV) are used to assess a project's performance. We also use environmental metrics based on Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). The study supports the technical and economic simulation of a biomass-solar-biomass hybrid cold storage facility in Jordan. This substantial amount of money, combined with reduced post-harvest losses, enables fuel consumption to be reduced. Finally, joint optimisation across multiple time scales, asset degradation modelling, and the need for standardized datasets and bankable business models are important research areas that warrant further work. This results in a targeted agenda for future research and policy.

title: RAG-Induced Failures in Multi-Agent Large Language Model Debate
authors: Insaf Kraidia, Iyas Qaddara, Nida Al-Shafi, Mohammad O. Hiari, Rania Alzubaidi, Amira Benlecheb
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) systems based on collaborative debate have been proposed to improve reasoning accuracy and robustness by aggregating diverse reasoning trajectories. However, most debate frameworks implicitly assume that all participating agents behave honestly. This paper demonstrates that this assumption fails under a realistic threat model in which a single malicious agent manipulates Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to selectively retrieve externally sourced evidence that plausibly supports an incorrect conclusion. Unlike prior work that relies on prompt injection or complex persuasion pipelines, the proposed attack requires only retrieval control and uses legitimate documents whose relevance is strategically overstated or misinterpreted. Experiments on TruthfulQA, MMLU, MedMCQA, and SCALR show that RAG-based attacks can reduce majority-vote accuracy by up to 38% while increasing group consensus around false answers. These results indicate that retrieval-often used to improve factual grounding-can instead amplify misinformation when adversarially controlled, motivating stronger verification and trust calibration in multi-agent LLM systems.

title: Text-Guided Approach for Accurate Lesion Detection Using Vision-Language
authors: J. Sofia Priya Dharshini, Pogabuddi Abdul Thouhid, Gorige Indhu, Akula Vamsi
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The role of accurate lesion segmentation is important in medical image assessment and diagnosis. Segmentation of key regions in images can be challenging, as these areas may vary in size and position. Common methods that rely solely on visual information often miss small or randomly located details, leading to reduced accuracy. This work aims to introduce a method for detecting lesions more accurately through text-guided training using a vision-language learning approach. During the training phase, this strategy enables the vision-based system to learn both image features and structured text descriptions. During inference, users do not need to provide structured text descriptions to identify lesion patterns. The model employs a cross-modality learning method to connect textual cues with visual patterns, which helps improve the detection of both large and small lesions. Experiments on the COVID-CT dataset show enhanced segmentation performance, achieving a Dice coefficient of 77.08 %, an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 66.06 %, precision of 83.54 %, and recall of 75.31 %, with an average processing time of 0.0189 seconds per image.

title: Fine-Tuning CLIP Models with Convolutional Features for Medical Anomaly Detection
authors: Jianfeng Fu, Minglong Xue
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Despite the significant success of large-scale vision-language pre-training models in zero-shot and few-shot anomaly detection for natural images, their effectiveness is sharply limited in the medical domain by the substantial discrepancy between natural and medical image data. To address this domain shift, This study presents a new, lightweight framework named Multi-Level Convolution Adaptation (MLCA), which is designed for medical anomaly detection by fine-tune the CLIP model. Our methodology enhances the pre-trained visual encoder by integrating multiple residual adapters to progressively refine visual features across various layers. Furthermore, we implement a ConvTokenizer to align CLIP’s image tokens directly with the raw input information, thereby preserving critical original details. This adaptation process is rigorously supervised by a pixel-level visual-text alignment loss function, which deliberately recalibrates the model’s attention away from recognizing object semantics towards the accurate recognition of anomalies in medical images. The resulting adapted features exhibit markedly enhanced generalization across diverse medical data types, yielding promising zero-shot capabilities even when the model engagement sentirely novel medical modalities or underexplored anatomical regions during inference. Experimental validation on standard MAD benchmarks confirms that MLCA surpasses existing SOTA models, showing the average value of AUC improvement of 2.44% and 1.18% for anomaly classification under zero-shot and few-shot conditions, respectively, and corresponding average AUC gains of 1.33% and 0.57% for anomaly segmentation.

title: Face Recognition Using a Hybrid InceptionV3-Based Simple Neural Network and Autoencoder
authors: Mohammed Chalwi, Ali Muwafaq Shaban, Roaa Chalwi, Muthanna Mishlish, Mohammed Ahmed Salih, Mohammed M. Al-Ani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: This paper ultimately proposed a hybrid deep learning (DL) framework for face recognition that incorporates InceptionV3 for powerful feature extraction with an autoencoder layer to reduce the dimensionality of the representation. A simple neural network classifies the extracted compact feature representations and enables effective discrimination among fake and real face images. We utilize a large-scale dataset of fake and real face images obtained from Kaggle sources, consisting of 140000 such images. The proposed approach achieved high accuracy of 95.73%, outperforming several existing deep-fake detection approaches. The main contributions of this work contain the development of a hybrid InceptionV3–autoencoder feature extraction architecture, effective reduction of feature dimensionality while preserving discriminative information, and comprehensive evaluation demonstrating superior performance over related methods.

title: Towards Robust Urban Obstacle Perception for Visually Impaired Navigation: A Dataset and Baseline Study
authors: Maryam Koraichi, Samira Chabaa, El Mehdi Cherrat, Abdelouhab Zeroual, Mohamed Zarboubi
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: Urban environments pose significant mobility challenges for visually impaired individuals due to the presence of diverse obstacles and complex scenes, while many existing assistive navigation approaches rely on generic datasets or simplifying assumptions that limit their applicability to real-world urban use. This paper presents a foundational study toward urban obstacle perception for visually impaired navigation by introducing a custom vision-based dataset comprising eight urban obstacle classes relevant to assistive mobility, manually annotated in YOLO format and reflecting realistic multi-object scenes. A lightweight YOLO-based model is trained as a baseline to assess detection performance on the proposed dataset. Experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of multi-class urban obstacle detection while highlighting challenges related to scale variability and scene complexity. This work establishes an initial benchmark and dataset to support future research on robust and deployable assistive navigation systems.

title: Bipolar Boost Converter for Transformerless Battery Energy Storage Integration in the Medium Voltage Distribution Grid
authors: Sabrina R. Helbig, Jacob A. Mueller, Jack Flicker, Brandon M. Grainger
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: A bidirectional bipolar boost converter was studied for integration of utility-scale battery energy storage systems (BESS) in medium voltage (MV) distribution grids. The converter accommodates the lower voltages produced by BESS and transforms them to a DC voltage level suitable for direct (transformerless) interconnection to an MV point of connection. For the application of BESS integration, this work analyzes the bipolar boost converter topology; develops a PI-based, dual-loop control method to track a voltage reference and to balance DC link capacitor voltages; and derives a representative dynamic model of the system. The dynamic model and control approach are validated in a scaled-down 80 V : 400 V, 1 kW prototype of the MV storage interface converter.

title: A Single-Phase Non-Isolated Six-Switch Buck-Boost Inverter using GaN Power Devices
authors: Halladi Shashwatha Kumara Kedlaya, Saleh Farzamkia, Vishal Kumar Meena, Alex Q. Huang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel single-stage non-isolated single-phase inverter based on a six-switch buck-boost (SSBB) converter followed by an unfolding bridge. Due to the flying capacitor in the boost-stage of the SSBB, the converter can achieve high gain with small passive components, leading to higher power density. Moreover, single-stage topology reduces switching losses and enables high power efficiency. In this paper, the operating modes and modulation techniques of the SSBB for generating a half-sine voltage are explained. The performance of the proposed inverter and its modulation technique is validated through simulation and experiments.

title: Assessing Privacy Risks in Android Applications Using ML and Explainable AI Techniques
authors: Abeeha Hasan, Sehba Masood
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Android applications frequently request many permissions, making privacy risks difficult to measure. The study presents a machine learning framework to assess such risks. It generates a single privacy risk score by combining static features from app manifests and metadata with SHAP-based explainable AI techniques. The process includes: (i) collecting the dataset and cleaning it, (ii) feature engineering from permissions and contextual metadata, (iii) training and evaluating models using multiple algorithms, (iv) SHAP-based explainability, and (v) calculating a weighted RiskScore. 9,872 Android apps were used to carry-out the experiments, which shows that ensemble models like Random Forest and XGBoost can be accurate up to 88.9% and have a macro-F1 score of 0.89, which is 8 10 % better than linear and single tree baselines. SHAP analysis found important permissions like (ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION, READ_CONTACTS, RECORD_AUDIO) as dominant privacy-risk indicators. The proposed method provide a reproducible and interpretable foundation for assessing over-permissioned Android apps and can be used for privacy auditing and policy enforcement.

title: Self-adjusting LFER-based Dynamic Gate Driving Technique for Fast-Switching GaN HEMTs in 48-V Applications
authors: Xiangyu Teng, Junting Chen, Yuwei Wu, Ji Shu, Kevin J. Chen, Wei Jia Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This work presents a self-adjusting lateral field-effect rectifier (LFER)-based dynamic gate drive design for 48-V, high-current GaN power stages. Building on our previously reported 400-V GaN LFER-based two-stage driver, the proposed design is adapted for 48-V applications with an additional gate-to-source resistor, which modulates the effective resistance (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF</inf>) of the LFER branch as the gate current changes. Immediately after the turn-on, R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF</inf> remains low and subsequently increases as the branch current grows. This self-regulating behavior allows fast switching transient while suppressing the gate ringing and shortens the post-switching damping interval. These features are critical for MHz range switching. Verified in a double-pulse test configuration, the proposed self-adjusting scheme has exhibited improved reduction in damping charge time by 25% compared to the prior LFER-based driver. The resulting gate overshoot has been lowered by 4.8% without compromising the fast turn-on time of 45 ns. These results indicate a higher switching frequency and lower EMI can be achieved for the 48-V converters at the multi-MHz frequencies.

title: Towards Fully Integrated Supercontinuum Sources in Silicon Photonics Platform
authors: Yijun Yang, Maria-Camila Diaz-Sanchez, Ilyes Betka, Etienne Herth, David Bouville, Samson Edmond, Jean-René Coudevylle, Daniele Melati, Delphine Marris-Morini, Quentin Wilmart, Carlos Alonso-Ramos, Benjamin Wetzel, Arnaud Mussot, Eric Cassan, Laurent Vivien
abstract: Supercontinuum generation (SCG) is essential for a wide range of applications, including metrology, imaging, spectroscopy, and optical communications. Although broad and efficient SCG has been demonstrated in integrated photonic platforms, most implementations rely on high-power and bulky pulsed laser sources. This paper reports recent advances toward fully integrated SCG sources including advances in on-chip SCG and pulse compression.

title: Semi-Supervised Carotid Plaque Segmentation with Multi-Scale Deep Supervision and Consistency Learning for Multi-View Vulnerable Plaque Classification
authors: Long Yang, Xiaoyin Chen, Bing Wang, Pengpeng Ou, Shaomin Niu, Peipei Zhang, Zeping Gao, Teng Ma
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Carotid plaque vulnerability is closely associated with cerebrovascular events, making accurate plaque analysis important for clinical risk assessment. In this study, we propose a framework for automated carotid ultrasound plaque analysis integrating semi-supervised segmentation and classification. A multi-scale deeply supervised dual-view DenseNet model with a UniMatch-based semi-supervised strategy was used for plaque segmentation, enabling effective utilization of labeled and unlabeled data. Radiomic features extracted from plaque, lumen, and union regions were used for vulnerable plaque classification with TabPFN and semi-supervised training. Evaluation on ultrasound examinations yielded a segmentation score of 61.72 and an F1-score of 63.83, with an efficient processing time of 163.54 ms per case (time score: 87.61), securing 7th place overall and demonstrating the potential of our framework for automated plaque analysis.

title: Synergizing Deep Learning and Biological Heuristics for Extreme Long-Tail White Blood Cell Classification
authors: Duc T. Nguyen, Hoang-Long Nguyen, Huy-Hieu Pham
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Automated white blood cell (WBC) classification is essential for leukemia screening yet remains challenging under extreme class imbalance and domain shift. These limitations often cause deep models to overfit dominant classes while failing to generalize to rare pathological subtypes. To address this issue, we propose a three-stage hybrid framework. First, a self-supervised Pix2Pix restoration module mitigates synthetic noise and restores high frequency cytoplasmic details. Second, we integrate a Swin Transformer ensemble with MedSigLIP contrastive embeddings to enhance rare-class semantic representation. Finally, we introduce a biologically inspired refinement strategy combining geometric spikiness analysis and Mahalanobis-based morphological constraints to explicitly rescue suppressed minority predictions. Our hybrid framework achieves a Macro-F1 score of 0.77139 on the private leaderboard, demonstrating strong robustness under extreme long-tail distributions.

title: Synthesizing Brain Magnetic Resonance Images for Myelin Assessment
authors: Nikhil N. Chaudhari, Nahian F. Chowdhury, Emily Dennis, Elisabeth Wilde, Andrei Irimia
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Myelin is a critical factor in brain development, aging, and neurological disorders, including Alzheimer's disease and traumatic brain injury. The ratio of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1}$</tex>-weighted to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2}$</tex> weighted (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1} \mathrm{w} / T_{2} \mathrm{w}$</tex>) MRI intensities is a proxy for intracortical myelin content, but the scarcity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2} \mathrm{w}$</tex> MRIs limits the utility of this method in research and clinical practice. We present T2VNet, a 3D autoencoder synthesizing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2} \mathrm{w}$</tex> MRIs from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1}$</tex> w MRIs to allow myelin computation. Trained on 1,033 participants and evaluated on an independent cohort of 140 participants, T2VNet demonstrates strong performance in recovering clinically relevant contrast (MultiSSIM3 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\simeq 0.90$</tex>, PSNR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>27 \text{dB}$</tex>). Group-level myelin estimates derived from synthetic <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2}$</tex> w MRIs correlate highly with those from genuine <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2}$</tex> w MRIs (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=0.75$</tex>), while individual-level correlations confirm subjectwise fidelity (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=0.53 \pm 0.094$</tex>). T2VNet lowers acquisition barriers to facilitate population studies of myelination, support research in under-resourced settings, and advance diagnosis for demyelinating conditions.

title: Personalized Federated Learning with Residual Fisher Information for Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Meilu Zhu, Yuxing Li, Zhiwei Wang, Edmund Y. Lam
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Federated learning enables multiple clients (institutions) to collaboratively train machine learning models without sharing their private data. To address the challenge of data heterogeneity across clients, personalized federated learning (pFL) aims to learn customized models for each client. In this work, we propose pFL -ResFIM, a novel pFL framework that achieves client-adaptive personalization at the parameter level. Specifically, we introduce a new metric, Residual Fisher Information Matrix (ResFIM), to quantify the sensitivity of model parameters to domain discrepancies. To estimate ResFIM for each client model under privacy constraints, we employ a spectral transfer strategy that generates simulated data reflecting the domain styles of different clients. Based on the estimated ResFIM, we partition model parameters into domain-sensitive and domain-invariant parts. A personalized model for each client is then constructed by aggregating only the domain-invariant parameters on the server. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that pFL -ResFIM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: SA-UNetv2: Rethinking Spatial Attention U-Net for Retinal Vessel Segmentation
authors: Changlu Guo, Anders Nymark Christensen, Anders Bjorholm Dahl, Yugen Yi, Morten Rieger Hannemose
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Retinal vessel segmentation is a crucial yet challenging task for early diagnosis of diseases such as diabetes and hypertension. SA-UNet achieves promising performance by introducing spatial attention in the bottleneck, but it underuses attention in skip connections and fails to address the severe foreground-background imbalance. We propose SA-UNetv2, a lightweight model that injects cross-scale spatial attention into all skip connections to strengthen multi-scale feature fusion and adopts a weighted Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) + Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) loss to improve robustness to class imbalance. On the public DRIVE and STARE datasets, SA-UNetv2 achieves state-of-the-art performance with only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 2 M B}$</tex> memory and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 2 6 M}$</tex> parameters (less than 50% of SA-UNet), and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1}$</tex> second CPU inference on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$592 \times 592 \times 3$</tex> images, demonstrating strong efficiency and deployability in resource-constrained, CPU-only settings. The code is available at github.com/clguo/SA-UNetv2.

title: Field-of-View–Based Spherical Video Stitching and Object Detection for Multi-Camera Drones
authors: Ghassan Al Jayyousi, Ghaith Al-Refai, Mutaz Ryalat, Hisham Elmoaqet
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: This work introduces a panoramic video stitching framework for multi-camera drones aimed at improving situational awareness and object detection in aerial monitoring applications. The system works with three video streams that were recorded at the same time from wide-angle cameras on the left, back, and right. To fix the distortion caused by the 160° lenses, each frame is first geometrically corrected using a field-of-view-based spherical warping model. We use ORB keypoints and RANSAC-based homography estimation to do feature-based alignment. There is also an interactive parameter tuning interface that lets you change the settings for the detector and alignment in real time. After that, a two-stage multiband Laplacian pyramid blending method is used to make a 270° stitched panorama that looks perfect. After that, the stitched frames are processed with a YOLOv5 object detection model to find objects in the larger field of view. Experiments show that the suggested framework works better for aerial perception tasks than single-camera detection. It also maintains the computational efficiency needed for onboard processing.

title: Analytical Modeling of Common-Mode and Differential-Mode Voltage Spectra for a Two-Level, Three-Phase Inverter
authors: Hassan Abdallah, Ryan Olson, Tianchen Li, Robert M. Cuzner
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The adoption of wide-bandgap (WBG) devices has improved the power density and enabled higher switching frequencies of converters, but these advantages come with elevated electromagnetic emissions due to fast voltage transitions and parasitic interactions. This paper introduces an analytical prediction approach for electromagnetic interference (EMI) spectra of a two-level, three-phase inverter systems. The proposed approach focuses on synthesizing the conducted emission spectra by capturing the effects of dominating parasitics and the modulation-dependent switching behavior to obtain high frequency accuracy within the models. Common-mode (CM) and differential-mode (DM) voltage spectrum models are developed using refined waveform synthesis and equivalent circuit transformations. Hardware measurements are used to confirm the accuracy of the predicted spectra, demonstrating that the proposed method is capable of enabling early-stage EMI filter synthesis without the need for iterative hardware prototyping.

title: Explainable Artificial Intelligence for Workforce Decision-Making in Enterprise Systems
authors: Monu Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is now becoming a more popular option with regards to decision making related to workforce, such as talent acquisition, workforce employee retention prediction, performance assessment, and workforce planning. Nevertheless, the intensive usage of black-box models has triggered alarm among the stakeholders of the enterprise in the areas of transparency, trust, fairness, and compliance with regulations. This paper will discuss how Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) can be used to overcome these problems in workforce prediction systems. A explainable machine learning system is built based on SHAP and local interpretability methods to offer significant results about the real-world workforce information, both on the global and features levels. According to the experimental findings, explainability is a key factor that enhances the level of expert trust in model outputs; in fact, the level of trust went up by more than 35 percent in contrast to non-explainable methods. Moreover, the suggested framework makes it possible to conduct successful human-AI interaction with the domain experts being able to examine and verify predictive results, with explainability being the key to responsible enterprise AI implementation.

title: Design and Optimization of a PI-Controlled Forward-Flyback Converter Using Matlab Simulation
authors: Prerna Bhingare, Girish K. Mahajan, Nilesh S. Wani, Rohini More, Kalyani Chavan, Ashwini J. Patil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: This paper presents a PI-Controlled based forward-fly back converter designed for LED driver applications. The operating principle of the proposed converter is analyzed and experimentally validated using a 24 W laboratory prototype. The converter is tested with and without a balancing capacitor. This shows that adding the balancing capacitor makes it possible for stable forward and flyback operation over the whole input voltage range. As a result, the magnetizing offset current is much lower, which means less energy is lost in the core, and the transformer takes up less space. The prototype is evaluated under pulse-generator (PG) control and proportional-integral (PI) control, with both approaches producing similar primary-current and outputcurrent waveforms. However, the magnetizing current is notably lower when PI control is applied. The results show that the proposed converter does a better job of suppressing magnetizing current and transferring power throughout the switching cycle. It has less core loss, works more efficiently, and works reliably with different input conditions. Simulation results show an efficiency of 92.4 %, power factor of 0.98, and total harmonic distortion (THD) of 6.5% under rated conditions. The results confirm that PI control provides better dynamic response and stability compared to the Pulse Generator method. The suggested balanced forward-flyback converter is a great choice for LED driver applications that need a lot of power.

title: 3D DoseGLA: Gated Linear Attention with Wavelet-Domain Constraint for Efficient 3D Radiotherapy Dose Prediction
authors: Ku Zhao, Xianhao Zhou, Wenjun Liao, Shichuan Zhang, Shaoting Zhang, Guotai Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Dose calculation, as the core of radiotherapy, remains a timeconsuming and experience-dependent process. While deep learning methods have been proposed to improve efficiency, they often face challenges in global modeling and computational complexity, especially with 3D data, limiting their clinical applicability. Moreover, their predicted dose maps are often over-smoothed due to pixel-wise loss functions such as L1 loss that ignore inter-pixel relationship. To address these issues, we propose an efficient framework DoseGLA for 3D dose prediction. First, we propose a Gated Linear Attention-based UNet (GLAUNet) that incorporates an improved gating mechanism inspired by Mamba into linear attention to enhance global modeling capacity while retaining linear computational complexity. Second, a Wavelet-domain Constraint Loss (WDCL) is proposed to guide the model in learning inter-pixel relationship with better prediction contrasts in frequency domain. Experiments on the OpenKBP dataset show that our framework significantly outperforms the state-of-theart model with around 80% fewer parameters, achieving a Dose Score of 2.33 and a DVH Score of 1.46.

title: Dual-Domain Hierarchical Adversarial Learning for Semi-Supervised Multi-View Fetal Cardiac Ultrasound Segmentation and Disease Classification
authors: Kerong Zhou
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate prenatal screening for congenital heart disease (CHD) relies on fetal echocardiography, yet clinical interpretation is heavily operator-dependent and hampered by the scarcity of high-quality annotated multi-view data. To address these challenges, we propose a Dual-Domain Hierarchical Adversarial Learning (DD-HAL) framework for the 2026 ISBI Fetal HearT UltraSound Challenge. DD-HAL incorporates three core designs: a multi-view-shared and view-specific multi-branch encoder-decoder architecture, a hierarchical adversarial domain adaptation module, and a Mean-Teacher semi-supervised framework with consistency regularization, which simultaneously addresses multi-view heterogeneity, label scarcity, and domain shift. Our codes will be publicly available at https://github.com/SnapeLocke/DDHAL.

title: A Data-driven Loss Model of GaN-HEMT-based Synchronous Buck Converter with Critical Operating Frequency
authors: Subhradip Mukherjee, Mahesh K. Mishra, N. Lakshminarasamma
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Wide bandgap (WBG) semiconductor devices such as gallium nitride (GaN) high electron mobility transistors (HEMTs) have significantly advanced power electronics by enabling higher switching frequencies, compact form factors, greater power density, and improved efficiency. Despite these advantages, accurately modeling losses in GaN-based converters remains a key challenge, especially under varying operating conditions. This paper presents a data-driven loss modeling approach for a GaN HEMT-based buck converter, combining empirical simulation data with theoretical analysis to estimate both fixed and variable losses across a range of switching frequencies and load levels. Curve-fitting techniques are employed to develop analytical loss functions for key parameters, enabling precise prediction of total power loss. The model also facilitates analysis of the trade-offs among switching frequency, efficiency, and thermal performance. Based on this analysis, an optimal switching frequency is identified that minimizes total power loss while maintaining thermal and operational reliability.

title: UAV-Based Thermal Sensing: Community-Based Learning and Research
authors: Kelvin Kang Wee Tang, Alvin Lau Meng Shin, Tan Liat Choon, Muhammad Hafiz Mohd Yatim, Norhadija Darwin, Nor Alafiza Yunus
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Today rapid urbanization has intensified urban heat islands (UHI) effects, particularly in the tropical regions where high solar irradiance and limited nocturnal cooling amplify surface heat retention. Consequently, these overwhelming temperatures bring negative consequences to the environmental ecosystem and human health. Surface material properties, specifically those constructed urban pavements and building facades, significantly contribute towards UHI effect due to their thermal properties, including albedo and thermal inertial absorption or solar radiation. Over the years, satellitederived land surface temperature (LST) has been extensively used to characterize broad and long-term thermal variations; nevertheless, micro-scale thermal contributions in tropical urban environments remain poorly quantified. To capture the localized dynamics of heat distribution, unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) or drone equipped with thermal sensors were deployed to generate high-resolution thermal maps with less than $3 \times 3$ centimeters per pixel of a public streetscape in Taman Teratai, Johor Bahru. Surface materials including asphalt, grass, concrete, ceramic roofs, car roofs, water and metal bodies were classified and analyzed. UAV-derived LST was validated against in-situ infrared thermometer measurements using RMSE and correlation analysis. Results demonstrate that industrial zones exhibit significantly higher ambient air temperatures (up to $\mathbf{1 - 5 ^ { \circ }} \mathbf{C}$) compared to residential areas, with metal, ceramic roofs and asphalt surfaces contributing disproportionately to daytime heat accumulation. Strong agreement between UAV-based LST and in-situ temperature data ($\mathrm{R}^{2}=0.91$, $\mathrm{RMSE}=1.87^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$) confirms the reliability of UAV thermal sensing for micro-scale UHI assessment. The findings provide quantitative evidence to support material-based heat mitigation strategies and demonstrate the value of UAV thermal imaging for urban climate studies in tropical environments and also highlights the potential of UAV thermal sensing as a valuable tool for urban microclimate monitoring, sustainable design and climate-resilient city planning.

title: Radiomics LUS Transformer for Liver Tumour Segmentation
authors: Ali Khalife, Mohammad Alkhatib, Erol Ozgur, Emmanuel Buc, Bertrand Le Roy, Youcef Mezouar, Adrien Bartoli
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurately segmenting liver tumours in laparoscopic ultrasonography (LUS) is critical for computer-assisted intervention guidance with augmented reality (AR). However, LUS images are often degraded by speckle noise, low contrast, and acoustic artifacts, while the liver's complex anatomy and tumour heterogeneity further complicate segmentation. These challenges are exacerbated by the probe's limited field of view and restricted manoeuvrability, which frequently produce partial or inconsistent tumour appearances across frames. Moreover, isoechoic tumours exhibit echogenicity similar to the surrounding liver tissue, rendering them nearly invisible in individual frames. To address these limitations, we propose RadioLUS, a hybrid deep-learning framework that operates on sequences of LUS frames.
