This case study illustrates the application of contemporary frameworks, API-centric design, and secure role-based access control. Key outcomes include validated strategies for integrating distinct technologies into a cohesive product and maintaining security within an educational context. The platform confirms the efficacy of the chosen stack for building maintainable, efficient, and user-centric educational tools.

title: A Multi-Stage Feature Selection Framework for Low-Cost CKD Screening
authors: Shaimaa I. Mostafa, Mahmoud M. Abdelhamied, A. M. M. Madbouly
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Chronic Kidney Disease is identified as a serious public health issue, especially because most cases are diagnosed at a later stage. Early diagnosis and monitoring of chronic kidney disease are critical as it helps defer the progression and minimize serious complications. However, in most resource-poor settings, it is not always possible to have early diagnoses as most comprehensive lab workups are not readily available. In this respect, there is an opportunity with the use of machine learning, which helps derive relevant information from available healthcare data. We present a multistage machine learning framework designed to balance diagnostic accuracy with cost and availability of clinical information. The proposed approach involves the sequential incorporation of patient data, beginning with a basic clinical history, followed by reasonable urinalysis results, and lastly comprehensive laboratory measurements when available. The framework was evaluated using the UCI Chronic Kidney Disease dataset, which contains medical records from 400 patients. Three analytic phases were built: the first phase used only clinical historical features, the second combined history with urinalysis, and the third integrated the full laboratory report. At each step, a random forest classifier was developed, and its performance was evaluated using standard metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. Results show that reliable chronic kidney disease estimate can be realized even with limited information. The model based solely on clinical history achieved an accuracy of 89.7%, which improved to 97.5% after incorporating urine test features. When complete laboratory data were included, the model achieved accurate classification.

title: TE-ViT: Gabor-Enhanced Vision Transformer for Breast Cancer Classification in Mammograms
authors: Dheeb Albashish, Nedaa Almansour, Shahnorbanun Sahran, Azizi Abdullah, Abdulla Amin Aburomman, Sofian Kassaymeh
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Breast cancer (BC) is the most prevalent malignancy in women worldwide. Early detection of BC is critical for effective treatment and ultimately for enhancing survival rates. Nevertheless, nonspecific symptoms in the early phases of BC make it challenging for mammography to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions. Furthermore, conventional Vision Transformers (ViTs), although successful in computer vision tasks, tend to overlook subtle texture-based biomarkers in mammograms, including spiculation and microcalcifications, which are significant for proper diagnosis. In light of the above, this study presents the Texture-Enhanced Vision Transformer (TEViT), a new dual-stream framework that explicitly combines Gabor filter-based texture analysis and transformer- based global representation learning. The first stream uses a standard ViT to model global spatial relationships, and the second stream extracts multi-scale, multi-orientation texture features from a bank of 40 Gabor filters, followed by a lightweight CNN to effectively encode these texture representations. These features are fused with ViT embeddings through cross-attention mechanisms at strategic transformer layers. The architecture is assessed using the publicly accessible mammography datasets (DDSM). The proposed method outperforms current models in binary classification tasks, achieving an accuracy of 95.09%, a sensitivity of 97.21% and an AUC of 99.26%, and statistically significant improvements <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(p<0.05)$</tex> in various evaluation criteria. The approach can reduce the diagnostic volume of work, lessen diagnostic variability, and offer prompt, dependable assistance for clinical decision-making.

title: Integrated Magnetics Design of a Two-winding HF Transformer Achieving Load Current-dependent Variable Leakage Inductance Characteristics
authors: Annoy Kumar Das, Baylon G. Fernandes
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Leakage inductance adjustment is pivotal for optimizing the performance of magnetically integrated soft-switched dc-dc converters. This work aims to achieve current-dependent variable leakage inductance profile in a high-frequency transformer using integrated magnetics design, which is simple, scalable, avoids any airgap in the main magnetic path, offers partly decoupled control of the magnetizing and leakage inductances, and eliminates bias/control windings and moving/changeover mechanisms, thereby ameliorating the limitations of existing methods reported in literature. First, the proposed methodology using high permeability ribbon/tape wound core is discussed; next, a magnetic equivalent circuit incorporating the load current-dependent variable leakage inductance is derived. Finally, the operating principle is verified using 2D FEM simulation based on a 7.2 kW, 360/360 V, 500 kHz transformer modeled in FEMM with ferrite as the main core and amorphous as the leakage layer. In comparison to conventional design, for the same interwinding isolation thickness, using the proposed scheme the integrated leakage inductance increases from 9.85 µH to 13.7 µH, corresponding to a 37.7% change, while the load current decreases from 100%–10% of its rated value. The proposed technique is also demonstrated experimentally using laboratory-scale prototypes built using amorphous- and nanocrystalline-based ribbon/tape-wound leakage layers. It shows that amorphous-and nanocrystalline-based designs can offer 15.5 and 30.1 times higher leakage inductance, respectively, compared to the conventional design; in addition, these exhibit respectively 7.67 and 13.2 times increment in its leakage inductance, corresponding to a decrement of 87.8% in the applied volt-second, thus exhibiting variable leakage inductance characteristics over a wide range.

title: Design and Implementation of Three-Phase Six-Switch Power Factor Corrector With DC-Link Capacitor Voltage Balance
authors: Shen Yan-Chen, Tsorng-Juu Liang, Wen-Chung Chen, Kai-Hui Chen, Kenneth Hong Yung Sheng
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: High-power power factor correction (PFC) topologies are becoming more significant gradually. Among these topologies, three-phase six-switch power factor corrector has the advantages of less components and a simple control method. However, the topology suffers from voltage imbalance problem between DC bus capacitors. In this paper, the average current mode control is implemented to achieve PFC by sensing the inductors feedback signals. Moreover, the voltage error signals between two DC bus capacitors are used to adjust the DC offset level of the sinusoidal modulation signal for achieving voltage balance between the DC bus capacitors. Finally, an experimental prototype of three-phase six-switch power factor corrector is implemented with AC input phase voltage of 220 V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</inf> / 60 Hz, DC bus voltage of 800 V, switching frequency of 50 kHz, and a rated power of 10 kW. The experimental results show that the maximum efficiency is 98.5% at 8 kW output power condition. Additionally, when injecting imbalance compensation at full load 10 kW, the total current harmonic distortion is 4.7% with a power factor of 0.991. Also, the bus voltage imbalance problem is reduced significantly.

title: Integrated Resonant Structures Using Stray Capacitance of Litz Wire Strands for Resonant Converters
authors: Pramod Apte, Xiao Yu, Jens Friebe
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Integration of passive elements leads to a reduction in the volume of power converters. In case of resonant converters, the resonant inductor is often integrated with the transformer. This work builds upon the idea of integrating the resonant capacitor into the magnetic components by using modified litz wires. By arranging the individual strands in a litz wire into two groups, the parasitic capacitance across these strands can be utilized as a capacitor in the converter design. This paper shows the feasibility of using the integrated structures in kilowatt-level resonant converters and provides a simpler method of modelling the litz wire capacitance. Additionally, this paper outlines the design considerations for two different integrated resonant structures, taking into account converter requirements and the availability of magnetic cores and litz wire.

title: A Source-Free Segmentation Quality Estimation of a Model Adapted to a New Domain
authors: Ido Nahum, Rotem Nizhar, Jacob Goldberger
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: We address the challenge of estimating segmentation quality in source-free domain adaptation (SFDA), where access to labeled source data is restricted and no labels are available in the target domain. Our approach leverages the segmentations produced by the source model as pseudo-labels and refines them to construct enhanced surrogates of the unknown ground truth. We show that standard metrics such as Dice and ASSD, when computed with these pseudo-labels, closely approximate the true segmentation quality of the adapted network. Evaluated on cardiac MRI and prostate MRI benchmarks, our method computes segmentation quality estimation that is more accurate than methods requiring labeled source data. These results demonstrate that pseudo-labels, commonly used for adaptation, can also provide a simple and effective basis for post-hoc segmentation quality estimation.

title: How Important are Inter-Dataset Interactions for Large Scale Analysis of FMRI Data: A Multi-Dimensional Comparison
authors: Clément Cosserat, Lucas Gois, Emilie Chouzenoux, Vince Calhoun, Tülay Adali
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Analyzing multi-subject functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data requires methods that can jointly capture shared and individual patterns of brain activity across participants. Joint blind source separation (JBSS) techniques, such as independent vector analysis (IVA), provide a principled framework for this purpose by modeling dependencies across subjects while identifying distinct functional networks. Constrained IVA variants, including adaptive-reverse cIVA-G (ar-cIVA-G) and threshold-free cIVA-G (tf-cIVA-G), further enhance interpretability through the use of reference templates and inter-subject correlation constraints. Alternatively, regression-based methods like IVA-G regression (regIVA-G) and reference-guided component analysis (RGCA) process subjects individually, aligning their components to references with improved computational efficiency. Despite their potential, systematic evaluations of reference-based JBSS approaches for fMRI analysis remain limited. In this work, we present a comparative study of these methods to assess their capacity for identifying schizophrenia-related biomarkers using real fMRI data from subjects with schizophrenia and healthy controls. Our results demonstrate that both constrained IVA and regression-based methods effectively extract meaningful biomarkers while the latter achieve comparable performance at substantially reduced computational cost.

title: Vision Transformer for Breast Cancer Diagnosis: Layer-Wise Analyses Beyond Attention Mechanism
authors: Kengo Takahashi, Yuwen Zeng, Kei Ichiji, Zhang Zhang, Haoyang Liu, Ryusei Inamori, Takuma Usuzaki, Noriyasu Homma
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Early detection of breast cancer is essential for improving survival, yet diagnostic accuracy remains limited in dense breasts where tumors overlap with complex glandular structures. Considering that breast density itself is an independent risk factor, an artificial intelligence (AI) framework that captures both local lesions and global anatomical context is required. Vision Transformer (ViT) has emerged as a promising model for representing longrange dependencies, and its potential has also been investigated in breast cancer diagnosis. Recently, the effectiveness of shape-guided attention mechanisms has been suggested; however, it remains unclear how the guidance influences the representation flow beyond the attention mechanism. In this study, we analyzed ViT models for breast cancer diagnosis using quantitative frameworks that track information propagation, including Norm-based Analysis, Contextualization Change, and Logit Lens. Our model with shape guidance achieved an area under the receiver-operating characteristic curve of 0.883, sensitivity of 0.923, and specificity of 0.704. In addition, it was quantitatively confirmed that the guidance affected not only the attention mechanism but also subsequent components.

title: Physics-Guided Dual-Domain Network with Attention-Based Fusion for Portable MRI Reconstruction
authors: Efe Ilıcak, Baris Imre, Chloé Najac, Ruben van den Broek, Beatrice Lena, Andrew Webb, Marius Staring
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Portable low-field magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) systems have gained renewed interest owing to their cost effectiveness and point-of-care imaging capabilities. Yet, portable MRI systems suffer from relatively low signal-to-noise ratio and limited hardware capabilities. While previous works have proposed the use of deep learning based reconstruction methods to improve low-field image quality, these operated only in the image-domain. Unlike other imaging modalities, MRI directly acquires data in the Fourier-domain (k-space), and exploiting both k-space and image-domain information can improve reconstruction quality. Here, we introduce DUNDD, a novel physics-guided 3D network for portable MRI reconstruction, with parallel dual-domain branches whose outputs are combined together via an attention-based fusion network. To demonstrate the performance of the proposed method, we present in vivo reconstructions obtained from both emulated datasets as well as images acquired with a 47mT Halbach-based portable MRI system. Our results show that DUN-DD outperforms state-of-the-art classical, datadriven, and physics-guided methods on both emulated and real portable MRI acquisitions.

title: Optimizing Image Segmentation Offloading in IoV: A Model-Free SAC Approach with PER
authors: Boran Yang, Wei Zou, Aohui Ren, Sergei Petrov, Alexander Grin, Uladzimir Vishniakou
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: The rapid development of intelligent driving imposes stringent demands for real-time image segmentation, a task that consumes substantial computational resources and often exceeds the capabilities of onboard vehicle systems. Edge computing offers a promising solution by providing proximate computing power. This paper investigates the critical problem of task offloading and resource allocation within vehicular edge computing networks. We propose an improved Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) based algorithm, termed iSAC, which integrates Prioritized Experience Replay (PER) to optimize task scheduling. The system model comprehensively incorporates key factors including server computational capacity, memory, bandwidth, task data volume, and transmission distance. The offloading problem is formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), where a newly designed reward function effectively balances task completion time, energy consumption, and server utilization. The PER-iSAC algorithm proposed herein exhibits superior performance to both Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and baseline SAC, as substantiated by simulation studies, notably in reducing average task completion time, especially for larger tasks, and achieving more balanced and efficient server selection. The results validate the effectiveness and stability of our approach for resource scheduling in IoV environments. Finally, we discuss deployment challenges and suggest future research directions, including the integration of task priority-aware scheduling mechanisms.

title: Agentic AI–Driven Autonomous Traffic Orchestration System for Sustainable Urban Mobility
authors: Delsi Robinsha S, Vanusha D, Vinoth Nas, Anbarasi A
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: City traffic jam is not a new issue within the urban environment that is associated with the consumption of high amounts of fuel, release of carbon dioxide, and even low productivity of the commuters. The old system of traffic lights that is fixed time does not adapt effectively to the dynamic nature of the distribution of traffic; hence, it does very poorly at the intersection. In this paper we introduce an AI based orchestration model of the traffic whereby the orchestration is an agentic environment which is able to automatically adjust the timing of the signals, depending on the present traffic volume. The system incorporates a very simple adaptive control algorithm, which can be used to compute the optimal periods of the green-times, without the necessity of using complex machine learning algorithms or pre-training. The offered Agentic AI controller is contrasted to a conventional fixed-time controller with the Metro Interstate Traffic Volume data comprising more than 48,000 timestamped observations of traffic on the entire time scale of the data. The methodology estimates the real time flow of traffic and applies the two control strategies to each time period and a performance measurement is done by a delay estimation model which measures the quantity of traffic that is not served at each time period. Experimentally, it has been established that the Agentic AI controller is superior to fixed-time control in most traffic situations. Performance is steadily improved on a monthly, weekly and peak hour basis and the Agentic AI can minimize traffic delay by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$27-42$</tex> percent, depending on the congestion. It is important to observe that the system is highly sensitive to abrupt demand fluctuations hence it can ensure low amounts of delay despite the need to compete in high volume operation in the event that the fixed time control is an issue. The discussed results bear the implication of the potential potential of lightweight Agentic AI systems to bring the efficiency of intersection to a higher level, provide a method of sustainable urban mobility, and serve as a means of scaling up the traditional traffic signal kind of functions. The paper forms a sound grounds of introducing autonomous traffic controllers in smart transports.

title: ADALORA-QAT: Adaptive Low Rank and Quantization Aware Segmentation
authors: Prantik Deb, Srimanth Dhondy, N. Ramakrishna, Anu Kapoor, Raju S. Bapi, Tapabrata Chakraborti
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Chest X-ray (CXR) segmentation is an important step in computer-aided diagnosis, yet deploying large foundation models in clinical settings remains challenging due to computational constraints. We propose AdaLoRA-QAT, a two-stage fine-tuning framework that combines adaptive low-rank encoder adaptation with full quantization-aware training. Adaptive rank allocation improves parameter efficiency, while selective mixed-precision INT8 quantization preserves structural fidelity crucial for clinical reliability. Evaluated across large-scale CXR datasets, AdaLoRA-QAT achieves 95.6% Dice, matching full-precision SAM decoder fine-tuning while reducing trainable parameters by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$16.6 \times$</tex> and yielding <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.24 \times$</tex> model compression. A Wilcoxon signed-rank test confirms that quantization does not significantly degrade segmentation accuracy. These results demonstrate that AdaLoRA-QAT effectively balances accuracy, efficiency, and structural trustworthiness, enabling compact and deployable foundation models for medical image segmentation. Code and pretrained models are available at: https://prantik-pdeb.gi thub.io/adaloraqat.github.io/

title: A Dataset-Based Comparative Analysis of Student Academic Performance
authors: Gulnar Naghiyeva
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: This study investigates student academic performance prediction as a key challenge in data-driven educational decision-making. Using the publicly available UCI Student Performance dataset, a comparative analysis of three regression-based machine learning models-k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN), Random Forest, and Support Vector Machine Regression (SMOreg)-is conducted. Model performance is evaluated using correlation coefficient, Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) under a $\mathbf{1 0}$-fold cross-validation framework. The experimental results show that ensemble-based learning, particularly Random Forest, significantly outperforms instance-based methods by achieving the highest correlation and lowest prediction errors. In addition to predictive robustness, the regression-based analysis provides interpretable insights into influential academic factors, identifying prior grades (G1 and G2) and absenteeism as the most significant predictors of final performance. The findings highlight the value of integrating robust predictive modeling with interpretable analytics to support early identification of at-risk students and informed instructional planning. Overall, the study contributes to the development of reliable, data-driven learning analytics systems aimed at enhancing educational quality and student retention.

title: Detection of Cyber Attacks in Networked Electric Drives via Physics-Guided Contrastive Learning with Few-Shot Labels
authors: He Yang, Jin Ye
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a physics-informed few-shot anomaly detection framework for intelligent motor drives by integrating contrastive learning into a Convolutional Neural Network(CNN)-based architecture. The framework leverages physics-informed parameters—specifically, second-harmonic components of stator current spectra—to guide the embedding space learned through Contrastive Learning(CL). This enhances the model’s ability to discriminate between normal and anomalous states, particularly under low-data conditions.All training cases assume a few-shot label setting, where only a limited number of labeled attack samples are available. Transfer Learning(TL) is first applied to transfer generalizable knowledge from simulation data to real-world domains, and CL is then fine-tuned with a physics-weighted loss to emphasize subtle anomalies. Experimental results show that, even with only a few labeled samples, the proposed method achieves up to 99.8% accuracy and reduces the false alarm rate by nearly 95% compared with CNN and other baseline models.The proposed method provides a practical and interpretable solution for cyber-attack detection in intelligent motor drives with minimal labeled data.

title: Resource-Efficient Automatic Refinement of Segmentations via Weak Supervision from Light Feedback
authors: Alix de Langlais, Benjamin Billot, Théo Aguilar Vidal, Marc-Olivier Gauci, Hervé Delingette
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Delineating anatomical regions is a key task in medical image analysis. Manual segmentation achieves high accuracy but is labor-intensive and prone to variability, thus prompting the development of automated approaches. Recently, a breadth of foundation models has enabled automated segmentations across diverse anatomies and imaging modalities, but these may not always meet the clinical accuracy standards. While segmentation refinement strategies can improve performance, current methods depend on heavy user interactions or require fully supervised segmentations for training. Here, we present SCORE (Segmentation COrrection from Regional Evaluations), a weakly supervised framework that learns to refine mask predictions only using light feedback during training. Specifically, instead of relying on dense training image annotations, SCORE introduces a novel loss that leverages region-wise quality scores and over/under-segmentation error labels. We demonstrate SCORE on humerus CT scans, where it considerably improves initial predictions from TotalSegmentator, and achieves performance on par with existing refinement methods, while greatly reducing their supervision requirements and annotation time. Our code is available at: https://gitlab.inria.fr/adelangl/SCORE.

title: Graphtrack: Automated Longitudinal Lesion Tracking in Ct Via Local-Global Reasoning With Contrastive and Graph Learning
authors: A. Vergara-Richart, C. Martín Vicario, G. Urbanos García, A. Alberich-Bayarri, A. Jiménez-Pastor
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate lesion tracking in longitudinal CT scans is essential for streamlining treatment response and disease progression evaluation in oncology, but current clinical workflows remain largely manual and variable. We propose GraphTrack, a two-stage deep learning framework that unifies local and global reasoning for automated lesion correspondence. First, a self-supervised 3D Masked Autoencoder (3DMAE) pretrains transferable lesion representations, fine-tuned within a 3D Siamese Neural Network (3DSNN) via contrastive learning for pairwise similarity estimation. Then, these features are integrated into an Edge Graph Attention Network (EdgeGAT) with similarity-based curriculum learning to model spatial, temporal, and morphological dependencies. On a large, multicentric, thoraco-abdominal CT dataset, the method achieves 88.9/80.5/97.8% precision/recall/AUC-ROC, and 90.2/77.7/89.6% on DeepLesion, demonstrating strong generalization and reliable, scalable lesion tracking.

title: Technological Pathways for Compact Electric Vehicle Onboard Charger
authors: Sadegh Esmaeili Rad, Sudip K Mazumder
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Design and Control of Automation and Drive Systems (IDCD)
abstract: Nowadays, there is an ever-growing need for electric vehicles (EVs), and thus, it is of importance to design efficient and compact onboard chargers (OBCs) at low cost to facilitate the popularization of EVs. To achieve that, several factors should be considered when designing the OBCs. However, the focus herein will be on integrating various up-to-date technologies to enhance power density while maintaining high efficiency. To that end, this paper proposes technological pathways for developing a compact and efficient EV OBC.

title: Discontinuous Space Vector PWM Strategies for Field-Oriented Control of Five-Phase Induction Motors with Reduced Switching Losses
authors: Jeeban Kumar Nayak, Ranjan Kumar Behera
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: The increased demand for high-efficiency and fault-tolerant motor drives has led to the adoption of multiphase machines, specifically the five-phase induction motors (FPIMs), emerging as a promising solution for high-power drive applications. The two-level voltage source inverter (VSI)-fed FPIM with space vector pulse width modulation (SVPWM)-based field-oriented control (FOC) offers precise torque and flux control, but the conventional SVPWM technique results in more switching transitions and thus the associated losses increase. However, in high-power and high-speed applications, the mechanical inertia of the FPIM rotor is very large and sufficient to damp out the torque ripple for smooth operation of the FPIM drive. This paper presents the implementation of discontinuous SVPWM (DSVPWM) strategies within the FOC framework for FPIMs to reduce switching losses without disturbing the dynamic performance of the drive. The proposed DSVPWM techniques reduce switching transitions in each modulation cycle. A comparative analysis between conventional SVPWM and DSVPWM is conducted using MATLAB/Simulink, evaluating switching losses and current ripple. In addition, the proposed technique is verified in hardware-in-the-loop mode with the Opal-RT real-time simulator (OP5700). The results demonstrate that DSVPWM significantly reduces switching losses while maintaining waveform quality and dynamic response, making it suitable for high-efficiency drive applications.

title: Multimodal Distillation-Driven Ensemble Learning for Long-Tailed Histopathology Whole Slide Images Analysis
authors: Xitong Ling, Xiaoxiao Li, Jiawen Li, Mingxi Fu, Yuxuan Chen, Jing Peng, Minxi Ouyang, Yizhi Wang, Yonghong He, Xiaoping Liu, Lianghui Zhu, Tian Guan
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) plays a significant role in computational pathology, enabling weakly supervised analysis of Whole Slide Image (WSI) datasets. The field of WSI analysis faces a severe long-tailed distribution problem, where some classes have sparse samples while others are abundant, making it difficult for classifiers to accurately identify minority class samples. To address this issue, we propose an ensemble learning method based on MIL, which employs expert decoders with shared aggregators and consistency constraints to learn diverse distributions and reduce the impact of class imbalance on classifier performance. Moreover, we introduce a multimodal distillation framework that leverages text encoders pre-trained on pathology-text pairs to distill knowledge and guide the MIL aggregator in capturing stronger semantic features relevant to class information. To ensure flexibility, we use learnable prompts to guide the distillation process, avoiding limitations imposed by specific prompts. Our method, MDE-MIL, integrates multiple expert branches focusing on specific data distributions to address long-tailed issues. Consistency control ensures generalization across classes. Multimodal distillation enhances feature extraction. Experiments on Camelyon+-LT and PANDA-LT datasets show it outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: An AI-Powered Adaptive Intrusion Detection System for Smart Healthcare Networks
authors: Lavish Kansal, Sanjeev Kukreti, Swati, K. Sushma, Sriharsha Makineni, Srinivasa Rao Bittla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: The IoT-enabled medical devices, cloud interoperability, and continuous patient monitoring systems are leading to an increase in sophisticated cyberattacks on smart healthcare networks. The paper suggests an Adaptive Intrusion Detection System (A-AIDS) that is AI-powered and customized to a heterogeneous smart healthcare setting. This system takes advantage of Self-Evolving Graph Neural Network (SE-GNN) and Temporal Neuro-Fuzzy Risk Evaluator (TNF-RE) to learn both device behaviors dynamically and determine whether communication patterns are anomalous, as well as the potential threat severity in real time. The suggested architecture is based on Federated Continual Learning (FCL) to support safe model regeneration across distributed hospital units without disclosing sensitive medical information as opposed to traditional IDS models. Moreover, a Lightweight Quantum-Resistant Operable Encoder (L - QRFE) improves the resistance towards upcoming cryptographic attacks and resource-restricted IoMT nodes. Investigations on the multi-modal renovation of healthcare traffic flows illustrate enhanced standing of detection, accelerated zero-day assault recognition, and weighty least overhead that can support the delay-unpleasant climate in the healthcare environments. The suggested A-AIDS model provides a more scalable, privacy-aware, and privacy-neutral, future-oriented security system to smart healthcare networks of the next generation.

title: Split-PCB Planar Inductor Design for High-density High-efficiency Power Converters
authors: Ashwini Kumar Dubey, Sayan Paul, Yanghe Liu, Shailesh Joshi, Dragan Maksimović
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Magnetic components often contribute to a significant portion of both the volume and power loss in power converters. This paper proposes a split-printed-circuit-board (split-PCB) design approach for planar inductors, which achieves a significant reduction in winding loss compared to a single-PCB design by mitigating fringing flux and proximity losses. The resulting decrease in total inductor loss enables smaller thermal management and higher power density. Both single-PCB and split-PCB planar inductor designs are modeled in Ansys Maxwell, and their performance is compared. Experimental validation is performed using a 40kW boost converter at input voltages of 100V, 120V, and 150V, with ac ripple currents of 40A, 50A, and 60A, respectively, operating at a light load with an average input current of 9A and a switching frequency of 200kHz. The experimental results confirm that the proposed split-PCB planar inductor achieves a 35% reduction in total converter loss compared to a conventional single-PCB planar inductor when the boost converter is operating at the input voltage of 150V, the output voltage of 288V, and the ripple current of 60A.

title: Series Arc and Hotspot Detection in Power Distribution Box for Industrial Loads
authors: Kuldeep Singh Shivran, Sarasij Das
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: The Industrial Power Distribution Box (PDB) distributes incoming power supply to diverse loads, including resistive, inductive, and power electronics-based devices, each with distinct current profiles. Circuit breakers allocate these loads to designated sections of the distribution unit. Mechanical vibrations in industrial environments can loosen connections at breaker poles, causing series arc faults. Such faults often remain undetected due to their low intensity, leading to progressive damage, potential shutdowns, and significant economic losses. Early detection of arc faults is therefore critical. While literature addresses series faults, it lacks effective methods for detecting arc faults in application-specific circuits with diverse loads. This work proposes a hybrid detection solution for resistive and power electronic (i.e., DC and RF generator) loads using current measurement and harmonic component analysis, complemented by a temperature-based detection algorithm for localized secondary protection. Arc current and temperature data are logged wirelessly, eliminating communication cabling and reducing space requirements. The proposed method is validated in semiconductor industry PDBs with varied loads, demonstrating high detection accuracy under different loading and noise levels.

title: DiffusionQC: Artifact Detection and Quality Control in Histopathology Images via Diffusion Model
authors: Zhenzhen Wang, Zhongliang Zhou, Zhuoyu Wen, Jeong Hwan Kook, John B. Wojcik, John Kang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Digital pathology plays a vital role across modern medicine, offering critical insights for disease diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment. However, histopathology images often contain artifacts introduced during slide preparation and digitization. Detecting and excluding them is essential to ensure reliable downstream analysis. Traditional supervised models typically require large annotated datasets, which is resourceintensive and not generalizable to novel artifact types. To address this, we propose DiffusionQC, which detects artifacts as outliers among clean images using a diffusion model. It requires only a set of clean images for training rather than pixel-level artifact annotations and predefined artifact types. Furthermore, we introduce a contrastive learning module to explicitly enlarge the distribution separation between artifact and clean images, yielding an enhanced version of our method. Empirical results demonstrate superior performance to state-of-the-art and offer cross-stain generalization capacity, with significantly less data and annotations.

title: A Maximum Power Point Tracking Algorithm with the Adaptive Moment Estimation for a PV System
authors: Jianwu Zeng, Zichun Lin, Taesic Kim
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The Perturb and Observe (P&O) algorithm is one of the most widely adopted Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) methods in photovoltaic (PV) systems due to its simplicity and low computational requirements. However, the P&O method, which heavily relies on step-size selection, suffers from power oscillations around the MPP under steady-state conditions and may incorrectly reflect power variations under rapidly changing conditions. This paper presents an adaptive moment estimation (Adam) MPPT that estimates the first and second moments of the gradients. The insensitivity to the initial learning rate eliminates the influence of the step size selection. The simulation and experimental results show that it has a faster transient response and smaller steady-state power oscillations than the P&O method, regardless of the step size.

title: Source Code Vulnerability Detection and Personalized Password Recovery Tool
authors: T Shyam Ganesh, T Vignesh, R Vasanthi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: The paper presents an intelligent machine learning-based security tool, which is used to process uploaded source code and detect possible vulnerabilities with the help of a feedforward neural network model. The system uses the method of token extraction, feature selection, model training and classification as the techniques of the system that are used to efficiently identify risky blocks of code. The application is trained on labelled datasets using a simple user interface and it helps developers and organisations to improve the security of their software by detecting flaws in real-time. Also, a password-recovery module is incorporated that provides possible passwords according to the keywords that were used in the past and typed in by the user. This project enhances cybersecurity, minimizes manual auditing, and allows identifying and addressing security vulnerabilities in time due to automatic password suggestions and vulnerability detection.

title: Eosinophil Counting with Green Learning
authors: Qixin Hu, Yixing Wu, Mate Levente Nagy, Hwan Dong, C.-C. Jay Kuo
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Quantifying eosinophils in gastrointestinal (GI) biopsies is critical for diagnosing eosinophilic esophagitis. The gold standard, manual counting by pathologists, is laborious, timeconsuming, and suffers from significant variability between physicians. Although deep learning (DL) models have automated this process with high accuracy, they often operate as black boxes, lacking transparency and interpretability. Furthermore, these neural networks require substantial computational resources for training and inference. This paper introduces a novel Green Learning (GL) framework for eosinophil counting, prioritizing transparency and computational efficiency. Our pipeline diverges from backpropagation-based DL. It uses unsupervised sparse-coding-based feature extractors. A dictionary selection module then identifies the most representative dictionaries, which are sent to the decisionlearning model for final classification and counting. We prove that this GL framework can achieve an accuracy competitive with the state-of-the-art DL models while drastically reducing the model size and complexity. This work represents a step towards green transparent AI in digital pathology, offering a computationally frugal and interpretable alternative for efficient clinical deployment.

title: Bytecode-Level Ensemble Framework for V8 Vulnerability Detection in Regression Tests
authors: Deepa Thilak K, Muhammed Sahal M N, Abdur Rahman
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: JavaScript engine vulnerabilities pose critical security risks to billions of web users, yet automated detection in regression test suites remains challenging due to high false-positive rates and limited semantic understanding of execution behavior. This paper presents a bytecode-level machine learning framework for vulnerability detection in V8 engine regression tests, extracting 41 interpretable features from Ignition bytecode including instruction diversity metrics, constant pool characteristics, and proximity-based semantic patterns. A soft-voting ensemble combining Random Forest and Gradient Boosting achieves 78.8% accuracy in balanced mode and–critically–94.1% precision at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 4 3 \%}$</tex> FPR in low-noise triage mode, the operating point most relevant for CI deployment. The ensemble outperforms LSTM-based deep learning by 10.2 percentage points, while SHAP analysis confirms instruction diversity as the primary discriminative feature. The framework supports threshold-based deployment enabling automated prioritization of regression tests requiring manual security review.

title: Joint Multiscale Image Learning for Segmentation in Macroscopic and Microscopic Pathology Images
authors: Yijie Zhu, Ayesha Azam, Ibrahim Alsanie, Ricardo Marques, Syed Ali Khurram, Shan E Ahmed Raza
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Image segmentation at both macroscopic and microscopic level is important in digital pathology. In these images, visually similar regions within the same image often share semantic characteristics, offering an opportunity for intra-image reference learning. We propose a novel binary segmentation framework using a joint multiscale image learning strategy. Our approach builds upon a whole image segmentation model and introduces a complementary part level module that enables mutual referencing among image regions. By computing the feature similarity, each target part image identifies its corresponding reference part images. Cross-attention is then applied between the target and reference part images to exchange information. This strategy not only enhances segmentation consistency but also increases the effective training sample size, improving generalization in small datasets. The proposed model significantly outperforms competitive methods in both macroscopic and microscopic datasets.

title:
Method for Determining Switching Timing of Gate Drive Current of Active Gate Driver to Maximize Turn-off Loss Reduction Under Fixed V
                    <sub>DS</sub>
                    Overshoot Condition in SiC MOSFETs
authors: Yohei Sukita, Toshiaki Inuma, Michihiro Ide, Kenichi Morokuma, Yukihiko Wada, Yuta Yamaoka, Yasushige Mukunoki, Makoto Takamiya
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: In the active gate drive, which switches the gate current setting (I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">G</inf> setting) three times when turning off an SiC MOSFET, a method for determining the switching timing of I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">G</inf> setting is proposed that maximizes the reduction rate of switching loss under V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS</inf> overshoot voltage aligned conditions, compared to conventional gate drive that does not change the gate drive current. Double pulse test measurements of SiC MOSFET are performed at 600 V and under four combinations of conditions including load current of 300 A and 500 A, and temperature of 25 °C and 125 °C, using proposed method. The proposed switching loss reduction rates under V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS</inf> overshoot voltage-aligned conditions are −33% to −38%. These values are close to the true maximum reduction rates of −48% to −60%, thus confirming the validity of the proposed method.

title: Digital Outcome Measures for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy: Machine Learning on Wearable Gait Signals Compared with Classical Classifiers and Deep Sequence Models for Good Health
authors: Saravanan. M. S, Siva Rama Krishna Prasad
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Digital outcome measures for Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) were derived by applying machine learning to gait signals collected from waist-mounted smartphone accelerometers during walking and running in children with Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) Digital outcome measures were obtained by using machine learning on gait signals filtered by waist mounted smartphone accelerometers during walking and running in patients with DMD and typical developing controls, and obtained temporospatial features (step length, step time, cadence, step velocity, and mediolateral power) to characterize DMD unique locomotor patterns. An example SVM classifier using the above example metrics gave a 94.3% accuracy, serving sensitivity of 93.8 and specificity of 94.7; a random forest model with 95.8 accuracy, 95.1 sensitivity and 96.2 specificity; a k nearest neighbors classifier with 92.5 accuracy, 91.9 sensitivity and 93.0 specificity; while deep sequence models, such as an LSTM network, obtained 99.6 accuracy, 99.2 sensitivity and 99.8 specific These accurate performance metrics ensure that both classical and deep learning models are valid to measure DMD specific signatures, which include reduced step length, and increased mediolateral power that may be likened to a Trendelenburg like gait and that deep sequence models are specifically useful in prescreening, monitoring and tracking progression of DMD in a real world environment with high accuracy and reliability, and outperform standard laboratory gait measurements and clinical scales, like the North Star Ambulatory Assessment (NSAA) when assessing DMD.

title: Multimodal Post-Disaster Building Damage Assessment with Weakly Supervised Learning
authors: P Vivekanandhan, Mithuna R, Tharun Kumar V, Swetha D, Denesh Kumar S
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Rapid post-disaster building damage assessment is critical for effective humanitarian response [1]. We present an edge-optimized framework that fuses optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) satellite imagery with crowd-sourced point annotations to produce real-time damage maps. An adaptive fusion module dynamically adjusts optical-to-SAR weighting based on estimated cloud cover, preserving all-weather capability [11]. A tiered Independent Bayesian Classifier Combination (IBCC) strategy scales computational load to available hardware [7], while progressive neural architectures span devices from smartphones <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{4 7 ~ M B})$</tex> to workstations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{8 9 0 ~ M B})$</tex> [14]. Experiments on the xBD and BRIGHT datasets show 78% accuracy retention under total cloud occlusion, 64% annotation throughput improvement over expert-only labeling, and robust cross-disaster generalization-establishing practical viability for time-critical disaster scenarios.

title: Transformer-Based Foundation Model for Universal Ultrasound Image Analysis
authors: Zhikai Yang, Junjie Hu, Félix Nieto-del-Amor, Guangyuan Li, Rodrigo Moreno
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: We propose a transformer-based foundation model for unified multi-task, multi-organ, multi-disease ultrasound image analysis, targeting 27 subtasks across segmentation, classification, detection, and landmark regression. The model follows hard parameter sharing: the encoder of a Swin Transformer pretrained on ImageNet is shared by all tasks, while each task uses its own prediction head. For segmentation and detection tasks, we use feature pyramid network decoders with separate segmentation and detection branches; for classification and regression tasks, we use encoder-level features with specific heads. Compared to other existing methods in offline experiments, the proposed method has the best performance. At the Foundation Model Challenge for Ultrasound Image Analysis (FMC UIA) challenge, our method achieved 8th place among 24th participating teams. The code is public at https://github.com/HJJ-D/Foundation-Model-Challenge-for-Ultrasound-Image-Analysis.

title: Edge-Based LSTM for Hyper-Local Precision Weather Forecasting
authors: Simhadri Vadrevu, Deepak Kumar, K. Harika Sri Lakshmi, P. Prasad
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: This paper presents an edge-based hyper-local weather forecasting system that integrates multi-sensor Internet of Things (IoT) data with recurrent deep learning models to achieve higher prediction accuracy than regional weather services. The proposed system employs temperature, humidity, atmospheric pressure, air quality, ultraviolet (UV), wind speed, and precipitation sensors interfaced with an Arduino Due and Rasp- berry Pi edge platform to collect real-time meteorological data at a fixed geographic location (Latitude: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17.016300^{\circ} \mathrm{N}$</tex>, Longitude: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$81.773435^{\circ}$</tex> E). Historical and live sensor data are preprocessed using standard scaling and label encoding and modeled using RNN-based LSTM architectures to forecast weather parameters over a 10-day horizon with low latency edge execution. The Edge- LSTM model has achieved prediction accuracy of 97.53% for temperature, 93.07% for humidity, 98.92% for pressure, and 99.43% for precipitation according to experimental evaluation. In comparing with Google Weather, there is a significant decrease in errors, with the temperature MAE decreasing from 10.88% to 2.47% (77.29% reduction) and the precipitation MAE decreasing from 17.51% to 0.57% (96.74% reduction). Further next, the proposed system achieves an average 10-day forecast coverage of 89%, compared to Google weather is 33%, demonstrating superior reliability in capturing micro-climatic variations. These results are validate that localized sensing combined with edge-based LSTM modeling provides substantial precision advantages over regional.

title: Forward Recovery Aware of Antiparallel Diodes for GaN-Based Synchronous Rectifier Turn-On Delay in MHz-Class LLC
authors: Dong Jiao, Zhengming Hou, Jih-Sheng Lai
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Synchronous-rectifier (SR) turn-on delay becomes a non-negligible loss mechanism in MHz-class GaN LLC converters because reverse current must initially flow through the GaN HEMT’s third-quadrant conduction path, which exhibits a relatively large dynamic drop. A widely used mitigation is to place an antiparallel diode across the SR device so that the reverse current can commutate to the diode before the SR channel turns on. However, experimental results often show inconsistent outcomes, and the underlying reason for the diode’s success or failure has not been clearly identified. This work demonstrates that forward recovery, the transient voltage overshoot that occurs during diode turn-on, is the dominant factor determining whether current commutation can occur within the limited SR turn-on interval. The SR delay is decomposed into two distinct intervals. Mode-1 is governed by loop parasitics and C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">oss</inf> discharge, during which no diode can conduct regardless of its characteristics. Mode-2 begins when V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ds</inf> reaches zero; only in this interval can the antiparallel diode take over current. A diode is effective only if its forward-recovery peak is lower than the GaN body-diode effective drop under the operating current and di/dt. A forward-recovery measurement method is presented to isolate intrinsic diode behavior from inductive overshoot. Hardware tests on an 800-kHz, 400-W LLC prototype confirm approximately 1 W loss reduction using a low-inductance SiC SBD, while ultrafast PN diodes provide no benefit due to large forward-recovery peaks. The study establishes a practical, physics-based selection criterion for antiparallel diodes in GaN SR applications.

title: Prompt-Free Lightweight Sam Adaptation for Histopathology Nuclei Segmentation with Strong Cross-Dataset Generalization
authors: Muhammad Hassan Maqsood, Yanming Zhu, Alfred Lam, Getamesay Dagnaw, Xuefei Yin, Alan Liew
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Histopathology nuclei segmentation is crucial for quantitative tissue analysis and cancer diagnosis. Although existing segmentation methods have achieved strong performance, they are often computationally heavy and show limited generalization across datasets, which constrains their practical deployment. Recent SAM-based approaches have shown great potential in general and medical imaging, but typically rely on prompt guidance or complex decoders, making them less suitable for histopathology images with dense nuclei and heterogeneous appearances. We propose a prompt-free and lightweight SAM adaptation that leverages multi-level encoder features and residual decoding for accurate and efficient nuclei segmentation. The framework fine-tunes only LoRA modules within the frozen SAM encoder, requiring just 4.1M trainable parameters. Experiments on three benchmark datasets TNBC, MoNuSeg, and PanNuke demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and strong cross-dataset generalization, highlighting the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed framework for histopathology applications.

title: Physics-Accurate Modeling of Quantum Effects in Photonic Integrated Circuits
authors: Richard Chung, Mahdi Nikdast, Kaveh Rahbardar Mojaver
abstract: We present a physics-accurate modeling framework for analyzing quantum effects in photonic integrated circuits. The framework is validated through comparisons with Hong-Ou-Mandel interference experimental data, and used to analyze the fidelity and bi-photon transmittance of Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI)-based meshes implementing Haarrandom matrices.

title: Mitigating the Reconstruction-Detection Trade-Off in Vae-Based Unsupervised Anomaly Detection
authors: Agathe Senellart, Maëlys Solal, Stéphanie Allassonnière, Ninon Burgos
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Variational autoencoders are widely used for unsupervised anomaly detection. Model selection however remains an open-question: to remain fully unsupervised, hyperparameters are often chosen to minimize the reconstruction error on normal samples. In this paper, we reveal a trade-off between reconstruction quality and anomaly detection among <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta$</tex>-VAE models. Models with constrained latent space reach higher detection metrics but lower reconstruction quality. We also assess the performance variability across random seeds and show it is linked to the distance between normal and abnormal latent distributions. From this analysis, we justify and investigate two methods to mitigate the reconstructiondetection tradeoff: beta-scheduling and the Sparse VAE. The latter especially shows an improvement in detection while maintaining high reconstruction quality.

title: LMPT: Conditional Point Transformer for Anatomical Landmark Detection on 3D Point Clouds
authors: Matteo Bastico, Pierre Onghena, David Ryckelynck, Beatriz Marcotegui, Santiago Velasco-Forero, Laurent Corté, Caroline Robine-Decourcelle, Etienne Decencière
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate identification of anatomical landmarks is crucial for various medical applications. Traditional manual landmarking is time-consuming and prone to inter-observer variability, while rule-based methods are often tailored to specific geometries or limited sets of landmarks. In recent years, anatomical surfaces have been effectively represented as point clouds, which are lightweight structures composed of spatial coordinates. Following this strategy and to overcome the limitations of existing landmarking techniques, we propose Landmark Point Transformer (LmPT), a method for automatic anatomical landmark detection on point clouds that can leverage homologous bones from different species for translational research. The LmPT model incorporates a conditioning mechanism that enables adaptability to different input types to conduct crossspecies learning. We focus the evaluation of our approach on femoral landmarking using both human and newly annotated dog femurs, demonstrating its generalization and effectiveness across species. The code and dog femur dataset will be publicly available at: https://github.com/Pierreoo/LandmarkPointTransformer.

title: Grid Reactive Power Control in Three-phase Unfolding-based Architectures
authors: Sanat Poddar, Regan Zane
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Three-phase unfolding-based power converter architectures provide a highly efficient and power-dense alternative to conventional multi-stage grid-tied converters. However, the absence of bulk energy storage inherently limits their functionality. This work proposes an auxiliary converter that interfaces with the soft dc-link voltages at the output of the unfolder and circulates only a fraction of the total load power to regulate grid reactive power. A key novelty is that the auxiliary converter is designed to operate with the characteristic time-varying dc voltages produced by a three-phase unfolding stage. The associated challenges of accommodating this voltage variation, as well as identifying a suitable converter topology, design, and control strategy, are addressed. The proposed system is evaluated through simulation and experimentally validated using a 1 kW prototype. The results demonstrate that integrating a partial-power auxiliary converter extends the functionality of unfolding-based architectures and allows for reactive power control.

title: 100kW 3-phase ARCP soft switched SiC (S2SiC) inverter - design and in-lab test verification
authors: Yushi Yang, Nan Lin, Yuheng Wu, Dongyu Jin, Zach Wehri, Muhammad Fasih Uddin, Brij Singh, Long Wu, Yue Zhao
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The auxiliary resonant commuted pole (ARCP) inverter is one of the most promising soft switching inverter topologies. In the ARCP inverter, resonant current supplied by the auxiliary circuit ensures soft switching operation. Successful soft switching of inverter results in lower level of electrical and thermal stresses on semiconductor power devices, and leads to lower temperature rise, and higher inverter efficiency. In this paper, 100kW ARCP 3-phase SiC inverter system was designed, fabricated, and double pulse test (DPT) test-verified to prove out feasibility for continuous operation. An excessive voltage overshoot was found under hard start, which poses a potential risk for premature failure of SiC power devices. To mitigate excessive voltage overshoot, authors have developed inverter start-up technique that was test-verified by DPT method. As expected with the developed soft-starting technique, power devices in the soft switched SiC (S2SiC) inverter don’t experience excessive voltage overshoot. The developed hardware for the S2SiC inverter and its control method is ready for interior permanent magnet (IPM) motor control. In this final paper, results from S2SiC inverter feeding a three-phase R-L load are included. Results to be obtained from a back-to-back motor dynamometer-based set-up will be reported in a future paper, where ARCP-based S2SiC inverter will drive an interior permanent magnet (IPM) motor with objective to prove out vitality and a compelling application of the 100kW S2SiC inverter.

title: Automated Midbrain-To-Pons Ratio Analysis for Differential Parkinsonism Diagnosis: Comparing U-net and Densenet Approaches
authors: Vipul Gohil, Manoj Sankhe, Mitusha Verma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: This paper tackles the problem of differential diagnosis in parkinsonian disorders by creating an optimized deep learning framework for automated midbrain to pons ratio measurement from MRI scans. While this ratio is a clinically validated biomarker, current manual measurement methods have inter-rater variability and are resource intensive. We test U-Net for segmentation and DenseNet for feature extraction on 43 PD and 40 controls from 2 datasets. Our results show U-Net is 96.77 % accurate and DenseNet is 95 % precise, better than previous methods and reducing measurement variability. This work solves the problems of small dataset, anatomical subtlety and clinical workflow integration.

title: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
authors: N/A

title: A Self-Supervised Uncertainty-Hybrid Machine Learning Approach for Modeling Tropospheric Zenith Wet Delay_supp1-3694884.docx
authors: Weixing Zhang

title: Statistical Delay Performance of STAR-RIS-Aided Short-Packet NOMA Systems for xURLLC Services
authors: Zixuan Xu, Xiong Li, Jin Xie, Qimei Cui, Xiaofeng Tao
abstract: The integration of non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) and simultaneously transmitting and reflecting re configurable intelligent surfaces (STAR-RIS) in short-packet communications offers a promising solution to meet the strin gent requirements of next-generation ultra-reliable low-latency communications (xURLLC). However, existing studies primarily focus on average performance metrics, which fail to capture the statistical characteristics of network performance. To address this gap, this paper proposes a novel STAR-RIS-assisted NOMA xURLLC network architecture that incorporates a stochastic network calculus (SNC)-based theoretical framework for statis tical delay performance analysis. Moreover, we adopt a novel correlated phase shift model for STAR-RIS, considering two distinct phase shift configuration strategies. Then, we derive the upper-bounded delay violation probability (DVP) for both phase shift configuration strategies. Extensive simulations validate the effectiveness of our theoretical analysis and comprehensively evaluate system performance under various network parameters, offering some insights for the STAR-RIS-assisted NOMA xURLLC system.

title: Fault Level Considerations in Tomorrow’s Wind and PV-Rich Power Grids: A Review
authors: Rafat Aljarrah, Mazaher Karimi, Hesamoddin Marzooghi
abstract: The increased penetration of Renewable Energy Sources (RESs), like Photovoltaic (PV) and wind energy, in transmission and distribution networks is changing features of current and future power systems. RESs may alter the characteristics of power systems due to their different dynamic behavior and limitations compared to centralized conventional Synchronous Generators (SGs). This is mainly attributed to Power Electronics (PE) interfaces (i.e., converters) utilized for integration with the power grid. As PE-based RESs show low and significantly different fault current contributions compared to SGs, fault level reduction is deemed to be one of the main challenges in future power systems. Besides, such RESs may bring new dynamics to short-circuit (SC) current characteristics of power systems, which in turn might cause challenges related to the strength, stability, operation, and protection of the grids. To tackle such challenges, it is essential to understand better the SC characteristics of the different RESs, specifically Wind and PV systems. In addition, evaluating their impact on the system fault level is necessary, especially in future power scenarios where most of the power is expected to be met by RESs. Hence, this study provides a comprehensive review of the SC current contributions of RESs and their impact on SC dynamics and the system fault level, addressing key challenges and gaps in the literature for researchers and utility engineers.

title: Discrete Time Delay Control for Dexterous Hand Joint Position Tracking Under Unknown Kinematics
authors: Gan Wang, Hongjie Li, Changliang Xia
abstract: Precise joint position control in tendon-driven dexterous hands with unknown kinematics is challenging due to the difficulty in accurately mapping joint angles to electric cylinder displacement. Furthermore, existing time delay control methods present inherent trade-offs, such as large overshoot due to integral windup in step responses or nonzero steady-state errors during time-varying signals tracking, limited by fixed forgetting factors. To address these issues, this article establishes a dynamically accurate mapping model by lumping hard-to-model parts into an uncertainty term, which is estimated in real time. After analyzing the causes of the limitations of existing methods, an enhanced method incorporating a forgetting factor and a prediction term is proposed, immune to tendon wear and elasticity. The forgetting factor is adjusted automatically to suppress integration during large errors and restore it near steady state, thereby balancing overshoot and accuracy. The prediction term, designed based on the residual error, adds an immediate proportional compensation component that accelerates system response. Then, the compensation mechanism is theoretically analyzed, and parameter tuning guidelines are provided under explicit stability constraints. Comparative experiments on a dexterous hand validate the superiority of the proposed method in achieving low overshoot, short settling time, and high tracking accuracy.

title: An Improved Convolutional Neural Network for Bias Correction of Satellite Precipitation Products: Demonstration in Complex Terrain Regions over Taiwan
authors: Liping Wang, Haonan Chen, Yun-Lan Chen, Pingping Xie, Pao-Liang Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Errors in passive microwave (PMW)-based precipitation retrievals from spaceborne sensors are often propagated into downstream global blended satellite precipitation products (SPPs), such as the NOAA Climate Prediction Center Morphing Technique (CMORPH) and NASA’s Global Precipitation Measurement (GPM) Integrated Multi-satellitE Retrievals for GPM (IMERG). To mitigate these errors, bias correction techniques are typically applied as a post-processing step during the derivation of SPPs. However, the current operational bias correction methods for CMORPH and IMERG are implemented at coarse spatial resolutions (0.25° and 2.5° latitude/longitude grid over land and ocean, respectively), which limits their ability to accurately capture spatial precipitation patterns. This article presents an improved deep learning–based framework for bias correction of CMORPH (version 1) and IMERG (version 6). The bias correction performance is validated over Taiwan’s complex terrain regions using ground-based precipitation estimates from the Quantitative Precipitation Estimation and Segregation Using Multiple Sensors (QPESUMS) system as references. Quantitative evaluations of the bias-corrected CMORPH V1 and IMERG V06 during the mei-yu and typhoon seasons show that the relative bias of CMORPH and IMERG is corrected from −46.25% to 3.43% and from −45.83% to -0.09%, respectively, during the mei-yu season; the relative bias improves from −37.09% to −7.43% for CMORPH and from −50.43% to 1.26% for IMERG, respectively, during the typhoon season. The Pearson correlation coefficient (CC), mean absolute error (MAE), root mean squared error (RMSE), and the coefficient of determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) also demonstrate significant improvements during both seasons.

title: PE-TSFM: Self-Supervised Time-Series Learning for Generalizable Power Converter Health Monitoring Under Unseen Conditions
authors: Xinyuan Liao, Xinyue Zhang, Xing Wei, Junwei Liu, Shuai Zhao, Siqi Bu, Yi Zhang
abstract: Data-driven health monitoring of power converters remains limited by poor generalization to unseen operating conditions. This work addresses this out-of-distribution (OOD) challenge by building a domain-specific time-series foundation model (PE-TSFM) that learns representations directly from large-scale unlabeled converter data. Unlike generic TSFMs trained on broad time-series datasets, the proposed PE-TSFM is pretrained entirely on domain data, enabling it to learn the physical relationships unique to power electronics. To further tailor the model to this domain, we introduce a dual-attention mechanism that captures both temporal patterns and inter-channel dependencies. While generic TSFMs primarily model temporal dependencies, the added channel attention (CA) captures inter-sensor physical relationships essential for converter degradation analysis. A dataset containing 141 million unlabeled timestamps from an operating power converter is used for pretraining. Experiments show that PE-TSFMs achieve 76%–92% accuracy under unseen operating conditions. In contrast, generic TSFMs achieve around 60% and conventional time-series models achieve around 40% accuracy. This result confirms the strong OOD generalization of the proposed PE-TSFM. Ablation studies further verify that the introduced CA mechanism significantly improves model performance. In addition, we conduct detailed studies on model scalability, hyperparameter sensitivity, and interpretability to provide a comprehensive understanding of the proposed approach.

title: ICARSC 2026 Commentary
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Autonomous Robot Systems and Competitions (ICARSC)

title: ITD-Aware CPU Power Modeling to Identify Peak-Efficiency Thermal Regions for Sustainable Data Center Operation
authors: Jason Crop, Hayden Moore, Sudeep Pasricha
abstract: Data center power consumption continues to grow rapidly with the increasing demand for cloud and enterprise computing. CPU active power and cooling account for a major share of this energy use, yet manufacturers provide no visibility into each CPU's unique power–thermal behavior. Process variations and inverse temperature dependence (ITD) cause individual CPUs to exhibit distinct electrical characteristics — including voltage, dynamic capacitance, and leakage — which together determine their optimal operating temperature for maximum performance-per-watt. This paper introduces a new ITD-aware CPU power modeling framework that integrates empirical voltage, leakage, and capacitance measurements to predict total power across temperature and frequency with high accuracy. The model is validated on 22 Intel Xeon server CPUs, achieving less than 3% error across operating conditions. Using the model, we identify each CPU's optimal temperature that minimizes power while sustaining performance. Analysis of real cloud servers reveals that around 50% of modern CPUs operate roughly 10°C below their optimal point, wasting approximately 15% in excess cooling energy. Classifying CPUs by their optimal temperature enables data centers to group and cool them more efficiently, reducing energy use and improving sustainability without performance degradation.

title: A Low Profile, Small Size Asymmetric Parabolic Cylindrical Vortex Antenna Based on Anisotropic Holographic Metasurface
authors: Zhaoneng Jiang, Xinzeng Gu, He Shuang, Hui Liang, Bingzan Sun, Lang Wu
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: In this paper, an asymmetric parabolic cylindrical antenna based on anisotropic holographic impedance modulation surface is proposed. The anisotropic unit incorporates a slotted octagonal patch with adjustable structural parameters, which enables independent and flexible control of tensor impedance. Based on the proposed anisotropic unit, a holographic impedance surface is designed for loading onto a parabolic cylinder. Using a monopole antenna as the feed, the structure can generate high-purity OAM beams with modes of l = +1, +2, and −3. For verification, the antenna with l = +1 mode is fabricated and measured. Both measurement and simulation results demonstrate that the proposed antenna achieves high-purity OAM beam generation with a compact size of 4.5λ×4.5λ and an extremely low profile of 0.05λ. The proposed structure breaks the geometric limits of conventional planar or symmetric conformal designs, making it suitable for curved conformal platforms such as UAV skins and wearable devices.

title: SilentNoise: Non-interactive Noise Generation for Differential Privacy with Malicious Security_supp1-3694826.pdf
authors: Reo Eriguchi
abstract: Differential privacy (DP) is a standard notion of privacy-preserving data analysis. Traditionally, DP has been studied in the central model, where a trusted curator collects all private inputs and releases aggregation results preserving DP. However, this model creates a single point of failure, as all private data are held by a single party. We aim to decentralize additive noise DP mechanisms by leveraging secure multiparty computation (MPC) techniques. The main technical challenge is how to obliviously sample secret noise from specific probability distributions in the presence of malicious parties. Most existing maliciously secure protocols require multiple rounds of interaction, resulting in high communication and computation overheads. Moreover, the only known maliciously secure, non-interactive protocol suffers from a utility loss that grows exponentially with the number of computing parties. In this work, we propose <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SilentNoise</small>, a general framework for maliciously secure, non-interactive protocols for sampling DP noise, and present several instantiations that improve utility and efficiency both theoretically and empirically. In our protocols, any incorrect noise contributed by malicious parties is rejected and meaningful utility is guaranteed whenever the protocols succeed. After a one-time interactive setup that can be amortized over multiple uses, parties can non-interactively generate an unbounded number of shares of noise. This leads to a significant improvement in efficiency compared to the previous interactive protocols. Furthermore, our protocols improve the utility loss of the prior maliciously secure, non-interactive protocol by an exponential factor in the number of computing parties. They can even achieve a constant utility loss that is comparable to central-model DP mechanisms, under a strong honest-majority setting.

title: Design and Modeling of a Human-Powered Pneumatic Supply System: Application as a Pneumatic Source for Peristaltic Pumps
authors: Miku Tsubouchi, Masaki Kawano, Chiaki Yamazaki, Fumio Ito, Taro Nakamura
abstract: This study proposes a manually operated pneumatic supply system that operates without an external power source. Soft robots, known for their high compliance, are increasingly used to be utilized in various fields such as manufacturing, healthcare, and agriculture. However, they often rely on bulky pneumatic systems that require external power, limiting their applicability in extreme environments such as outer space and disaster-stricken areas. To overcome this limitation, this study introduces a pneumatic supply system that eliminates the need for conventional compressors and electric vacuum pumps by utilizing a manually operated pump to pressurize an air tank. A theoretical model was developed to determine the required tank specifications based on the air consumption and operating pressure of the actuator. Furthermore, a model was formulated to estimate the number of manual pumping cycles necessary to achieve the target pressure. Experimental validation identified key parameters and confirmed the model’s accuracy. The results demonstrated that the model is particularly effective for tanks with 1 L or larger. To assess its feasibility in extreme environments, the system was tested using a soft peristaltic pump designed to mimic intestinal peristalsis. The drive experiment verified that the pump successfully completed the target of five cycles, thereby validating the predictions of the model.

title: Co-design of Low-Profile Leaky-Wave-Fed Transmit-Array Antennas Using the Generalized Risley Prism Concept for Side-Lobe Reduction
authors: Sérgio A. Matos, Margarida Fernandes, João M. Felício, Jorge R. Costa, Carlos A. Fernandes, Nelson J. G. Fonseca
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Emerging millimeter wave communications systems require compact, low-mass, low-cost, and energy-efficient antennas, capable of high-gain, wide-angle beam steering. Recent advances in mechanical beam-scanning techniques make them attractive for the above requirements, compared to the phased array antennas, but are still bulkier.
