However, how different types of movement are modulated by the cortico-muscular network in response to a fatigue remains unclear. In this study, ten healthy subjects were recruited to perform sit-to-stand tasks (T1: concentric sit-to-stand, T2: eccentric stand-to-sit) in both pre-fatigue and post-fatigue states. During these tasks, electroencephalogram (EEG) and electromyogram (EMG) signals from the lower extremities were recorded. Muscle and mental fatigue were confirmed by EMG mean power frequency (MPF) and changes in EEG Lempel-Ziv complexity (LZC). Inter-cortical and cortico-muscular networks were analyzed using generalized partial directed coherence (GPDC), with clustering coefficient (CC) and global efficiency (GE) as network metrics. In the pre-fatigue state, T1 exhibited greater inter-cortical network connectivity compared to T2. In the post-fatigue state, T1 increased inter-cortical connectivity while T2 showed significant increases in CC and GE in the beta band (P=0.018, P=0.034). At the cortico-muscular level, T1 shifted the descending pathway from Beta band to Gamma band while T2 significantly upregulated the strength of descending cortico-muscular pathway and ascending pathways in both Beta and Gamma bands. These findings indicate concentric movements exhibited more compensation in inter-cortical connectivity and band shift in cortico-muscular network, while eccentric movements showed greater efficiency in inter-cortical level and stronger cortico-muscular network strength. This study reveals distinct modulation patterns for concentric and eccentric movements under fatigued conditions, which may provide insights for developing targeted training and therapeutic programs for rehabilitation.

title: A Review on the Control of Magnetless Inductive Rotary Transformer-Based Excitation Systems
authors: Saleh A Ali, Farshid Mahmouditabar, Volker Pickert, Shafiq Odhano, Muhammad Awan, Zepeng Liu
abstract: Magnetless Rotary transformer (RT) excitation systems enable fully brushless operation of electrically excited synchronous machines (EESMs), offering a scalable alternative to permanent-magnet drives. This paper presents a control-oriented review of RT-excited EESMs, integrating modeling, converter topologies, and control strategies within a unified framework. A complete modeling chain, from fundamental harmonic approximation (FHA) to dynamic dq-axis and multi-physics analysis, is outlined to support accurate field regulation and efficiency optimization. Inverter and compensation topologies enabling soft switching (ZVS/ZCS) are compared in terms of control performance. Emerging self-excitation schemes such as common-mode voltage injection, harmonic excitation, and carrier-phase modulation are evaluated for their implementation simplicity and control flexibility. The paper concludes by identifying key challenges in sensorless excitation control, thermal coupling, and high-frequency operation, and outlines research trends that can further enhance the performance, reliability, and scalability of brushless EESM drives.

title: A Unified Framework With Capped Tensor Norm Minimization for Multiview Subspace Learning
authors: Yao Fu, Zhi Wang, Dong Hu, Tao Jia, Chao Gao, Zhen Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: In recent years, multi-view subspace clustering (MVSC) has emerged as a powerful tool for high-dimensional data analysis, due to its ability to exploit intrinsic structures across multiple feature spaces. However, existing MVSC methods still suffer from two shortcomings: 1) the prevalent use of convex low-rank approximations inadequately exploits complementary subspace information, and 2) the neglect of intrinsic geometric relationships and the execution of spectral clustering as a separate step often lead to suboptimal solutions. To alleviate these problems, in this paper we propose a robust and effective model for the MVSC task by jointly incorporating the capped norm, which not only effectively captures the intrinsic global low-rank structure and suppresses the influence of feature outliers by utilizing capped tensor nuclear norm (CTNN), but also integrates the hyper- Laplacian regularization and the spectral embedding through joint optimization to organically benefit from each other. Then, a high computational efficiency algorithm is developed under the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) framework to solve the proposed model. Meanwhile, rigorous mathematical analysis shows that the global optimum of the CTNN regularized least squares subproblem can be analytically determined in closed-form. More importantly, the convergence of the proposed algorithm is theoretically established by proving that the sequence it generates converges to a desirable Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) point. Finally, extensive experiments on various datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed model. MATLAB code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/wangzhi-swu/UFCTNM</uri>.

title: Predicting Cerebral Aneurysms Rupture From Hemodynamics
authors: S. Castonguay, P. Dirix, P. Thurner, S. Kozerke, M. Holzner
abstract: Cerebral aneurysms are relatively common vascular abnormalities with potentially severe consequences. Although most remain unruptured, a small number do rupture, sometimes leading to subarachnoid hemorrhage. Reliably identifying which aneurysms pose the greatest risk remains an unresolved challenge in clinical practice. In this work, we show that combining computational fluid dynamics (CFD) with statistical testing and basic classification models can help identify features linked to aneurysm rupture and assess rupture risk. Using simulations of 103 aneurysms, we extracted a wide set of hemodynamic metrics, combined with other available geometric, anatomical, and patient-specific information. Using PERMANOVA and simple classification models, we assessed the ability of different datasets to differentiate between ruptured and unruptured cases. Results show that monovariate analyses do not perform especially well, with a prediction accuracy of 65% at best, and that combining features from different categories improves rupture prediction significantly. More specifically, we showed that datasets using non-CFD data reached up to 67% accuracy, while adding flow-based features increased performance to nearly 79%. Some hemodynamic variables also showed stronger statistical relevancy during specific phases of the cardiac cycle, suggesting that transient flow effects may be key in uncovering rupture mechanisms. Overall, our findings demonstrate that combining hemodynamics with statistical techniques enables a robust assessment of rupture risk and offers insights into the underlying mechanisms of rupture. Given rapid advances in image-based hemodynamic analysis using CFD in clinical settings, we anticipate that our approach could be translated into clinical practice to support more informed and individualized rupture risk assessment.

title: RSEdit: Text-Guided Image Editing for Remote Sensing
authors: Zhenyuan Chen, Zechuan Zhang, Feng Zhang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: In this paper, we explore text-guided image editing in the remote sensing domain using generative modeling. We propose RSEdit, a collection of models from U-Net to DiT with various configurations. Specifically, we present the first comprehensive study of conditioning strategies for building image editing models from off-the-shelf text-to-image ones. Our experiments show that RSEdit achieves the best instruction-faithful edits while preserving geospatial structure. We release the code at https://github.com/Bili-Sakura/RSEdit-Preview and checkpoints at https://huggingface.co/collections/BiliSakura/rsedit.

title: Ultra Compact Eleven-Level Single-Phase Stacked T-Type Inverter Enabled by a Solid-State Multi-Terminal Bidirectional Switch
authors: Theofilos Moraitis, Georgios Kampitsis
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Multi-level inverters have increasingly gathered interest over the past decade due to their increased efficiency and power density for demanding applications such as power generation from renewable energy sources and high-performance drive and traction systems. For lower power systems in the kW range, gate driving circuitry can occupy area and volume much larger than that of the semiconductor devices, which is especially challenging in high device count topologies, thus prohibiting increase in power density. In an attempt to minimize such circuitry, this paper proposes an implementation of an 11-level single phase inverter, which can be extended to N-levels, with minimal driving requirements in terms of signal and power isolation. The concept is then validated through an 850 W laboratory prototype, reaching a peak efficiency of 97.1% and power density of over 4 kW/L.

title: Optimal Demand Side Management using Multi-Objective Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Rachita R Sarangi, Jyoti R. Sahu, Prakash K Ray, Asit Mohanty, Soumya R. Mohanty, Nand Kishor
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: In this study, a probable programming model is proposed to optimize the peak demand-supply gap of a smart grid, in a short term to minimize operating costs and emissions with renewable sources. In order to achieve an accurate model, the use of a probability density function to predict the wind speed and solar irradiance is proposed. On the other hand, in order to resolve the power produced from the wind and the solar renewable uncertainty of sources, the use of demand response programs with the participation of residential, commercial and industrial consumers is proposed. The multi-objective particle swarm optimization method is utilized to solve this problem. In order to validate the proposed model, it is employed on a sample smart micro-grid, and the obtained numerical results clearly indicate the impact of demand side management on reducing the effect of uncertainty induced by the predicted power generation using wind turbines and solar cells.

title: Modeling Multi-View Impedance-Based Cross-Geometry SOH Estimator for Li-ion Batteries
authors: Ahmed Bakr, Md. Samiul Islam Sagar, Mohammad K. Al-Smadi, Sajad Saberi, Jaber A. Abu Qahouq
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Accurately estimating battery’s State of Health (SOH) remains challenging when models must generalize across cell designs and operating conditions. Most Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)-based approaches either (i) hand-engineer a few Nyquist-plot features for shallow models—fast but does not generalize across geometries—or (ii) learn directly from Nyquist plots with deep networks, which removes manual feature extraction, yet still limited to a single plot type. As a result, cross-geometry robustness and deployability on constrained Internet of Things (IoT) devices remain open problems. We propose a compact Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) (∼ 10k parameters) that takes multi-representation EIS inputs—Nyquist (real/imaginary) and phase–magnitude (|Z|/ϕ) stacked as four channels, so the model can learn complementary degradation signatures while remaining small enough for fast inference. We build a dataset from cyclic aging of two geometries (LG INR18650MJ1 cylindrical cells and LIR2032 coin cells), acquire EIS every ten cycles from 10 kHz to 10 mHz (10 points/decade), and evaluate with leave-one-cell-out testing strategy. We further study fusion vs. single-representation inputs and assess feasibility for on-device deployment (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson device). The results show that training on multiple EIS representations improves SOH estimation accuracy and cross-geometry generalization compared to single-representation models, which uses only Nyquist or phase–magnitude plots. This design targets accurate, generalizable SOH prediction without manual feature engineering while enabling practical real-time use.

title: Efficient Transformer-Based Motor Imagery Classification for Edge-Deployed Brain-Computer Interfaces
authors: Xianzhe Cheng, Yifan Zhang, Yang Yu, Yuhan Huang, Anqi Wu, Ming Zhang, Ling-Li Zeng
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Motor Imagery (MI) based Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs) offer promising solutions for assistive technology and rehabilitation, but their practical deployment remains challenging due to the computational demands of high-performance decoding algorithms. This work introduces EdgeMIFormer, a novel Transformer-based architecture specifically optimized for edge-deployed MI-EEG classification. The proposed approach features a local attention fusion mechanism that effectively captures temporal dynamics in EEG signals while significantly reducing computational complexity. We implement comprehensive model optimization through quantization and structured pruning, maintaining classification performance while enabling efficient deployment. The optimized model is successfully deployed on the RK NPU, achieving real-time inference with latency of 31 ms. Experimental results on benchmark MI-EEG datasets demonstrate classification accuracy of 83.42%, outperforming comparable edge-deployed models by 15.28%. Our work bridges the gap between high-performance Transformer-based EEG decoding and practical edge deployment, enabling more accessible and portable MI-BCI systems for real-world applications.

title: Artificial Intelligence-Based Decision Support Systems for Enhanced Public Health Policy and Crisis Management
authors: Bhanuvardhan Nune, Balpreet Singh, Ernawati, Anshu Malhotra, Bakkathatla Swapna, Srinivasa Rao Bittla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: The article presents the Hierarchical Predictive Fusion Engine (HPFE) a new computational architecture that aims to optimize risk prediction of populations in large scale during changing health upheavals and intervention streaming. The system incorporates the heterogeneous streams of data, such as clinical indicators, behavioral patterns, environmental variables, mobility structures, etc., into a single dynamically adaptive architecture. The graph enhanced temporal encoder represents the dynamics of spatiotemporal propagation, and the behavior environment feedback module gives a representation of human behavior in a real world that is adapting to various changing environments. These elements input into a metaepidemic simulator that can produce multi-horizon scenario forecasts that indicate nonlinear risks development. In order to assist in grounded action decision making, an evolutionary optimization layer examines the intervention strategies against conflicting goals like maximum risk minimization, resource use and economic consequences. The framework pits in a confidence-regulated action engine that supports interpretable recommendations with quantified uncertainty thus permitting readable and reliable planning. Experimental tests indicate that HPFE does not only increase predictive robustness in noisy environment, with quickly changing situations, but also leads to the increased clarity and effectiveness of recommended response strategies. HPFE has the ability to standardize forecasting, behavioral modeling, simulation, and optimization under a common architecture to provide a revolutionary way to control high-stakes population-scale interventions in guiding and empowering capabilities in the face of large-scale disruption in the future. The suggested approach attains an overall accuracy of 93.3 percent.

title: Weather Condition Classification for Safe Drone Flight Using Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Marwa Boully, Aya Eddaniaoui, Majdouline Sabri, Fatimaazehra Jakjoud
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: This paper is about a system that helps keep drones safe. It uses computer vision to look at the weather and stop crashes from happening. The system was trained with a lot of pictures. 1,125 Of them. That show kinds of weather: cloudy rain, shine and sunrise. The drone safety system is really good at figuring out the weather it gets it right 83.1% of the time. It works fast it can look at each frame in just 48 milliseconds, which is very quick. This is because it runs on a device called the NVIDIA Jetson Nano.The drone safety system was compared to models like ResNet50, VGG16 and MobileNetV2. ResNet50 is a bit better at figuring out the weather it gets it right 2.1% more often. But it takes a lot longer to do it it needs 4.2 times time to process the information. The drone safety system is very good at telling the difference between rain and sunshine which’s very important for keeping drones safe.The system works with the Pixhawk flight controller using the MAVLink protocol. This means that when the drone safety system detects weather the drone can automatically fly back home. This work shows that using computer vision is a way to make drones safer it is cheaper and more efficient than using traditional weather sensors. The drone safety system is an alternative, for improving autonomous flight safety.

title: CSEval: A Framework for Evaluating Clinical Semantics in Text-to-Image Generation
authors: Robert Cronshaw, Konstantinos Vilouras, Junyu Yan, Yuning Du, Feng Chen, Steven McDonagh, Sotirios A. Tsaftaris
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Text-to-image generation has been increasingly applied in medical domains for various purposes such as data augmentation and education. Evaluating the quality and clinical reliability of these generated images is essential. However, existing methods mainly assess image realism or diversity, while failing to capture whether the generated images reflect the intended clinical semantics, such as anatomical location and pathology. In this study, we propose the Clinical Semantics Evaluator (CSEval), a framework that leverages language models to assess clinical semantic alignment between the generated images and their conditioning prompts. Our experiments show that CSEval identifies semantic inconsistencies overlooked by other metrics and correlates with expert judgment. CSEval provides a scalable and clinically meaningful complement to existing evaluation methods, supporting the safe adoption of generative models in healthcare.

title: Quantifying Latency and Jitter in Distributed Mobile Robotics Control: A Dual-Core RP2350 and Micro-ROS Ethernet Multicast Analysis
authors: Diogo F. Gomes, Paulo Costa, José Gonçalves, Ricardo Silva, Gil Gonçalves, Gonçalo Teixeira, Vítor H. Pinto
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Autonomous Robot Systems and Competitions (ICARSC)
abstract: The transition from centralized to distributed control architectures in mobile robotics introduces the challenge of "Temporal Coherence"—the requirement that physically separated actuators execute commands simultaneously. This paper presents a validation of standard Ethernet Multicast to achieve Soft Real-Time synchronization suitable for dynamic locomotion. An "Inertial Filtering Condition" is proposed, positing that if network jitter (σ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</inf>) is negligible relative to the robot’s mechanical time constant (τ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">mech</inf>), the system becomes functionally deterministic. Using a Dual-Core SMP architecture on RP2350 microcontrollers, this study demonstrates that standard Layer 2 Ethernet switches serve as effective hardware synchronizers. Experimental results show a packet-arrival reliability of 99.92% for a 1 kHz control loop and a network jitter standard deviation of σ ≈65 μs. Furthermore, a Virtual Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) stress test confirmed that inter-leg actuation skew is reduced to ≈ 275 μs, representing a 32x improvement over the 9 ms latency bottleneck observed in previous USB-Serial architectures. Finally, it is concluded that standard Ethernet transport provides sufficient statistical determinism for coordinated gait stability without requiring complex hard real-time protocols.

title: Constraint-Driven Control of SEPIC Converters Using Offline-Optimized MPC
authors: Rangoli Singh, Shiv Prakash, Dhawal Dwivedi, Sandip Ghosh, Chinmaya K. A.
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Model Predictive Control (MPC) is widely recognized for its exceptional constraint-handling and optimal control capabilities. However, its substantial computational burden poses a significant barrier to real-time implementation, especially in embedded systems with limited processing resources. To address this challenge, this paper presents the application of Explicit MPC, wherein control laws are computed offline, enabling ultrafast online execution with minimal computational overhead. The proposed strategy is applied to a 100 W Single-Ended Primary Inductor Converter (SEPIC), with real-time validation performed using dSPACE hardware. Experimental results demonstrate that Explicit MPC significantly outperforms conventional MPC and PI controllers in terms of transient response, steady-state precision, and disturbance rejection. Crucially, these enhancements are achieved with drastically reduced computational effort, positioning Explicit MPC as a highly practical and efficient solution for real-time control in modern power electronic systems.

title: Demist: Decoupled Multi-Stream Latent Diffusion for Quantitative Myelin Map Synthesis
authors: Jiacheng Wang, Hao Li, Xing Yao, Ahmad Toubasi, Taegan Vinarsky, Caroline Gheen, Joy Derwenskus, Chaoyang Jin, Richard Dortch, Junzhong Xu, Francesca Bagnato, Ipek Oguz
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Quantitative magnetization transfer (qMT) imaging provides myelin-sensitive biomarkers, such as the pool size ratio (PSR), which is valuable for multiple sclerosis (MS) assessment. However, qMT requires specialized <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20-30$</tex> minute scans. We propose DEMIST to synthesize PSR maps from standard T1w and FLAIR images using a 3D latent diffusion model with three complementary conditioning mechanisms. Our approach has two stages: first, we train separate autoencoders for PSR and anatomical images to learn aligned latent representations. Second, we train a conditional diffusion model in this latent space on top of a frozen diffusion foundation backbone. Conditioning is decoupled into: (i) semantic tokens via cross-attention, (ii) spatial per-scale residual hints via a 3D ControlNet branch, and (iii) adaptive LoRAmodulated attention. We include edge-aware loss terms to preserve lesion boundaries and alignment losses to maintain quantitative consistency, while keeping the number of trainable parameters low and retaining the inductive bias of the pretrained model. We evaluate on 163 scans from 99 subjects using 5-fold cross-validation. Our method outperforms VAE, GAN and diffusion baselines on multiple metrics, producing sharper boundaries and better quantitative agreement with ground truth. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/MedICL-VU/MS-Synthesis-3DcLDM.

title: ZVS Turn-on Integrated Triangular Current Mode Control for Three-Phase Three-Level Flying Capacitor Bidirectional PFC
authors: Nidhi Haryani, Ripun Phukan, Laszlo Huber, Anup Anurag, Juan Ruiz, Peter Barbosa
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: As power demand for on-board chargers (OBC) is increasing to meet the fast-charging requirements, 800 V DC bus voltage is being adopted for PFCs to reduce the copper losses in OBCs. For higher voltage operation, multi-level converter topologies employ the benefit of reducing device stress and common-mode voltage. The flying capacitor multi-level (FCML) converter has the added advantage of frequency multiplication. However, switching frequency is still limited by the high turn-on losses, hence ZVS turn-on integrated Triangular Current Mode (iTCM) control is presented in this paper for multi-level PFCs. As compared to Triangular Current Mode (TCM), iTCM has the benefit of very small high frequency current in the main boost inductor which is achieved by adding an LC branch to the converter such that the high frequency current is filtered by this branch. Hence, iTCM is introduced in this paper for three-level FC converter with one single common LC branch for ZVS turn-on of all the devices. The proposed technique is implemented on a GaN based three-level FC 800 V PFC at 11 kW. The efficiency achieved is 98.6% at a minimum inductor current frequency of 330 kHz.

title: Multi-Frequency and Locality-Guided Attention U-Net Gan for Limited-Angle CT Reconstruction
authors: Haytham A. Ali, Hiroyuki Kudo
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Limited-angle computed tomography (CT) reconstruction is a highly challenging inverse problem due to incomplete projection data, which leads to severe artifacts and loss of structural fidelity. Although several deep learning methods have been proposed, they often fail to address the dual challenges of frequency-domain degradation and spatial inconsistency. To address this challenge, we propose a novel reconstruction framework based on a U-Net GAN architecture enhanced with two key components: Multi-Frequency Guided Attention (MFGA) and Locality-Guided Self-Attention (LGSA). The MFGA recovers the missing frequency components by decomposing the input features into low, mid, and high frequency bands and enhancing degraded components in the Fourier domain using learnable masks. The LGSA introduces a locality-guided self-attention mechanism in which the query, key, and value are weighted by learned importance maps to focus on regions most affected by missing projections. The model is trained with a hybrid loss combining adversarial, perceptual, frequency-domain, and sinogramconsistency terms. Experiments on the Mayo Clinic dataset demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches, achieving superior PSNR and SSIM.

title: Vertical Shunt Resistor for Double-Sided Cooling Power Modules
authors: Sihoon Choi, Thiyu Warnakulasooriya, Jiyoon Choi, Jun Imaoka, Masayoshi Yamamoto, Keishi Nakamura, Susumu Toyoda, Koji Shigesawa
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This study proposes a novel vertical shunt resistor and its packaging into double-sided cooling power modules. The proposed shunt resistor has significantly low thermal resistance compared to that of lateral counterparts by utilizing large cooling surface and double-sided cooling. The proposed resistor can also employ a high-resistivity NiCr-based alloy, unlike conventional Mn–Cu-based alloys that exhibit instability at high temperatures. The manufacturing process of the power module and its overall manufacturability are described in detail. Two types of shunt-resistor-integrated power modules were prototyped, and design guidelines are provided to achieve a low temperature coefficient of resistance.

title: Explainable Fuzzy Autonomous Decision System for Multi-Country Human Capital Stratification
authors: Agnus Infanta B, M. Jayanthi
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Assessing national human capital development is important for strategic policymaking, conventional machine learning approaches usually struggle when the sample is small, highly uncertain mostly in socio economic datasets. This study introduces autonomous fuzzy inference system that will classify 20 major economies into four progressive human capital clusters: Emerging, Transitioning, Advanced and Knowledge Economy Mature. Utilizes six normalized indicators from World Bank sources, Mamdani-type fuzzy engine generates interpretable classifications via linguistic membership functions and automatically derived rules. Results show coherent, policy-relevant stratification with leading OECD economies classified as Knowledge Economy Mature, India, Indonesia as Transitioning and China as Emerging under a human-capital lens. Benchmarking against KNN and SVM classifiers using Leave-One out cross validation shows ML models collapse to majority class predictions (accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 45-50 \%, \kappa \approx 0$</tex>) highlighting their limitations in small N regimes. The fuzzy approach offers superior explain ability, uncertainty handling and robustness providing a valuable tool for international development benchmarking and long-term human capital strategy formulation.

title: An Automated Pipeline for Processing and Analysis of Clinical Fetal Diffusion Mri
authors: M. Cazzolla, A. Manchon, M. Milh, N. Girard, O. Coulon, F. Rousseau, G. Auzias
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: In this work, we present an automated pipeline for processing diffusion MRI (dMRI) data acquired in fetuses in clinical routine. In-vivo fetal dMRI provides critical insight into brain development but is challenging due to motion, low SNR ratio, and artifacts. These challenges are prominent in the clinical routine, with additional constraints on the duration of the scanning session. Existing state-of-the-art pipelines are designed for high-quality research acquisition protocols that are not feasible in routine clinical practice. We introduce a comprehensive framework that integrates robust opensource tools to handle clinical fetal dMRI, performing motion correction, anatomical standardization, and diffusion tensor estimation. The pipeline is complemented by an extended set of anatomical parcellations for the dHCP fetal atlas, enabling detailed regional analysis. Applied to a large clinical cohort (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N}=314$</tex> sessions), the pipeline successfully processed all datasets without manual intervention. Following quality control, the resulting diffusion metrics from the 152 datasets with the best quality score revealed developmental trajectories consistent with established literature, demonstrating the pipeline's validity and robustness. The code is available at https://github.com/MecaLab/Fetal_DWI.

title: Personalized Stress Detection Through Reinforcement Learning Based Domain Adaptation
authors: Swati Kochhar, Nishchala Thakur, Katja Heilmann, Lejla Colic, Laith Hamid, Tara Chand, Martin Walter, Veronika Engert, Deepti R. Bathula
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Personalized stress detection aims to model individual-specific physiological and behavioral patterns; however, such personalization requires effective knowledge transfer across users, particularly in data-scarce healthcare settings. A key challenge lies in identifying the most relevant source data for a new target user, as transferring knowledge from all sources can introduce negative transfer and degrade performance. To address this, we introduce Target-Aware Reward-Optimized Selection (TAROS), a reinforcement learning based framework that adaptively selects source samples most relevant to the target domain. The Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent learns optimal selection policies by approximating Q-values, ensuring that only informative and contextually aligned samples contribute to the adaptation process. We evaluate TAROS on a real-world, multi-user stress detection dataset characterized by high inter-subject variability. Experimental results show substantial improvements across multiple evaluation metrics compared to conventional methods, demonstrating that TAROS effectively mitigates negative transfer and significantly enhances personalized inter-subject adaptation.

title: Performance Analysis of Shaft Voltage in 3L-NPC fed Induction Motor using LS-PWM and PS-PWM with a Carbon brush
authors: Asad Hussain, Jyoti Ranjan Dash, Rohit Raj, Pramod Agarwal
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: In this paper, the effects on shaft voltage generated in an Induction motor driven by a three-phase, three-level NPC inverter operating under high switching frequency PWM techniques are presented. High-frequency switching operation generates common-mode voltage, leading to shaft voltage build-up and potentially damaging the bearings in the long run due to the circulating bearing current. Phase-shifted carrier PWM and Level-shifted carrier PWM are analyzed to determine their impact on the shaft voltage characteristics. Later, a sealed brass holder containing a carbon brush from Helwig Carbon Products is placed on the motor body perpendicular to the shaft as a mitigation tool. The results provide insights into the influence of high switching frequency PWM techniques and carbon brush on shaft voltage levels. Simulation and experimental-based studies are analyzed to assess how much shaft voltage is mitigated. Thus, protective measures against bearing failure can be developed to enhance the reliability and longevity of induction motor drive systems.

title: Noise-Aware Image Classification Using Deep Denoising Prior with Data-Driven Noise Modeling for Remote Sensing Application
authors: P. Jacquelin Mary, V. Nagarajan, J. Suganya Devi, C. Karthiga, N. Kavitha
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Image classification accuracy in real-world conditions is significantly degraded by various noise types such as Gaussian noise, speckle interference, atmospheric distortions, and sensor noise. These effects are especially severe in remote-sensing applications, where images are acquired under challenging environmental and hardware constraints. Traditional pipelines rely on separate preprocessing steps for denoising, which often remove important structural information. This paper proposes a Noise-Aware Classification Network (NAC-Net) that integrates a Deep Denoising Prior Module (DDPM) directly into a ResNet-50 architecture. The model jointly learns noise statistics and discriminative features through end-to-end optimization. Experiments on the CIFAR-10 dataset augmented with synthetic remote-sensing noise (Gaussian, speckle, motion blur) demonstrate that the proposed method achieves up to 14.7% improvement in accuracy under heavy noise compared to conventional preprocessing pipelines. The approach improves robustness, generalization, and stability and is suitable for remote-sensing platforms such as UAVs, satellites, and drones.

title: TAR-Diff: Diffusion-Based Ultrasound Video Generation for Medical Diagnosis with Temporal Attention Residuals
authors: Jisu Kim, Seok-Hwan Oh, Hyeon-Min Bae
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Deep learning methods have demonstrated remarkable potential in analyzing breast ultrasound images, offering advantages in clinical efficiency. However, while dynamic lesion changes are key for diagnosis, large-scale annotated video datasets are scarce, limiting the performance and generalization of vision-based models. We propose a diffusion-based framework that synthesizes realistic breast ultrasound videos while capturing temporal changes relevant to diagnosis. Through the integration of multi-scale conditioning and temporal attention residual layers, the model effectively learns to focus on relevant spatiotemporal features, improving the generation of coherent and clinically useful ultrasound video sequences. Extensive results on the BUSI and BUSV datasets are presented, evaluating the generated videos through qualitative and quantitative analyses as well as across downstream tasks including object detection and classification. The results demonstrate the high perceptual similarity and temporal consistency compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Bias Mitigation for Brain Age Prediction via Age Delta Correlation Loss
authors: Tianyi Ren, Juampablo Heras Rivera, Agamdeep Chopra, Mehmet Kurt
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Deep regression models for brain age prediction derived from T1-weighted MRI are known to suffer from the regression-to-the-mean bias, leading to overestimation in younger individuals and underestimation in older ones. Although post-hoc linear calibration is commonly used to correct the bias, this two-step approach produces a problematic biomarker for Alzheimer's disease classification, as it results in a suboptimal model. To address this, we introduce <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{L}_{\text{ADC}}$</tex>, a loss term designed to penalize the Pearson correlation between the brain-age delta and chronological age, which we integrate end-to-end with the standard Mean Squared Error (MSE). The proposed training pipeline enables balanced control of the accuracy-bias trade-off during model training without depending on post-hoc adjustments alone. Across ResNet-18 and VQ-VAE backbones, our method consistently reduces the bias (absolute error-age correlation) from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.54-0.64$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.20-0.28$</tex>, with only a modest increase in MAE for all the experiments. By penalizing age-dependent prediction trends during learning, this approach improves the scientific and clinical applicability and interpretability of the current brain age model while adding negligible computational overhead.

title: Optimization of Communication Techniques for Efficient WPT
authors: Giulia Segatti, Liyu Yang, Liang Jia, Li Wang, Yanchao Li, Stefano Saggini
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: In wireless power transfer systems, control relies on a communication loop between receiver and transmitter, a key factor for ensuring efficiency and reliability. In mobile devices, this loop is typically implemented through load modulation at the receiver, while the transmitter employs frequency-shift keying (FSK) by varying the full-bridge frequency. A common architecture combines load modulation with a buck converter for battery charging; however, the magnetic component of the buck results in a bulky implementation, making it unsuitable for compact form factors. Fixed-ratio switched-capacitor (SC) converters represent a promising alternative, providing higher power density and reduced size. Yet, their use disrupts the conventional load modulation mechanism, raising new challenges for the communication feedback path. This work presents a modeling approach for the regulation channel from the portable device to the charging station and proposes an optimized strategy compatible with fixed-ratio converters. The concept has been experimentally validated on a MagSafe-based prototype, demonstrating a communication system capable of sustaining high signal levels over a wide operating range with a load of up to 25 W.

title: Three-Phase Hybrid Rectifier with Symmetric DC-bus for More Electric Aircrafts
authors: Érico Chagas Guimarães, Marcus Evandro Teixeira Souza Junior, Danillo Borges Rodrigues, Luiz Carlos Gomes Freitas
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This work presents a proposal of a three-phase hybrid rectifier with symmetric DC-bus (TPHR-SDCB) for replacing Autotransformer Rectifier Unit (ATRU) in DC power distribution systems in More Electric Aircrafts (MEAs). The circuit topology derives from a simple Diode Bridge Rectifier (DBR) and a Four-Wire-Based Boost PFC converter associated with two Half-bridges resonant LLC converters (Boost PFC+LLC). The benefits of the proposed hybrid architecture are reduced system weight and higher efficiency, since the Boost PFC+LLC processes at most 15% of the rated power. The system’s performance was evaluated using a real time simulation platform. The proposed converter provides symmetric ±270V dc (540 V dc) from a three-phase 230V ac. The preliminary results obtained demonstrate the potential of the proposed solution for MEA application.

title: Al-Based Simulated Auto-Trading Bot for Stock and Cryptocurrency Markets using Technical Indicators
authors: Noel Kenny Chadipiralla, P Ameerulla Khan, Saran Raj Sowrirajan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: This paper proposes a new hybrid algorithmic trading The trading framework using Kaufman’s Adaptive Moving Average (KAMA) with FinBERT, a financial sentiment provided. model, and issue high-confidence buy/sell signals across equity and cryptocurrency markets. Unlike conventional strategies that static Simple Moving Average (SMA) crossovers, the pro- adjusts the sensitivity of its trend detection dynamically due to market efficiency, whereas a sentiment-gating mechanism removes signals that are technically correct but contextually unfavorable. We perform extensive backtesting on hourly price of Apple Inc. (AAPL) data and Bitcoin/USD (BTC/USDT) daily over a 12-month period using python libraries: yfinance and ccxt. It reached an ROI of $22.7 \%$ and max drawdown of $13.4 \%$, both being better than the SMA-based baselines for both assets classes. The architecture is fully modular, written in Python on Google Colab, and is planned to be used with broker APIs and deep sequence models in the future. The results offer a cross-asset and replicable validation of the effectiveness of adaptive multimodal trading strategies for academic research communities and practical applications in markets.

title: Least-cost generation expansion modeling for Karnataka for FY 2031–32
authors: Shristy Srivastava, Ammu Susanna Jacob
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Karnataka’s rapidly growing economy has led to a surge in energy demand, making it one of the top ten energy-consuming states in India. Therefore, it becomes essential to conduct a least-cost generation expansion planning study for the state’s power sector. This paper provides a simulation-based comprehensive least-cost planning methodology for Karnataka, which includes capacity expansion modeling to identify the optimal capacity additions required and production cost modeling to analyze the optimal dispatch with the new plant additions planned by the financial year (FY) 2031–32. The least-cost planning aligns with Karnataka’s energy requirements by FY 2031–32 and decarbonization goals, thereby serving the policy mandates. In this study, FY 2023–24 and FY 2031–32 were considered the base and target years, respectively. Constraints such as the renewable purchase obligation (RPO) target, reserves, spinning reserves, reliability requirements, and resource availability for Karnataka were applied. Results from the simulation indicate that a versatile mix of nonfossil fuel energy sources can meet Karnataka’s FY 2031–32 energy demand. Moreover, although some investment is required for the new plant additions, a significant decrease is observed in the state’s average power purchase cost for FY 2031–32. This study provides actionable insights to policymakers, utilities, energy planners, and stakeholders, enabling the planning for a resilient and economically efficient power sector in Karnataka by FY 2031–32.

title: A Study on Improved Automatic Pronunciation Detection of FSMN Pronunciation for High Quality Popularization and Universalization of National Common Language Scripts
authors: Yuanfang Ding
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: With the popularization of the national common language script, the accuracy of Mandarin pronunciation is of great significance in promoting national unity and cultural integration, so the research constructs an efficient and accurate automatic pronunciation detection system to assist the teaching and learning of Mandarin and improve the quality of pronunciation. The study proposes a pronunciation detection model based on an improved feed-forward sequential memory network. The study designs the improved structure of the deep feed-forward sequential memory network, introduces the tone detection and pronunciation goodness algorithms, and constructs a mediator corpus and a standard pronunciation corpus for model training and testing. The results show that the improved feed-forward sequential memory network model outperforms other models in several key performance indicators. The improved feed-forward sequence memory network achieves a detection accuracy of 96.3%, a recall rate of 95.5%, a precision rate of 95.9%, an F1 score of 2.0%, a mispronunciation rate of 93.2%, a weighted F1 score of 97.1%, and a missed detection rate of 2.9%. The study successfully designed an efficient automatic Mandarin pronunciation detection model based on an improved feed-forward sequential memory network, which significantly improves the accuracy and efficiency of pronunciation bias detection, and can improve the quality of the popularization of the national common language and characters and promote the standardization of the language and characters.

title: Towards ROI-Free Modality Translation for MRI with Test-Time Augmentation
authors: Jae Wan Park, Suhyun Kim, Geunrip Park, Seong Jae Hwang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) equips medical professions with comprehensive information to examine various regions of interest (ROI). Nevertheless, the acquisition of a sufficient amount of MRI modalities can be arduous due to limitations such as financial constraints or disabilities of the patient. Recently, researchers have suggested image-to-image translation between MRI modalities utilizing generative models. However, these models still suffer from the disparity between the data quantities of different ROI. In this research, we propose MRI Translation Test-Time Augmentation (MRI-TA), an original method that utilizes the modality representation of a ROI with sufficient data to augment the model for another ROI with lacking data during test time. We perform experiments using T1 and T2 modalities from BraTS2024, GTU and Mendeley Lumber datasets and show promising results in performance and robustness during data insufficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/11291996/spine-mri.

title: A 1295W/in3 160A Quad-Phase VPD Module with Flexible Ganging and Multiphysics Optimization for Large-Scale HPC Systems
authors: Haoyu Wang, Zhoutong Liu, Junxiang Xu, Xuliang Wang, Meng Xiong, Yilong Liu, Xiaodong Li, Jishun Cui, Yan Wang, Xiaosen Liu
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Driven by AI technology, high-performance computing processors impose increasingly stringent demands on power delivery architectures. Traditional lateral power delivery approaches encounter bottlenecks at the kilowatt level, whereas newly proposed vertical power delivery architectures can effectively reduce losses, enhance power density, and elevate power limits to support future requirements. This work presents a vertical power delivery (VPD) module design for next-generation large-scale high-performance computing (HPC) processors. A 3D bottom-in/top-out structure with copper pillar arrays shortens the vertical IR path, enabling 160 A per quad-phase module. An optimization framework addresses power integrity (PI) and thermal challenges via multiphysics analysis, allowing flexible ganging of modules while achieving 97% efficiency and an 84 °C full-load temperature in 10 kW-level HPC systems.

title: Transient Mitigation in Dual Active Bridge Converter Using an Auxiliary Converter with High-Frequency Selective Control for Load Step-Down Condition
authors: Sachith Wijesooriya, Sandun S. Kuruppu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Design and Control of Automation and Drive Systems (IDCD)
abstract: Dual Active Bridge (DAB) converters are widely used in electric vehicles, renewable energy systems, and solid-state transformers due to their high efficiency, galvanic isolation, and bidirectional power flow. Rapid load transitions, however, can cause significant voltage overshoot and prolonged settling, increasing device stress and reducing reliability. This paper proposes an auxiliary-circuit-based method for transient mitigation. The auxiliary circuit operates in Boost mode during load step-downs to absorb excess energy and suppress overshoot. The converter topology and control strategy are presented, with simulation and experimental validation. Simulation of a 15 kW DAB shows substantial overshoot reduction and faster settling, while a scaled prototype confirms improved transient response and stable voltage regulation, achieving about 60% overshoot reduction.

title: A Multimodal Deep Learning Framework for Prostate Cancer Risk Prediction Integrating Biparametric MRI, Radiology Reports, and Clinical Biomarkers
authors: Walid Mohamed, Ibrahim Abdelhalim, Tayseer Hassan, Amr AbdelAziz, Mohammed Badawy, Moumen El-Melegy, Asem Ali, Ali Mahmoud, Fatma Taher, Mohammed A. Ghazal, Sohail Contractor, Ayman El-Baz
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate prostate cancer risk stratification requires integration of heterogeneous clinical data sources that exhibit variable quality and complementary diagnostic information. We propose EFNet-SAF (Expert Fusion Network with Staged Adaptive Fusion), a multimodal framework that integrates Biparametric MRI (DWI, T2-weighted), radiology reports, and clinical biomarkers (e.g.,PSA, Age) through an instance-specific adaptive approach. The architecture employs modality-specific expert networks trained independently with pretrained encoders, followed by adaptive fusion that learns patient-specific modality importance. Hierarchical imaging processing exploits spatial correspondence between DWI and T2 sequences, while clinical biomarkers are incorporated through a learned weighting mechanism. Evaluated on 73 prostate cancer cases using 5 -fold cross-validation, EFNet-SAF achieved an AUROC of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.945 \pm 0.06$</tex>, significantly outperforming single-modality approaches (imaging: 0.842, text: 0.918, clinical: 0.747, all <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p<0.05)$</tex> and standard fusion baselines (early fusion: 0.825, late fusion: 0.861). Ablation studies show that each modality adds useful information, and removing text has the biggest effect on performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\triangle \text{AUROC}=0.137)$</tex>. The staged training paradigm and adaptive weighting mechanism facilitate resilient multimodal integration on constrained clinical data while preserving interpretability via acquired instance-level modality significance.

title: SolveX A Plug and Play AI Tutor for Multi Variable Math Word Problems
authors: V. Phani Sirisha, P. Harshitha, P. Naveen Kumar, V. Revanth, M. V. Ramakrishna, J. RamaDevi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: The automated resolution of Mathematical Word Problems (MWPs) represents a central challenge in artificial intelligence, requiring the transformation of unstructured natural language into formally valid mathematical representations. Although recent Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance in semantic understanding and text-to-equation generation, they remain susceptible to reasoning inconsistencies, hallucinated variables, and unreliable handling of multi-variable equation systems. The proposed system employs a structured orchestration strategy using LangChain and LangGraph to coordinate multiple specialized agents within a unified reasoning workflow. The pipeline consists of three key stages: (i) problem-type classification to enable adaptive prompt selection, (ii) structured extraction of variables and equations from natural language descriptions and (iii) deterministic equation consistency verification to ensure mathematical correctness.This architecture is a response to failure cases encountered in previous LLM-based methods, especially when dealing with complex problem classes featuring interdependent magnitudes. In addition to giving correct solutions, SolveX also provides answers with coherent solution step by step so as to improve transparency and educational value. We show that separating linguistic interpretation from verification-driven reasoning achieves 87% accuracy— 8 points higher than GPT—4-while improving robustness and explainability over end-to-end generative baselines.

title: KNN-Driven Intelligent Job Recommendation and Automated Applicant Tracking for Smart Campus Placement Systems
authors: Asutosh Tripathy, Chamala Pallavi, Ch Mahesh Babu, Sheo Kumar, Jangam Subbarayudu, Jalampelly Ramya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: In this paper, I will describe the Smart Campus Placement System (SCPS) which is a web-based tool that is based on the Django framework and automates the campus recruitment pipeline of academic institutions. The system offers student, Training and Placement Officers (TPOs) and recruiting company focused modules, replacing the normal disjointed manual processes of the traditional campus placement. The key automated processes are student registration, resume management, eligibility against established guidelines (CGPA, department, backlogs), job application status, scheduling of interviews, and publication of results. The technical contribution leading to publication is a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) recommendation engine which matches students to job opportunities by calculating Euclidean distance between TF-IDF weighted skill vectors, which exposes the five most compatible jobs per student. It was trained and tested on 215 student records of the CMR Engineering College (2022-26), providing an accuracy of 87.4, precision of 0.86, recall of 0.88 and a F1-score of 0.87, which is higher than Naive Bayes (F1: 0.79), Decision Tree (F1: 0.82), and SVM-RBF (F1: 0.85) baselines. The application of the system saved an average of 85 percent of shortlisting time per drive, minimized errors by 91 percent in data entry and increased the rate of institutional placement by 74 percent of 61 percent. These findings show that the SCPS is technically viable, has been shown to be measurably effective, and can be used with large groups of students.

title: Online Monitoring and Control of Power Converter Junction Temperature in Wave Energy Systems Using Artificial Neural-Network-Based Model Predictive Control
authors: Qiang Mu, Kamal Paul, Zaheen Mustakin, Jiale Zhou, Lucas Pereira, Babak Parkhideh, Tiefu Zhao
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Ocean waves are more consistent and predictable than solar or wind energy, enabling power generation for up to 90% of the time. Power converters represent one of the most vulnerable elements in wave energy systems, and their malfunction may cause severe system failures and substantial financial losses. Therefore, real-time temperature monitoring of power converters during operation has become increasingly important. At present, limited research has investigated online junction temperature monitoring of power semiconductor devices based on thermosensitive electrical parameters (TSEPs) methods. Implementing such techniques can effectively prevent single-device failures induced by transient thermal overstress, thereby enhancing the overall reliability of the converter. The junction temperature monitoring approach proposed in this paper employs the on-state resistance as a TSEP. To achieve this, a newly developed R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ds-on</inf> sensor board integrating dual voltage and current sensors has been designed. Based on junction temperature monitoring, a novel hybrid control strategy is proposed, which integrates artificial neural network (ANN)-baseed finite control set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) to reduce the junction temperature of SiC MOSFETs while maintaining low total harmonic distortion (THD), thereby improving the converter’s reliability and output voltage quality. The proposed hybrid control strategy is validated through simulations conducted in MATLAB/Simulink, with online junction temperature monitoring of power semiconductor devices implemented using PLECS. The results demonstrate that the proposed method enables real-time monitoring of the junction temperatures of all SiC MOSFETs in a three-phase inverter and achieves closed-loop regulation of their overall junction temperature while maintaining low THD.

title: Microstructure Informed Mamba Vision Masked Autoencoder for Personalized Brain Injury Detection from Diffusion MRI
authors: Shuxin Cao, Tenglong Wang, Yazhe Zhai, Jiahao Yu, Jiaolong Qin, Ye Wu
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Personalized brain injury detection is crucial for clinical diagnosis, yet current methods often overlook subtle injuries and diverse disease diagnoses. This study introduces MEMAE, a new approach that improves global context modeling during 3D dMRI reconstruction while maintaining local details. By leveraging reconstruction errors from healthy dMRI data, MEMAE enables personalized detection of brain injury. It employs three advanced diffusion models to generate a multidimensional microstructural measure, enabling analysis of heterogeneous brain injuries across various health conditions. Experimental results show that MEMAE can establish a robust prior knowledge base with minimal healthy control data, distinguishing between tumor infiltration and cocaine-induced axonal degeneration. This approach shifts the focus from group to individualized analysis, offering a promising solution for early detection of subtle injuries in precision medicine. The source code is available at https://github.com/csx1009/MEMAE.

title: Hybrid Resnet-Unetr for Semi-Supervised Fetal Cardiac Segmentation and CHD Classification
authors: Rodrigo Medellin-Robles, Flavio Alfonso Juárez-Castro, Mohammad A. Bukhari
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: We present a multi-task framework for simultaneous fetal cardiac structure segmentation (14 classes, 4 views) and congenital heart disease (CHD) classification (7 pathologies) from prenatal ultrasound, developed for the FETUS 2026 Challenge at ISBI. Our approach introduces a hybrid architecture combining a fully trainable ResNet-50 encoder with a UNETR-style decoder enhanced by deep supervision at four levels and a boundary detection head that fuses contour features into the decoding path. Classification is performed through a multi-scale attention pooling and a morphological feature head. We demonstrated that a two-phase trainable general-purpose framework can outperform a domain specific foundation model under label scarcity obtaining segmentation metrics of Dice = 73.32 and NSD = 48.76, while for classification Macro F1 = 32.64, with a score of 52.16/80., achieving first place in the challenge.

title: MPD-CXR: Diffusion Model with Multi-Perspective Semantic Conditioning for Chest X-Ray Synthesis from Report
authors: Avadhut Eknath Kabadi, Angshuman Paul
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Clinically accurate medical images, synthesized artificially, can facilitate data augmentation, and training of medical personnel. However, existing models often struggle to maintain fine-grained anatomical structures and faithfully represent pathological findings. We propose a diffusion framework to synthesize images from chest x-ray reports. Our model captures multiple perspectives from chest x-ray reports through time-aware text guidance, potentially acquiring anatomical detail and pathology. This contributes to the synthesis of accurate chest x-ray images from the reports. The quality of the synthesized images is further enhanced through noise initialization strategies and adversarial refinement that evaluate both image realism and consistency with the corresponding reports. Experiments on publicly available chest x-ray datasets show a 12.23 % improvement in FID compared to state-of-the-art approaches. The code is available at https://github.com/D23CSA001/MPD-CXR-Diffusion-Model-for-CXR-synthesis.git.

title: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Neural Networks: Bridging Deep Learning and Quantum Computing for Image Classification
authors: Aditya Kumar Jha, Rahul Bhandari, Raman Chadha
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Given the recent prominence of Noisy Intermediate Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices, hybrid quantum-classical neural networks have recently drawn a lot of excitement as a potential solution to improving learning efficiency. Our current paper describes a Hybrid Quantum-Classical Convolutional Neural Network (HQCNN) which is defined as a pretrained backbone of ResNet-18 and a small quantum-enhanced feature-transformation layer of Qiskit. The convolutional high-dimensional features are reduced to the classical eight-dimensional one, which in turn is encoded into a four-qubit parameterized quantum circuit. The resultant quantum expectation values are combined with the compressed features to come up with a hybridized representation of twelve dimensions, used to classify multi-class dataset. Experimental analysis of CIFAR10 data has shown that the suggested HQCNN achieves a test accuracy of 94.74% and improves class-wise precision, recall, and F1-scores than a number of existing hybrid models while using a significantly smaller number of latent features. More investigation into the quantum module indicates that it is predictable about scalability to batch, resistant to perturbations in features, and efficiently performed by evaluating circuit vectors. These results indicate that compact and expressive representations of images that can be helpful in the NISQ era can be acquired using hybrid architectures, thus providing a practical way to forward the benefits of quantum to modern deep-learning pipelines.

title: DermAI: Clinical Dermatology Acquisition Through Quality-Driven Image Collection for AI Classification in Mobile
authors: Thales Bezerra, Emanoel dos Santos, Kelvin Cunha, Rodrigo Mota, Fábio Papais, Francisco Filho, Natália Lopes, Érico Medeiros, Jéssica Araujo, Shirley Cruz, Paulo Borba, Tsang Ing Ren
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: AI-based dermatology adoption remains limited by biased datasets, variable image quality, and limited validation. We introduce DermAI, a lightweight, smartphone-based application that enables real-time capture, annotation, and classification of skin lesions during routine consultations. Unlike prior dermoscopy-focused tools, DermAI performs on-device quality checks, and local model adaptation. The DermAI clinical dataset, encompasses a wide range of skin tones, ethinicity and source devices. In preliminary experiments, models trained on public datasets failed to generalize to our samples, while fine-tuning with local data improved performance. These results highlight the importance of standardized, diverse data collection aligned with healthcare needs and oriented to machine learning development. The dataset will be made available for research purposes upon request to the corresponding authors.

title: Human Brain Excitation-Inhibition Balance Through Fmri Dynamics: Estimators and in-Vivo Validation
authors: Francesca Saviola, Matilde Bisi, Asia Ferrari, J. Karolis Degutis, Stefano Tambalo, Michael V. Lombardo, Jorge Jovicich, Dimitri Van De Ville
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Long-range temporal interactions of brain functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) signals offer a noninvasive window into human excitation-inhibition balance (EIB). We evaluated four Hurst exponent (H) estimation methods: continuous wavelet transform (CWT), discrete wavelet transform (DWT), detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA), and fractionally integrated noise (FIN) modeling. Validation first employed simulated fractional Gaussian noise and fractional Brownian motion signals of varying timeseries lengths, then phantom data to assess robustness to scanner noise. Restingstate fMRI and spectroscopy (MRS) data from 36 healthy participants enabled in-vivo validation by correlating H with biologicallyrelevant MRS-derived EIB in the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex. Estimation accuracy improved with longer series, and for short signals, DFA and FIN performed best. Phantom and in-vivo results demonstrate consistent, reproducible H estimation across methods. Crucially, H correlated negatively with EIB, supporting H as a meaningful noninvasive biomarker for EIB in cognitive and clinical neuroscience.

title: Overview of the CXR-LT 2026 Challenge: Multi-Center Long-Tailed and Zero Shot Chest X-Ray Classification
authors: Hexin Dong, Yi Lin, Pengyu Zhou, Xuan Zhong Feng, Alan Clint Legasto, Mingquan Lin, Hao Chen, Yuzhe Yang, George Shih, Yifan Peng
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Chest X-ray (CXR) interpretation is hindered by the long-tailed distribution of pathologies and the open-world nature of clinical environments. Existing benchmarks often rely on closed-set classes from single institutions, failing to capture the prevalence of rare diseases or the appearance of novel findings. To address this, we present the CXR-LT 2026 challenge. This third iteration of the benchmark introduces a multi-center dataset comprising over 145,000 images from PadChest and NIH Chest X-ray datasets. The challenge defines two core tasks: (1) Robust Multi-Label Classification on 30 known classes and (2) Open-World Generalization to 6 unseen (out-of-distribution) rare disease classes. We report the results of the top-performing teams, evaluating them via mean Average Precision (mAP), AUROC, and F1-score. The winning solutions achieved an mAP of 0.5854 on Task 1 and 0.4315 on Task 2, demonstrating that large-scale vision-language pre-training significantly mitigates the performance drop typically associated with zero-shot diagnosis.

title: Single-Stage Transformer-Based Multilevel Inverter with FCS-MPC Control for High-Performance Grid-Tied Applications
authors: Ahmed Ismail M. Ali, Mahmoud S. R. Saeed, Alaaeldien Hassan, Ahmed Hemeida, Mahetab Alam, Anouar Belahcen
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: DC–AC inverters, with voltage step-up capability, are commonly used in various power conversion applications, particularly in low/medium-power PV systems. Predictive control has been verified as an effective strategy for attaining faster and more precise system regulation. Finite-control-set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) enables the inclusion of additional terms to enhance the total-harmonic distortion and system dynamics. Nevertheless, uncertainties within the system model and the unpredictable behavior of grid voltages introduce significant challenges in practical implementations. This paper introduces a grid-connected single-phase transformer-based multilevel inverter (TMI), which is controlled using a simple MPC scheme. The proposed MPC-based TMI exhibits several features, including a reduced count of components, bulky filter mitigation, and good power density. A distinctive feature of the system is its high voltage step-up capability (up to 1:13), along with reduced THD (< 5%) and superior dynamic performance (i.e., < 5 ms settling time, and <5% system overshoot). The system has been verified using a 1.2 kW, 220 V laboratory set-up, confirming the practical effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: LOCBAM: Advancing 3D Patch-Based Image Segmentation by Integrating Location Context
authors: Donnate Hooft, Stefan M. Fischer, Cosmin Bercea, Jan C. Peeken, Julia A. Schnabel
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Patch-based methods are widely used in 3D medical image segmentation to address memory constraints in processing highresolution volumetric data. However, these approaches often neglect the patch's location within the global volume, which can limit segmentation performance when anatomical context is important. In this paper, we investigate the role of location context in patch-based 3D segmentation and propose a novel attention mechanism, LocBAM, that explicitly processes spatial information. Experiments on BTCV, AMOS22, and KiTS23 demonstrate that incorporating location context stabilizes training and improves segmentation performance, particularly under low patch-to-volume coverage where global context is missing. Furthermore, LocBAM consistently outperforms classical coordinate encoding via CoordConv. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/compai-lab/2026-ISBI-hooft.

title: MEA-YOLO: Medical Enhanced Attention YOLO for Simultaneous Polyp and Lumen Detection in Robotic Colonoscopy
authors: Jian Li, Fuxin Liang, Chen Zhang, Chi Kwan Lee, Sai Ho Ling
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Robotic colonoscopy requires accurate simultaneous detection of polyps and lumens for autonomous navigation. However, existing object detectors lack medical domain-specific designs. We present MEA-YOLO (Medical Enhanced Attention YOLO), a unified framework for simultaneous polyp and lumen detection that integrates domain-specific attention mechanisms tailored for colonoscopy imaging. Our novel C2f-MEA module combines efficient multi-scale attention with specialized edge and lumen enhancement branches, addressing the unique visual characteristics of colonoscopic images. Evaluated on the Extended-LDPolyp dataset (37,947 images with dual annotations, video-level split), MEA-YOLO achieves 74.52% mAP@0.5, outperforming YOLOv8n by 2.58% and Faster R-CNN by 41.65%. Task-specific evaluation shows 72.52% AP for polyp detection and 76.52% AP for lumen detection, with consistent improvements in both precision and recall. Ablation studies confirm that medical-specific enhancements are crucial-while generic attention mechanisms degrade performance, our domain-aware design contributes 2.58% mAP improvement. Despite adding only 6.25% parameters overhead, MEA-YOLO maintains real-time performance at 111.5 FPS, demonstrating that architectural innovations guided by clinical insight can achieve superior accuracy without sacrificing efficiency for robotic colonoscopy deployment.

title: Efficiency Enhancement of Three-Level Flying Capacitor Totem-Pole PFC with Trapezoidal Current Mode Control
authors: Bac-Bien Ngo, Zhigang Yao, Ziheng Xiao, Jingrui Liu, Yi Tang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The three-level flying capacitor totem-pole power factor correction (3L-FC-TP-PFC) converter is a multilevel PFC topology that offers significant benefits, including low voltage stress on the high-frequency leg and a reduced filter size. When operating in continuous conduction mode (CCM), the converter can achieve high efficiency; however, CCM operation suffers from inherent drawbacks, such as switching and reverse-recovery losses, which can limit overall performance. To address these challenges, this paper presents the application of trapezoidal current mode (TZCM) in 3L-FC-TP-PFC converters. By employing TZCM, zero-voltage switching (ZVS) is achieved for all high-frequency switches, reducing switching losses and improving overall efficiency. The operating principles and control techniques required to implement TZCM in the PFC are presented in detail. Moreover, to validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed approach, both simulation and experimental tests are conducted at a 500 W output power level.

title: AI-Driven Predictive Maintenance for IoT Devices in Smart Factories
authors: C. Renuga, R.V.R. Manisha, R.K. Harshavardini, M. Varshini, Shreeya Gayathri S S, Amita Biju
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: One of the solutions that have evolved to attain optimal value with AI in optimizing the overall performance of the IoT devices in smart factories is predictive maintenance. Predictive maintenance presupposes applying the machine learning algorithm and real-time sensor data to identify potential faults before their manifestations at the cost of minimized unpredictable downtime and maintenance costs. With the progress of Industry 4.0, AI and IoT are important in predicting maintenance to become effective, more dependable, and efficient. Moreover, we address such problems as data quality, computational complexity, and the cost of their implementation. This AI-based analytics will guide you in proposing the best way of operation and therefore ensure that intelligent factories are operational with minimum disruptions.

title: Transient and Steady-State Short-Circuit Fault Analysis in Triple Active Bridge DC/DC Converters
authors: Md Didarul Alam, Iqbal Husain
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents a time-domain analytical framework for DC-terminal short-circuit fault analysis in Triple Active Bridge (TAB) DC–DC converters. Unlike prior harmonic-domain approaches limited to post-fault steady-state behavior, the proposed method captures both transient and steady-state fault dynamics under open-loop operation. Closed-form expressions for instantaneous, peak, and RMS leakage-inductor currents are derived, accounting for phase-shift modulation, port-voltage imbalance, and leakage-inductance redistribution. The fault current severity strongly depends on pre-fault operating conditions as expected and even with fixed hardware parameters. Analytical, simulation, and experimental results validate the proposed framework, which provides practical guidance for semiconductor rating, transformer leakage design, and reliable TAB operation under DC-side faults.

title: Federated Deep RAG for Privacy-Preserving Knowledge Intelligence in Edge–Cloud IoT
authors: Latha J, Karthick Murugan S, Sumathy S, Lissy D, S. Jayachandran, Shunmugavadivoo T
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has introduced significant security challenges, particularly in detecting and interpreting malicious network activity across distributed and resource-constrained environments. Existing intrusion detection approaches either rely on centralized data aggregation, which raises serious privacy concerns, or lack the contextual reasoning capacity needed to produce interpretable incident summaries from heterogeneous IoT telemetry. Conventional federated learning (FL) classifiers address data locality to some degree, yet they still fall short in evidence-grounded reasoning and exhibit vulnerability to membership inference attacks. This paper proposes a Federated Deep Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework that integrates privacy-preserving FL with a deep semantic retrieval pipeline to simultaneously perform IoT event classification and evidence-backed incident summarization across distributed edge nodes. Experiments were conducted on the TON_IoT dataset comprising 600,000 records distributed across 10 non-Independent and Identically Distributed (non-IID) federated clients covering six traffic categories including Normal, Denial-of-Service/Distributed Denial-of-Service (DoS/DDoS), Injection, Malware, Scanning, and Password/Bruteforce. The proposed framework achieved an accuracy of 0.971, a macroF1 of 0.956, a Recall@5 of 0.931, and an attack Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.51 under membership inference evaluation, outperforming all baseline configurations across retrieval, classification, and privacy dimensions. The research results demonstrate that federated retrieval-augmented architectures provide an operational solution which maintains user privacy for detecting IoT intrusions at large scales.

title: Modeling, Nonlinear Analysis, and Design of LLC Converter under Digital Impedance Shaping Frequency Adaptation Control
authors: Dipayan Chatterjee, Santanu Kapat, Indra Narayan Kar
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Extended describing function (EDF) and reduced-order models are widely employed to derive small-signal transfer functions (TF) for PID controller design of digitally controlled LLC converters under pulse-frequency modulation (PFM). However, achieving well-damped, high bandwidth (BW) transients with linear control remains challenging, as higher-order TFs are highly sensitive to parameter variations and load or input changes. In contrast, discrete-time (DT) models accurately capture small-signal behavior and enable high BW operation with enhanced dynamic performance. Existing digital solutions can achieve near-optimal response but often require additional voltage/current sensors, fast computation, precise parameter information, and still exhibit limited step-load dynamics and poor robustness. This paper presents a generalized DT modeling framework for the analysis of full-bridge LLC converter by characterizing the dynamic behavior across all operating modes and defining the boundary conditions between adjacent modes. Using this model, a digital PID controller is designed to achieve well-damped, load-invariant transients at high BW. Thereafter, a hybrid digital nonlinear frequency adaptation technique is developed using a commercial microcontroller (MCU) to enhance transient performance and closed-loop output impedance using only voltage sensing. The effectiveness of this technique and nonlinear analysis are validated through a hardware prototype.

title: Feasibility of Zinc-Ion and Zinc-Air Batteries for Electric Vehicles
