authors: Shriram S. Rangarajan, Chandan Kumar Shiva, Vedik Basetti, Sachidananda Sen, Nirmalya Mallick, Geetha Manoharan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: Zinc ion batteries and zinc air batteries are safe, cheap, and very energy-intensive, so they can be used in cars rather than lithium-ion batteries. Zinc batteries are safer because they use oxygen that is not burned as active cathode material. Zinc batteries have many good features, but because zinc dendrites form, cycle life is short, and reactions that decrease oxygen and release oxygen into air anodes are slow, they cannot be used constantly. Zinc anodes also reduce the size and weight of batteries, occupying more energy than other types of batteries. When air is used as an active material, it can greatly reduce the weight, size and cost of batteries. Zinc metals with copper are designed to prevent corrosion and improve chemical stability and electrochemical mechanisms. Using new technologies such as 3D electrodes and microfluid devices, zinc and zinc battery batteries make them better functional, last longer and easier to extend. This gives an advantage in terms of energy storage at a less cost. This paper brings out the highlights of Zinc ion and Zinc air batteries that will benefit the Electric Vehicles in the form of a brief review.

title: A Benchmark Analysis of Graph and Non-Graph Methods for Caenorhabditis Elegans Neuron Classification
authors: Jingqi Lu, Keqi Han, Yun Wang, Lu Mi, Carl Yang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: This study establishes a benchmark for Caenorhabditis elegans neuron classification, comparing four graph methods (GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT, GraphTransformer) against four non-graph methods (Logistic Regression, MLP, LOLCAT, NeuPRINT). Using the functional connectome, we classified Sensory, Interneuron, and Motor neurons based on Spatial, Connection, and Neuronal Activity features. Results show that attention-based GNNs significantly outperform baselines on the Spatial and Connection features. The Neuronal Activity features yielded poor performance, likely due to the low temporal resolution of the underlying neuronal activity data. Our benchmark validates the use of GNNs and highlights that Spatial and Connection features are key predictors for Caenorhabditis elegans neuron classes. Code is available at: https://github.com/JingqiLuu/neuronclf-gnn-benchmark.

title: High-Accuracy Recognition of Yemeni Sign Language Using ConvNeXt Architecture with Focal Loss and Advanced Data Augmentation
authors: Mohammad Qatawnah, Khaled Hamad, Suhaila Abuowaida, Nawaf Alshdaifat, Mo'men Malkawi, Mohammad Alshinwan
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Sign Language Recognition Systems are important tools that will enable Deaf/Hard of Hearing (DHH) individuals to communicate with hearing people and bridge the gap between DHH communities and the hearing populations. Research has been done on ASL (American Sign Language) and ArSL (Arabic Sign Language), but there is limited research on regional sign language variants. This paper explores this gap by developing a deep learning-based sign language recognition system for Yemeni Sign Language, which is a regional variant of Arabic Sign Language that is spoken in Yemen. A ConvNeXt-Tiny architecture was developed with focal loss to address potential class imbalance issues, and data augmentation techniques were also used. The data augmentation techniques included those from the Albumentations library. The system was tested on a dataset of 33 classes of YSL (Yemeni Sign Language) signs, containing 4850 test images. The system achieved a high level of accuracy, with a test accuracy of 99.92%, a validation accuracy of 99.96%, and an F 1 score of 99.99%. This demonstrates that, if modern CNN architectures are appropriately optimized with proper loss functions and augmentation methods, they can have nearly perfect recognition rates for regional sign language variants. The proposed method provides a basis for creating accessible communication technologies for the Yemeni DHH community and can be applied to other under-represented sign language systems.

title: Deep-Learning-Based Energy-Conscious Scheduling of Green Cloud Data Centers
authors: Lakshminarayanan S, C. Rohith Bhat
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The growing power of cloud data centers is a serious cost and environmental problem, and traditional workload scheduling methods have no flexibility to dynamically and heterogeneous clouds. The paper puts forward a Deep reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based energy-aware scheduling system to reduce the power consumption in the data center with the purpose of providing the optimal resource use and the high-quality compliance with the Service-Level Agreement (SLA). The scheduling problem is posed as a sequence of decisions and addressed with the help of a Deep Q-Network (DQN) which is trained on optimal policies of task-to-server allocation given realtime system conditions as server utilization, energy consumption and workload characteristics. Comprehensive simulations of a heterogeneous cloud environment prove that the given DRL-based scheduler attains the best results of the total energy consumption (up to 34.4 percent), average server utilization (up to 68.7 percent), and SLA violations (3.2 percent) as compared to FCFS, Round Robin, threshold-based, and heuristic schedulers. These findings show that learning-based scheduling is a good balance to both energy and service performance. The framework proposed illustrates the opportunities of DNR as a scalable and adaptative control system of sustainable operation of cloud data centers and offers a base of intelligent and carbon-conscious cloud management systems to come.

title: SemiETPicker: Fast and Label-Efficient Particle Picking for CryoET Tomography Using Semi-Supervised Learning
authors: Linhan Wang, Jianwen Dou, Wang Li, Shengkun Wang, Zhiwu Xie, Chang-Tien Lu, Yinlin Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Cryogenic Electron Tomography (CryoET) combined with sub-volume averaging (SVA) is the only imaging modality capable of resolving protein structures inside cells at molecular resolution. Particle picking, the task of localizing and classifying target proteins in 3D CryoET volumes, remains the main bottleneck. Due to the reliance on time-consuming manual labels, the vast reserve of unlabeled tomograms remains underutilized. In this work, we present a fast, label-efficient semi-supervised framework that exploits this untapped data. Our framework consists of two components: (i) an end-toend heatmap-supervised detection model inspired by keypoint detection, and (ii) a teacher-student co-training mechanism that enhances performance under sparse labeling conditions. Furthermore, we introduce multi-view pseudo-labeling and a CryoET-specific DropBlock augmentation strategy to further boost performance. Extensive evaluations on the large-scale CZII dataset show that our approach improves F1 by 10 % over supervised baselines, underscoring the promise of semisupervised learning for leveraging unlabeled CryoET data.

title: Adaptive Gated Fusion for Robust Water Body Segmentation in Cloud-Covered Environments
authors: P Anish, D Rajalakshmi, Sanjay M, Kaviya Senthil Kumar
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Low light, haze, and clouds all interfere with optical water mapping. The image may occasionally be partially usable. There are times when it is completely unusable. This paper develops a system that learns which to rely on at each pixel using both optical and SAR data. Tests consistently revealed the same issue. Over clouds and shallow water, optical alone was ineffective. At the edges, SAR lost detail but remained stable. Both problems were resolved by using both in conjunction with the network's decision of which to trust. IoU outperformed optical only models by two to four points in every test region. The edges were more precise and clean. Areas missed by optical models were accurately identified.

title: First Demonstration of Buck Converter using Diamond MOSFET
authors: Sora Kawai, Shunsuke Shoji, Nobutaka Oi, Ryo Yoshida, Kosuke Ota, Hiroyuki Uchida, Akihiko Watanabe, Kenji Ueda, Takanori Isobe, Hiroshi Kawarada, Ichiro Omura, Tatsuya Fujishima
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Diamond is an ultra-wide bandgap material with outstanding properties such as high thermal conductivity and high breakdown electric field, making it a promising candidate for next-generation power devices. Diamond MOSFETs using two-dimensional hole gas (2DHG) with low on-resistance, high critical breakdown field and fast switching characteristics have been reported. However, despite these advances, the demonstration of continuous switching and power converter operation using diamond MOSFETs, which is essential for evaluating their practical potential in power electronics applications, has not yet been achieved. In this study, we demonstrate the operation of a first buck converter using a cascode diamond MOSFET. The converter successfully steps down the input voltage even at a switching frequency of 400 kHz. These results indicate that diamond MOSFETs have the potential to improve power density by enabling high-speed switching, which enables downsizing of passive components, and by taking advantage of their high thermal conductivity, which enables downsizing of cooling systems.

title: DCQG-DETR: Dynamic Query-Based DETR for Remote Sensing Object Detection
authors: Jiahao Han, Zhe Hu, Xinge Jv, Yang Li, Tao Cui
publish: 2026 International Conference on Vision, Advanced Imaging and Computer Technology (VAICT)
abstract: To address the challenges of large scale variations, sparse object distribution, and complex backgrounds in remote sensing object detection, existing CNN-based methods often rely on static queries, which struggle to adapt to complex scenes. To this end, this paper proposes a DETR-based method with dynamic queries. Specifically, a feature alignment network is employed to fuse multi-scale features, and a dual-branch attention mechanism is designed to evaluate spatial saliency and semantic importance, adaptively selecting the most informative locations. Meanwhile, content queries and initial bounding box parameters are dynamically generated, enabling the decoder queries to fully perceive the image content. On the NWPU VHR-10 dataset, the proposed method achieves 91.9% Map, significantly improving the detection performance for small and medium-sized objects, meeting the requirements of remote sensing object detection. This work provides a novel dynamic query paradigm that enhances the adaptability of Transformer-based detectors in complex remote sensing scenes, offering potential applications in aerial surveillance, urban planning, and environmental monitoring.

title: Seamless Integration of Passive Islanding Detection and Low Voltage Ride-Through in Synchronverter-Based Grid-Forming Inverters
authors: Lipun Kumar Naik, Animesh Kumar Sahoo, Sanjib Kumar Panda
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Traditional islanding detection and low-voltage ride-through (LVRT) mechanisms are often implemented separately in inverter control. This separation can lead to conflicts during grid disturbances, especially in systems with high-penetration inverter-based resources (IBRs). Grid-forming inverters (GFMIs), particularly synchronverter-based designs, must satisfy both anti-islanding and LVRT requirements under evolving grid codes. This paper proposes a seamless integration strategy that combines passive islanding detection with LVRT functionality for synchronverter-based GFMIs. A coordinated control framework is developed, unifying a novel passive islanding detection function (IDF) with a voltage-dependent LVRT response. The IDF uses virtual flux, inverter current, and virtual speed deviation as key indicators. A time-domain logic discerns between unintentional islanding and grid faults within 30 ms. This ensures the timely activation of either disconnection or LVRT protocols. Simulation results on an IEEE 1547-based test system validate the approach. The method maintains voltage support during fault-induced sags and detects islanding reliably within 80 ms. It significantly reduces the non-detection zone (NDZ) without compromising system stability. The proposed strategy provides an efficient and practical solution for next-generation synchronverter-controlled GFM Inverters.

title: Infrastructuring Street-Level Imagery in Post-Disaster Contexts: Lessons from the 2024 Noto Peninsula Earthquake CSCW021
authors: Noriyasu Obushi, Kensuke Otsuyama, Masahiko Inami

title: Doubly minimized Petz & sandwiched Rényi mutual information: Operational interpretation from binary quantum state discrimination
authors: Laura Burri
abstract: The doubly minimized Petz Rényi mutual information of order α is defined as the minimum of the Petz divergence of order α of a given bipartite quantum state relative to all product states. The doubly minimized sandwiched Rényi mutual information is defined analogously, with the Petz divergence replaced by the sandwiched divergence. In this work, we study certain binary quantum state discrimination problems related to correlation detection. We show that the corresponding direct exponent is determined by the doubly minimized Petz Rényi mutual information of order α ∈ (1/2, 1), and that the strong converse exponent is determined by the doubly minimized sandwiched Rényi mutual information of order α ∈ (1,∞). This provides an operational interpretation of these types of Rényi mutual information and generalizes previous results for classical probability distributions to the quantum setting. For completeness, we also study the corresponding moderate deviation regime both below and above the threshold, and determine the Stein exponent and the second-order asymptotics.

title: Optimal Two-Sided Pricing and Service Introduction Strategies for Competing Platforms_supp1-3694200.docx
authors: Lipan Feng
abstract: Nowadays, many two-sided platforms are actively launching new services beyond their main service to reach a broader range of consumers (e.g., A map adding local lifestyle services in addition to its navigation service). As users migrate across different services, the original two-sided pricing mechanisms are altered due to network effects. Furthermore, different service introduction strategies adopted by competing platforms also reshape the competitive landscape. In this paper, we develop a game-theoretical model to examine the optimal pricing strategies of two competing platforms when introducing new services. We theoretically show that when platforms introduce new services, they typically reduce consumer-side fees or even offer subsidies. However, on the provider side, whether platforms adjust their pricing strategies depends on the service introduction strategies of their competitors. Counter intuitively, the profitability of new services does not depend solely on their development costs; the intensity of market competition also leads platforms to adopt different equilibrium strategies. When competition is intense, platforms may fall into a “prisoner's dilemma” when introducing new services. In contrast, when competition is weak, introducing new services is more likely to result in a win-win outcome. Finally, we show that service complementarity increases the likelihood of a prisoner's dilemma but does not affect the win-win outcome. We further extend the analysis by endogenizing the user conversion rate and introducing asymmetric consumer base utility, and show that our main findings remain robust.

title: A Robust Grid Interactive SPV fed EV Battery Charger with SASNPAA Controller
authors: Dheeraj Kumar, Sanjeev Singh, Shailendra Kumar
abstract: Increasing electrical energy demand driven by rapid population growth has placed significant pressure on conventional energy resources. Moreover, the depletion of fossil fuels and rising environmental pollution have accelerated the need for alternative, renewable energy sources such as solar, wind, biomass, and tidal energy. This paper presents a charger model that integrates these non-conventional energy sources with the electrical grid. A single-phase grid-interactive charger is developed with solar photovoltaic (SPV) for electric vehicle (EV) battery. In grid-connected charger with SPV array, conventional adaptive controllers often become ineffective under nonlinear load conditions and rapid fluctuations in solar irradiance, adversely affecting convergence speed and system stability. To address these challenges, a Sparse Andrew's Sine Norm Promoting Adaptive Algorithm (SASNPAA)-based control mechanism is proposed. The SASNPAA approach enhances disturbance rejection capability and ensures faster convergence, resulting in improved power quality and reliable operation of the PV-grid fed charger. The proposed control strategy estimates the weight of fundamental component of load current. This estimated weight is further integrated with the SPV contribution and DC-link voltage component for effective control of the voltage source converter (VSC). Additionally, an EV battery is incorporated to enable bidirectional power flow under dynamic conditions, including charging and discharging, along with varying load demands. The system is tested under varying solar insolation, grid disturbances, and load conditions to demonstrate its robustness. Simulated results are obtained using MATLAB/Simulink which confirm fast convergence and reduced mean square error (MSE). Experimental validation on a laboratory-scale setup further demonstrates the practical applicability of the proposed method.

title: HD-MUNet: integrating artificial intelligence and high-density electromyography for motor unit number estimation
authors: M. Gagliardi, A. Ortiz, A. Conte, D. Belvisi, G. Leodori, G. Fabbrini, G. Ferrazzano, T.M. Vieira
abstract: Objective: In this study we developed and evaluated the performance of HD-MUNet: a novel method for motor unit number estimation (MUNE), integrating high-density surface electromyograms (HD-sEMG) with artificial intelligence (AI). Methods: We designed a dual-branch neural network to process raw M-waves elicited by electrical stimulation and their incremental changes through convolutional and recurrent layers, producing stepwise estimates of recruited motor units (MUs). Because no experimental gold standard exists for counting MUs, a physiologically realistic model of the medial gastrocnemius was used to simulate HD-sEMG. Simulations included variations in MUs population (20-50-100-150-200-250-300), subcutaneous tissue thickness (4-8 mm), signal-to-noise ratio (0-8-16 dB), and stimulation protocol (50-100-200 steps), yielding 5040 subjects. HD-MUNet performance was benchmarked against two state-of the-art methods: Incremental MUNE and StairFit. Results: HD MUNet achieved consistently low median relative errors (≤ 7%) across conditions with high test-retest reliability (Pearson r > 0.995), whereas StairFit and Incremental MUNE showed strong sensitivity to noise and stimulation scenarios, with median errors ranging from −2% to −195% and −20% to −66%, respectively. Of practical relevance, differently from the other two methods, HD MUNet performed equally well for the three stimulation steps considered. We confirmed this result on a set of experimental data. Conclusion: By combining HD-sEMG with AI, HD-MUNet achieved robust and reliable MUNE across a broad range of simulated conditions, independently of stimulation protocol. Significance: This method has immediate potential to simplify MUNE protocols in both healthy and pathological conditions, with direct applicability to clinical neuromuscular disease diagnosis and monitoring.

title: Fast Time-Aware Shaper Scheduling for In-Vehicle Networks via Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Mohammadparsa Karimi, Majid Nabi, Andrew Nelson, Kees Goossens, Twan Basten
abstract: Modern vehicles increasingly rely on distributed computing platforms that exchange large volumes of sensor and control data with strict timing requirements. Ensuring that this traffic meets its deadlines over Ethernet-based in-vehicle networks requires Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) and, in particular, effective configuration of the Time-Aware Shaper (TAS). However, generating and updating TAS schedules that remain valid as traffic patterns evolve is an NP-hard problem that traditional optimization or heuristic methods address only partially. This paper introduces a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) scheduler that learns to configure TAS schedules directly from network state while preserving standard compliance through analytical validation. The proposed DRL scheduler encodes the scenario (network topology and workload) of the in-vehicle network using a Graph Neural Network (GNN) and learns scheduling policies that balance deadline satisfaction, latency, and resource utilization. Evaluation on a comprehensive benchmark shows that the proposed approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art heuristics and a topology-specific DRL baseline, achieving higher success rate and lower delay while maintaining efficient bandwidth use. Once trained, it can adapt to new traffic scenarios within milliseconds, demonstrating the potential of the DRL-based scheduler as a foundation for adaptive and reliable communication in next-generation software-defined vehicles.

authors: N/A

title: Image-Guided Video Retrieval Using ResNet-50 and FAISS Indexing
authors: B. Sathiyaprasad, K M Santhosh, Sanjay B
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The proliferation of online video material in exponential rates has posed a difficulty in the effective search and retrieving of the material. The common methods of traditional search markets are far much reliant on the manual tagging, captions, or metadata that are time-consuming, error-prone as well as lack scalability. SceneMatch will deal with this issue by providing content-based video retrieval when a single query image is provided. The system uses literature techniques of deep learning and similarity searching in order to detect visually similar frames in large video databases. The work is very fundamentally based on the ResNet-50, a trained convolutional neural network, to assist high-dimensional feature vectors that will represent the semantic content of frames. These vectors express shapes, textures and objects automatically without having to be manually labeled. The retrieved features are indexed with the use of FAISS, a high-performance similarity search library, which enables nearest-neighbor queries in seconds regardless of search size it is sub-optimized with millions of vectors. Preprocessing of videos is managed with the help of OpenCV which enables a systematic extraction of frames in a video file at a fixed time interval. The type of metadata that includes timestamps, frame paths and video names are recorded in the JSON to ensure it can be easily mapped to the results and the primary content. It is equipped with a simple Streamlit interface enabling users to upload a query picture and get a search result of matching video clips in real time, with visual previews and times. This design provides a high level of scalability, performance, and usability and SceneMatch can be used across several applications in surveillance, media analysis, creative application tool, and entertainment. Moreover, the system itself is easy to extend, the additional, e.g., text-to-video search (with the use of CLIP) or action recognition network (with dynamic scene retrieval) models may be added later. SceneMatch exhibits the potential of an AI smart-based solution to video search as it removes the need to be labeled manually.

title: Predictive Analytics Using AI to Reduce Batch Failures and Deviations in GMP-Regulated Environments
authors: Ramineni Snehamrutha
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The increased complexity of contemporary production operations demands anticipatory defects and explicatory data concerning the causes of quality issues, and unexpected machine breakdowns lead to critical operational failures, downtime and excessive maintenance expenses. To overcome these obstacles, the proposed study suggests an AI-based predictive analytics model of machine failure and manufacturing defect prediction based on two Kaggle datasets, which are publicly available. Feature normalization utilizing a standard scale, comprehensive data cleaning (removing duplicates), and SMOTE to alleviate the issue of class imbalance will be part of its approaches. Accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC were used as performance measures to assess a range of ML models, including Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting (GB), and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). The performance of the SVM in predicting machine failures was the most impressive, as evidenced by the experimental results, which showed an accuracy of 93.64 %, an F1-score of 93.75 %, and a ROC-AUC of 97.95 %. Tuned Gradient Boosting performed better <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(97.34 \%, 97.37 \%$</tex>, 99.41 %) with 97.34 % accuracy, 97.37 % F1-score and 99.41 % ROC-AUC on manufacturing defect detection. Moreover, the cost of failure of the machines and manufacturing defects were found to be reduced by 91.9 % and 98.3 %, respectively, which showed cost-efficiency and cost-effectiveness of the proposed structure in predictive maintenance and quality control.

title: Analysis of Crosstalk in Solid-State Circuit Breakers Equipped with Transient Voltage Suppression Diodes
authors: Yang Xu, Xingyue Tian, Samuel J. Klein, Ching-Hsiang Yang, Ruirui Chen, Zheyu Zhang, Hua Bai, Fred Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Crosstalk in voltage source converters (VSCs) occurs when the fast-switching transition of one device induces unwanted gate or drain voltage transients in its complementary device, causing unintended turn-on or turn-off. A similar effect can also exist in solid-state circuit breakers (SSCBs) even without a phase-leg structure, because the high dv/dt produced by the transient voltage suppression (TVS) diode during fault interruption can be coupled into the main switch gate loop and create a negative gate-voltage undershoot. This work analyzes the crosstalk mechanism in TVS-diode-based SSCBs by identifying the dominant parasitic coupling paths and explaining the reason why only the negative gate-voltage transient is critical. Two mitigation methods suitable for SSCBs, i.e. Zener-diode clamping and external drain-source capacitance, are introduced and experimentally verified. Tests on a 1.7 kV, 100 A prototype show that both methods can suppress the gate-voltage undershoot well. These findings provide practical guidance for improving the robustness and reliability of fast SSCBs using SiC MOSFETs with TVS diode clamping.

title: Single-Loop Output Power Control Strategy for Standalone Single-Phase Inverters in Dynamic Load Applications
authors: Akshat Saini, Ajit Kumar Upadhiya, N. Lakshminarasamma
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: This paper presents a simplified output power control strategy for a single-phase inverter, suited for applications where the load parameters are unknown or varying. Various control strategies based on both stationary and synchronous reference frames exist. Typically, widely adopted methods rely on synchronous reference frame transformations; however, in single-phase applications, the addition of orthogonal imaginary components introduces delays into the system, degrading the dynamic response and making it slower and oscillatory. Whereas, stationary frame-based approaches struggle to eliminate steady-state error while tracking sinusoidal references. The proposed method provides a simplified approach by computing the averaged output power and employing a tuned PI controller to track the reference load power with zero steady-state error. The key advantages of this approach lie in its simplicity, easier tuning, reduced computational burden, and ease of practical implementation, making it particularly suitable for high-frequency, power-sensitive applications. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method in maintaining required power delivery.

title: MambaME: A Mamba-Based Framework for Efficient and Accurate Joint Microstructure Estimation from Diffusion MRI
authors: Taohui Xiao, Cheng Li, Wenxin Fan, Ruoyou Wu, Sheng Zhang, Enqing Dong, Qiegen Liu, Shanshan Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Brain microstructure estimation using diffusion MRI (dMRI) enables quantitative characterization of brain tissue organization. Traditional dMRI models typically estimate isolated parameters, leading to fragmented interpretations. Recent deep learning approaches have explored joint estimation across multiple diffusion models, but often suffer from limited computational efficiency and accuracy. In this study, we propose MambaME, a Mamba-based framework for efficient and accurate joint microstructural estimation across multiple diffusion models. By leveraging the Mamba architecture, MambaME captures long-range dependencies in high-dimensional dMRI signals, enabling effective multi-model estimation. A joint estimation loss is further introduced to improve optimization across diffusion models. Experiments on the Human Connectome Project (HCP) dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of MambaME for joint Diffusion Kurtosis Imaging (DKI) and Neurite Orientation Dispersion and Density Imaging (NODDI) estimation. Compared with five state-of-the-art methods, MambaME achieves superior accuracy while providing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3-10 \times$</tex> faster inference than existing deep learning-based microstructural estimation approaches such as q-DL, MESC-SD, and HGT. Overall, MambaME substantially improves computational efficiency while also enhancing estimation accuracy.

title: CASeS: Concurrent Contingency Analysis-Based Security Metric Deployment for the Smart Grid
authors: Ashish Reddy Kotla
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: This paper introduces CASES, a new architecture of real-time security evaluation of smart grids that breaks down some of the most vital constraints of traditional cyber security strategies. The framework combines dynamically motivated security measures and real-time contingency analysis to test the resiliency of the system to coordinated cyber-physical attacks. In contrast to sequential techniques, CASES uses parallel processing in order to monitor several security dimensions at the same time, such as confidentiality, integrity, and availability, by adaptive thresholding, graph-based network modeling. Experimental assessment of IEEE test systems and real-world data show that multi-vector attacks are detected with 96.3 percent accuracy, 34.7 percent false positives are reduced and 28.9 percent faster time to detect advanced persistent threats. The architecture provides grid operators with a higher situational awareness and mitigation actions that act as a strong response to cyber incidents in the modern smart-grid setting where cyber incidents directly impact physical infrastructure.

title: Multi-Port Fast EV Charging Architecture with Renewable Integration and Adaptive Filtering for Power Quality Enhancement
authors: Pavan Kumar Singh, Divyank Srivastava, Bhim Singh, Arunima Verma, Saurabh Mani Tripathi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: This paper presents a robust and intelligent fast DC electric vehicle (EV) charging architecture integrating a photovoltaic (PV) array and a battery energy storage system (BESS) through a bidirectional DC-DC converter (BDC). System is designed to support dual fast-charging stations: a 33 kW charger for heavy EVs operating at 420 V and a 10 kW charger for light EVs (LEV) such as e-rickshaws operating at 120 V. Both chargers utilize dual active bridge (DAB) converters, enabling bidirectional power flow, which gives vehicle-to-grid (V2G) capability. PV system follows a double-stage configuration, employing a boost converter between PV array and voltage source converter (VSC). BDC converter maintains DC link voltage stability and charges/ discharges the battery using excess PV power in constant/variable power mode. A three-phase VSC interfaces common DC link with grid and manages power exchange under grid-connected mode (GCM) and standalone mode (SAM). To enhance dynamic response and power quality, a nearest Kronecker product decomposition-based generalized hyperbolic secant robust adaptive filter (NKP-GHSRAF) is implemented for generating VSC reference currents. This filter demonstrates strong harmonic suppression, fast convergence, and resilience to noise, ensuring compliance with IEEE-519 standard. VSC operates under current control in GCM and under voltage control in SAM to support stable and seamless operation. Simulation results in MATLAB/Simulink validate effectiveness of presented system in maintaining system stability, minimize total harmonic distortion (THD), and enabling smooth transitions across operational modes.

title: Analyzing Deep Learning Architecture for Glioma Segmentation and Non-Invasive Grading from Multi-Modal MRI Images
authors: Ajatray Swagat Bhuyan, Neha Dutta
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Gliomas are also one of the most malignant brain tumours that need to be accurately and non-invasively graded, because invasive biopsy is not always possible. To solve this, we came up with the GSG U-Net, a multi-task deep learning (DL) model that was trained to segment and predict the grade of glioma at the same time using multi-modal MRI. The model uses a general encoder and a dual-domain attention mechanism to successfully combine spatial and pathological features. Comparing GSG U-Net to the BraTS 2020 dataset, the average Dice score of tumour segmentation was high at 87.66. Its multi-task classifier (MTC) had a strong prognostic performance with an AUC of 0.946 on internal validation, and an excellent generalisation with an external AUC of 0.860. These findings indicate that multi-task learning offers a more precise and holistic non-invasive measure of gliomas than the conventional single-task models, which implies that it can be used to improve clinical decision-making.

title: Perceived Influence of AI on Organisational Decision-Making in the UK Manufacturing Sector
authors: Mohammad Rizwan, Irfan Ahmed
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: The research is based on the impact of artificial intelligence (AI) on the decision-making process in organisational settings within the UK manufacturing sector. The present survey focuses on potential artificial intelligence (AI) applications in the decision-making processes of UK manufacturing organisations in accordance with the feedback provided by 50 participants. The study employed a quantitative survey instrument to gather data of perceived effects of AI technologies, namely, machine learning and predictive analytics. The paper will focus on the connection that exists between AI technologies, such as machine learning and predictive analytics, and decision-making. Findings indicate AI has a positive impact on decision-making particularly in enhancing operation efficiency and productivity. However, it has been identified that data security problem and the shortage of skills are impediments to AI adoption. The statistical analysis has moderate positive correlation between increased productivity and AI adoption <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(r=0.63)$</tex>. The reliability test revealed that the alpha of Cronbach was high at 0.85 that the responses used in the survey are consistent. The practical implications of the research are focusing on the fact that the barriers should be overcome with the help of a special set of employee training programs that would guarantee the successful implementation of AI. The research would be informative to manufacturing companies that aim to apply AI in improving their operations and business decisions.

title: Planar Magnetics in Switched-Mode Power Supplies: Achieving Compact, High-Density and IP65-Rated Performance
authors: Jonathan Jabraj Nadar, Srikanthan Sridharan
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The growing demand for high-density power solutions in computing, avionics, DC lighting, and isolated industrial applications necessitates efficient power processing within compact volumes. Further, outdoor and industrial environments require designs with adequate ingress protection. This paper presents the design, simulation and experimental validation of a planar magnetics based IP65 rated 48 V, 750 W Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS). Finite element analysis was used to optimize the winding pattern by balancing DC and AC losses in the windings. The design demonstrates that planar magnetics enable compact, efficient and cost-effective SMPS without compromising performance for demanding industrial and automotive applications. A combination of planar magnetics and wide-bandgap semiconductors results in a high power density converter. The resulting converter has a power density of 1 W/cc (16 W/in<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>), achieved due to the compact form factor and the dual thermal conduction paths for the planar magnetics.

title: An Evaluation of Rare-Earth-Free External Rotor Permanent Magnet Assisted Synchronous Reluctance Machine for Micro-Wind Turbines
authors: MD Rashedur Rahman, Md Khurshedul Islam, Md Moniruzzaman, Seungdeog Choi, Shuhui Li, Yang-Ki Hong
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This study evaluates the suitability of a rare-earth-free external rotor permanent magnet-assisted synchronous reluctance machine (REF-Ex-PMaSynRM) for use in wind turbine generators. The push toward higher power density in wind farm installations makes traditional permanent magnet synchronous generators (PMSGs) appealing, but their widespread adoption is constrained by the significant cost fluctuations and supply chain volatility associated with rare-earth (RE) permanent magnets (PMs). While RE PMs provide exceptional magnetic field strength, crucial for achieving high torque density in compact machine designs, their vulnerability to demagnetization at high operating temperatures further limits their long-term reliability and effectiveness in demanding applications. This research focuses on utilizing rare-earth-free (REF) permanent magnets as a highly promising alternative. These magnets offer a dual advantage: significantly lower cost and substantially improved thermal compatibility compared to their RE counterparts. This specific machine design was fabricated and initially subjected to testing under high-speed, low-torque conditions. The core contribution of this work lies in the detailed evaluation and analysis of this REF-Ex-PMaSynRM's performance characteristics—specifically how its design and operational parameters are adapted and optimized for the low-speed, high-torque environment that is characteristic of direct-drive wind turbine generator applications. The objective is to demonstrate that the REF-Ex-PMaSynRM can offer a viable, sustainable, and cost-effective high-torque solution without relying on critical rare-earth materials

title: Selective Harmonic Self-Elimination with Curve Fitting Method for a Five-Level Single Phase Inverter with Fixed DC voltage
authors: Sayantika Mukherjee, Pratik Kalkal, A. V. Ravi Teja
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: In a five-level single-phase voltage source inverter, a modified selective harmonic elimination approach is proposed in this paper that can eliminate n<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sup> harmonics and their multiples. To find the switching angles for a particular modulation index, a curve fitting technique is utilized to construct the polynomial equation with the proper order as a function of modulation index. The proposed method is tested in both simulation and hardware for elimination of triplen harmonics, and the results are presented. This method shows that all of the triplen harmonics are self-eliminated across a 43% range of modulation index.

title: EMI Filter Design for Electric Vehicle with PCB-Integrated Capacitors
authors: Genzhai Peng, Yu Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The adoption of 800V electric drive systems and SiC power devices has intensified electromagnetic interference, imposing stricter requirements on the high frequency performance of EMI filters. This paper proposes an optimized DC side EMI filter design that combines parameter optimization with PCB-integrated capacitors to reduce parasitic inductance and improve high frequency performance. A CLCLC topology is employed, with the final stage utilizing PCB capacitors. Experimental results validate the proposed methodology's effectiveness.

title: Time-Domain Modeling of Dead Time in a 1-MHz, 5-kW GaN-Based LLC Converter
authors: Aqarib Hussain, Kristen Booth
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Traditional dead-time selection methods developed for kHz-range LLC converters are inadequate for MHz, kW-scale designs employing GaN devices. This paper presents a closed-form time-domain equation for dead-time determination based on the nonlinear output charge of GaN switches. Because GaN devices lack a body diode, incorrect dead-time selection can increase third-quadrant conduction losses and compromise zero-voltage switching (ZVS). The proposed formulation accurately predicts the minimum dead time required to fully discharge the device output capacitance with the resonant current, thereby ensuring robust ZVS and reducing switching and conduction losses. The method is validated through detailed circuit simulations and experimentally demonstrated on a 1-MHz, 5-kW LLC resonant converter, confirming its suitability for high-frequency, wide-bandgap-based resonant power stages.

title: Neuropsychiatric Deviations from Normative Profiles: An MRI-Derived Marker for Early Alzheimer Disease Detection
authors: Synne Hjertager Osenbroch, Lisa Ramona Rosvold, Yao Lu, Alvaro Fernandez-Quilez
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Neuropsychiatric symptoms (NPS) such as depression and apathy are common in Alzheimer's disease (AD) and often precede cognitive decline. NPS assessments hold promise as early detection markers due to their correlation with disease progression and their non-invasive nature. Yet current tools cannot distinguish whether NPS are part of aging or early signs of AD, limiting their utility. We present a deep learning-based normative modelling framework to identify atypical NPS burden from structural MRI. A 3D convolutional neural network was trained on cognitively stable participants from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative, learning the mapping between brain anatomy and Neuropsychiatric Inventory Questionnaire (NPIQ) scores. Deviations between predicted and observed scores defined the Divergence from NPIQ scores (DNPI). Higher DNPI was associated with future AD conversion (adjusted OR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=2.5; p<$</tex> 0.01) and achieved predictive accuracy comparable to cerebrospinal fluid <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{A} \beta 42$</tex> (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{AUC}=0.74$</tex> vs 0.75). Our approach supports scalable, non-invasive strategies for early AD detection.

title: A Novel Radiopathomic Signature for Oncotype Dx® Prediction in Breast Cancer
authors: Arianna Defeudis, Claudia Iuliani, Giulia Nicoletti, Eleonora Battista, Greta Vercella, Alessia Demichelis, Manuela Durando, Isabella Castellano, Valentina Giannini
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: The Oncotype DX® Recurrence Score (RS) is essential for guiding adjuvant chemotherapy decisions in early-stage ER+/HER2- breast cancer, but it is costly, centralized, and not always accessible. This study developed a radiopathomic approach to estimate RS from routinely available data. A radiomic model trained on MRI using LightGBM achieved an AUC of 0.74 on an external test set, while the pathomic model using ElasticNet reached an AUC of 0.74 in cross-validation. While multimodal fusion improved discrimination in 20 paired cases (AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.74$</tex>; balanced accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.77$</tex>). Integration with Ki67 and PGR within a nomogram further enhanced prediction (AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.76$</tex>). High specificity and NPV (0.93 and 0.88, respectively) supported the reliable identification of lowrisk patients, potentially helping reduce unnecessary chemotherapy. To our knowledge, this represents the first integrated radiopathomic signature for Oncotype DX® prediction, highlighting the feasibility of noninvasive genomic risk estimation and the potential of multiomic AI to support personalized treatment guidance in ER+/HER2breast cancer.

title: Radgaze-LLM: Anatomical Region-Grounded Radiology Report Generation via Learning From Expert Gaze
authors: Jamalia Sultana, Ruwen Qin, Zhaozheng Yin
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: The increasing demand for fast and accurate diagnoses places significant strain on radiologists. Automated radiology report generation offers a solution by alleviating the time-intensive task of manual report writing. However, existing methods generate reports from broad image-level features, often overlooking critical anatomical regions. We propose a simple yet effective radiology report generation method (RadGazeLLM) that learns from expert eye-gaze data and anatomical regions. Using three-pronged multi-modal prompts that integrate expert eye-gaze, anatomical regions, and image features, RadGaze-LLM achieves state-of-the-art results on MIMIC-CXR and an overall <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 9 \%$</tex> relative improvement on IU-Xray over the strongest prior method.

title: Local $K$-Similarity Constraint for Medical Federated Learning with Label Noise
authors: Sanskar Amgain, Prashant Shrestha, Bidur Khanal, Alina Devkota, Yash Raj Shrestha, Sengryul Baek, Prashnna Gyawali, Binod Bhattarai
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Federated learning enables leveraging private medical data from multiple institutions, without requiring the actual data to be shared. However, medical datasets in clients often exhibit erroneous annotations, affecting the robustness of the resulting global model. Most existing strategies to handle noisy clients in federation assume that a sufficient number of clients with clean labels are available, which can be leveraged to learn a robust global model while dampening the impact of noisy clients -an impractical assumption in real-world healthcare settings. In such scenarios, it is important to locally regularize the clients before communication with the server, to ensure the global model isn't corrupted by noisy clients. In this work, we propose a novel approach for effectively leveraging self-supervised pretrained model from the natural domain to regularize the local client training on medical data. This regularization, when applied with standard cross entropy objective, significantly improves performance, outperforming existing state-of-the-art federated methods across two medical image classification benchmarks. Unlike techniques that rely on self-supervised pretrained initialization, our method does not require the pretrained model and classifier backbone to share the same architecture, making it architecture-agnostic.

title: Fine-Grained Image Reconstruction for Generalizable Gaze Estimation
authors: Xucong Liu, Sanbo Ding
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Gaze estimation is a crucial cue for understanding human visual behavior. With the advancement of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), gaze estimation models have achieved significant improvements. However, due to differences between the training and target datasets in terms of head pose, illumination, and subject identity, these models often suffer from large estimation errors in cross-dataset applications. To address the challenge of domain generalization in gaze estimation, we propose a Fine-Grained Image Reconstruction framework (FGIR) to enhance the generalization ability of gaze estimation models without accessing target domain data. FGIR comprises two key modules: the Refinement Module (RM) and the Image Reconstruction Module (IRM). RM improves the conventional gaze estimation network by independently regressing each gaze angle, thereby enhancing the prediction accuracy for both yaw and pitch. IRM reconstructs input images to suppress irrelevant features and mitigate domain discrepancies, ultimately improving the model’s generalization capability. Experimental results demonstrate that when trained on the Gaze360 dataset and evaluated on the MPIIFaceGaze dataset, FGIR significantly outperforms baseline methods in both accuracy and generalization performance.

title: Short-Term Memory Single Object Tracking Method Based on Transformer
authors: Linhan Zheng, Jingyu Wang, Xinru Zhang
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: In recent years, Single Object Tracking (SOT) has become increasingly important in the field of computer vision and has made significant progress. However, it still faces challenges in complex scenarios such as fast-moving targets, occlusions, or deformations. Although existing two-stream, two-stage tracking frameworks demonstrate excellent performance, The decoupled design of feature extraction and relation modeling limits target perception accuracy and fails to fully exploit temporal cues. To address these issues, this paper uses a Transformer-based short-term memory single object tracking method (Stm_SOT), which introduces a short-term memory module to dynamically integrate historical frame information, thereby improving tracking robustness.

title: Experimental Evaluation of MPTCP Congestion Control Algorithms in Multi-Interface Linux Environments
authors: Abdelkareem Zidan, Jihad Khlouf, Mohammad M N Hamarsheh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: Multipath TCP (MPTCP) enhances connection throughput, reliability, and overall performance by enabling a single TCP flow to traverse multiple sub-paths. In this study, we conducted an experimental evaluation on a Linux-based testbed (Ubuntu 14.04, MPTCP) comprising dual-interface clients and servers connected via two broadband routers, each limited to 5 Mbps per link. Four MPTCP congestion control algorithms—LIA, OLIA, BALIA, and wVegas—were assessed under varying congestion conditions. The results indicate that MPTCP consistently outperforms standard TCP, with BALIA and wVegas exhibiting superior responsiveness under congested scenarios, while all algorithms demonstrate similar packet retransmission performance in uncongested conditions.

title: FocusSDF: Boundary-Aware Learning for Medical Image Segmentation via Signed Distance Supervision
authors: Muzammal Shafique, Nasir Rahim, Jamil Ahmad, Mohammad-Reza Siadat, Khalid Malik, Ghaus Malik
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Segmentation of medical images constitutes an essential component of medical image analysis, providing the foundation for precise diagnosis and efficient therapeutic interventions in clinical practices. Despite substantial progress, most segmentation models do not explicitly encode boundary information; as a result, making boundary preservation a persistent challenge in medical image segmentation. To address this challenge, we introduce FocusSDF, a novel loss function based on the signed distance functions (SDFs), which redirects the network to concentrate on boundary regions by adaptively assigning higher weights to pixels closer to the lesion or organ boundary, effectively making it boundary aware. To rigorously validate FocusSDF, we perform extensive evaluations against five state-of-the-art medical image segmentation models, including the foundation model MedSAM, using four distance-based loss functions across diverse datasets covering cerebral aneurysm, stroke, liver, and breast tumor segmentation tasks spanning multiple imaging modalities. The experimental results consistently demonstrate the superior performance of FocusSDF over existing distance transform based loss functions. Code is available at GitHub.

title: CoPose: Generalizable Multi-Robot 6DoF Pose Estimation with Cross-View Perception
authors: Jiale Ren, Yaxuan He, Hong Liu, Hua Wang
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Estimating the 6DoF pose of unseen objects remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision and robotics, especially in generalizable and real-world settings. We propose CoPose, a collaborative pose estimation framework designed for multi-robot systems, which leverages shared multi-view observations and reference-view-based perception to enable accurate and scalable object pose estimation. Unlike prior generalizable methods that rely on high-quality 3D object models, or depth maps, CoPose operates entirely on RGB images and only requires a small set of posed reference images of the target object. It integrates robust multi-scale object detection with a cascaded Transformer-based refinement module that explicitly models cross-view geometric consistency. This design allows the system to effectively integrate complementary cues from different perspectives, enhancing pose accuracy and robustness. Extensive experiments on LINEMOD and GenMOP benchmarks show that CoPose achieves state-of-the-art results, with ADD-0.1d accuracy improvements of 4.91% with 64 reference views on LINEMOD over prior methods, validating the effectiveness of our method.

title: A Deep Learning Framework for Real-Time Cyberattack Detection Using Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
authors: Anusha Sowbarnika Veluswamy
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Cybersecurity is crucial in the prevention of external attacks on hardware, software, and data, mostly in systems that have active internet connections. Organizations implement cybersecurity measures to restrict access to their records and systems to only authorized individuals. Spear-phishing, drive-by assaults, password attacks, denial-of-service attacks, and phishing are all a major security issue. This paper suggests that deep learning models, specifically Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT), will be used as a novel method of cybersecurity development. The transformer-based paradigm BERT examines and understands sequential data, including network traffic and system logs, to reveal complex patterns. The LSTM networks employ temporal dependencies to identify the anomalies that are brought about by real-time attacks. The suggested model was trained using both labeled cyberattack data and the system's regular operation in order to identify zero-day attacks and precisely determine the intensity of attacks. The hybrid BERT-LSTM model performs better than standard machine learning models in terms of detection rate and false positive reduction, according to experimental results. The technology can be successfully used to automate cybersecurity processes such as intrusion detection and malware classification in a variety of sectors.

title: A-star Algorithm-Based Automated Routing for Multilayer PCB
authors: Chanhee Park, Kyeongpil Kim, Jeongwoo Son, Gyunghoon Park, Seung-Hwan Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The continuous increase in functional density and operating frequency in modern electronic systems has intensified the design challenges associated with multilayer printed circuit boards (PCBs). Beyond ensuring basic connectivity, high-speed and high-density layouts must simultaneously satisfy signal integrity (SI), power integrity (PI), and electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) constraints. However, conventional maze-search-based autorouting algorithms, such as Lee’s algorithm and the A search method, primarily focus on minimizing routing length and computational effort, and thus fail to address multiobjective requirements including via minimization and conflict-free routing in three-dimensional (3D) design environments. In this study, an enhanced multilayer PCB autorouting algorithm is proposed. The proposed method extends the conventional A algorithm by adopting an eight-direction Chebyshev distance heuristic, a composite cost function that incorporates routing length and via penalties, and a dynamic re-routing mechanism for conflict resolution. The algorithm was evaluated using a representative two-layer dc–dc converter PCB layout. The proposed approach achieved 100% routing completion while reducing the average routing length by 21.55%, the number of vias by 50.00%, and the search time by 55.71% compared with the conventional A router. The results verify the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm for dense, constraint-driven PCB layouts and demonstrate its potential extension to SI- and EMC-aware autorouting in high-performance electronic systems.

title: Analysis and Implementation of a Series-Capacitor Zero-Bias Trans-Inductor Voltage Regulator
authors: Shuyu Zhang, Yicheng Zhu, Nathan Biesterfeld, Robert C. N. Pilawa-Podgurski
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes and demonstrates a series-capacitor zero-bias trans-inductor voltage regulator (SC-ZB-TLVR) for data center power delivery applications, along with its ${i_{{L_{\text{C}}}}}$-based control strategy. Compared to conventional series-capacitor buck converters, the interphase magnetic coupling enhances the load-transient response while maintaining small inductor current ripple. DC flux bias in the indirect-coupled inductor cores is canceled through flux injection from the last phase compensation inductor current, enabling reduced core size and improved power density. In this work, we show how the inherent current balancing among phases may not hold under non-ideal magnetic coupling. A phase-shift modulation strategy is proposed to fully remove the DC flux imbalance. The ${i_{{L_{\text{C}}}}}$-based control strategy is proposed and validated in simulation, enabling the use of the current sensing output of DrMOS and greatly reduces the system complexity. Finally, the proposed topology is verified on a 24 V-to-1 V, four-phase, 120 A experimental prototype.

title: A Non-invasive Cattle Identification Using Muzzle Pattern Recognition in Livestock Management
authors: Alexander D. Maralit, Roselyn A. Maaño, Pedro Jose C. De Castro, Jose B. Tan, Ronaldo C. Maaño
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: In the Philippines, small hold or backyard cattle farms play a vital role in food security, contributing about 85% of beef production. However, issues such as theft, fraud, and ownership conflicts remain common, especially in decentralized cattle marketplaces. This project introduces a non-destructive cattle identification system that uses muzzle pattern recognition to enhance livestock management. A deep learning-based application was developed using TensorFlow, Keras, and OpenCV to identify cattle by analyzing their muzzle patterns, offering a nondestructive, non-invasive, tamper-proof, cost-effective, and secured livestock identification. The application is deployed as a web application which is hosted on Hostinger it was tested using five cattle in Sariaya, achieving a score of 91.69%, 92.61%, 93.62%, 87.75%, and 94.27%, additionally five cattle is from Pakistan from the open-source dataset which was able to have an output of $96.85 \%, 97.22 \%, 96.39 \%, 94.81 \%$, and 97.99% and was able to generate a 91% of model accuracy. User Acceptability is used to evaluate the system which is based on ISO/IEC 25010:2011 and was answered by nine respondents in where they were asked to use the application at least 3 times to test the application, and they are required to input new entries of cattle and identify individually. It was able to generate an overall weighted mean of 4.26 which resulted that the users strongly agree that the system was able to achieve functionality, reliability, and usability as a supportive tool for livestock management.

title: Cost-effective and Flexible Bias Generator for SiC MOSFETs in High-Power Applications
authors: Sumana Ghosh
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents a novel transformer-less solution for generating flexible gate voltage in SiC MOSFETs. This circuit is implemented in a digitally controlled 4kW totem pole power factor correction (PFC) circuit using a topside cooled leadless (TOLT) SiC device. By utilizing zener diodes, capacitors, and resistors, the design efficiently produces negative gate voltage at first switching cycle, offering a cost-effective alternative to traditional solutions. It supports flexible gate voltages generation enhancing suitability of SiC for high-power applications. The paper discusses design benefits, hardware design, heat sink assembly, and performance comparisons for negative and 0V turn-off operations with TOLT and TOLL package-based totem pole PFC design.

title: Infrared-Visible Image Fusion for Transformer Bushing Inspection Using Enhanced YOLOv8
authors: Zhenxing Ye, Shuchen Yang, Bixiang Guo, Xiaochun Zhang, Zhenyi Xu, Jun Huang, Shumi Zhao
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: This paper proposes an enhanced YOLOv8-based approach for transformer bushing defect detection, leveraging the fusion of infrared and visible images to significantly improve detection accuracy in scenarios involving small targets and dense occlusions. The proposed method incorporates a spatial-depth convolution module to strengthen the model’s sensitivity to local features and employs an efficient multi-scale attention module to enhance cross-scale feature representation. Extensive experiments conducted on the self-built dataset demonstrate that the improved model achieves a detection accuracy of mAP50 = 0.918 and a mean precision of 0.915, surpassing the original YOLOv8 framework. Additionally, ablation studies and comparative analyses illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed modules and the superiority of the overall approach. The presented method provides a high-accuracy and efficient solution for transformer fault diagnosis with practical engineering significance.

title: Enhancing Chest X-ray Classification Performance Through Contrast Stretching and MobileNetV2
authors: Maulisa Oktiana, Mutia Salima, Muharratul Mina Rizky, Ahmad Jazlan
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Chest X-ray imaging is widely used for diagnosing pulmonary diseases. However, suboptimal contrast and image noise can degrade image quality and limit deep learning models’ ability to extract discriminative features. This paper proposes the integration of contrast stretching as an image enhancement technique to improve the performance of a MobileNetV2-based chest X-ray classification system. Experiments were conducted on 854 chest X-ray images collected from Dr. Zainoel Abidin Regional General Hospital, Banda Aceh, Indonesia, comprising three classes: Normal, Pneumonia, and COVID-19. The experimental results show that the proposed approach improves classification accuracy from $91 \%$ to $95 \%$, with consistent gains in precision, recall, and F 1 Score. The findings suggest that contrast stretching improves image quality and supports more effective feature learning in the lightweight MobileNetV2 architecture, contributing to better classification performance while maintaining low computational complexity.

title: Harmonization Mitigates Diffusion MRI Scanner Effects in Infancy: Insights From the Healthy Brain and Child Development (HBCD) Study
authors: Elyssa M. McMaster, Gaurav Rudravaram, Michael E. Kim, Trent M. Schwartz, Chloe Scholten, Jongyeon Yoon, Adam M. Saunders, Andre T. S. Hucke, Karthik Ramadass, Emily M. Harriott, Steven L. Meisler, Simon N. Vandekar, Allen Newton, Seth A. Smith, Saikat Sengupta, Kathryn L. Humphreys, Sarah Osmundson, Daniel Moyer, Laurie E. Cutting, Bennett A. Landman
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: The HEALthy Brain and Childhood Development (HBCD) Study is an ongoing longitudinal initiative to understand population-level brain maturation; however, large-scale studies must overcome site-related variance and preserve biologically relevant signal. In addition to diffusionweighted magnetic resonance imaging images, the HBCD dataset offers analysis-ready derivatives for scientists to conduct their analysis, including scalar diffusion tensor (DTI) metrics in a predetermined set of bundles. The purpose of this study is to characterize HBCD-specific site effects in diffusion MRI data, which have not been systematically reported. In this work, we investigate the sensitivity of HBCD bundle metrics to scanner model-related variance and address these variations with ComBat-GAM harmonization within the current HBCD data release 1.1 across six scanner models. Following ComBat-GAM, we observe zero statistically significant differences between the distributions from any scanner model following FDR correction and reduce Cohen's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$f$</tex> effect sizes across all metrics. Our work underscores the importance of rigorous harmonization efforts in large-scale studies, and we encourage future investigations of HBCD data to control for these effects.

title: Deformation Detection and Self-Calibration Method for Solid Rocket Motor CT Slices
authors: Bin Liu, Yiping Liu, Liangliang Li, Chuchao He, Buqing Pang, Hongbin An
publish: 2026 International Conference on Vision, Advanced Imaging and Computer Technology (VAICT)
abstract: bstract-As quality control for solid rocket motors (SRMs) escalates, high-precision detection of throat ablation using industrial computed tomography (CT) is pivotal for mission safety. However, varying imaging conditions and high-vibration environments cause grayscale fluctuations and mechanical displacements, rendering traditional single-modality methods ineffective. To address these bottlenecks, this paper proposes a dual-state joint constrained self-calibration framework. It integrates adaptive grayscale compensation and a stepped-circle mean gradient technique into static edge extraction to resolve parameter mismatches. Using the Enhanced Correlation Coefficient (ECC) algorithm and the Farneback dense optical flow model, the framework accurately decouples rigid displacements from non-rigid material degradation. Experiments demonstrate a sub- 0.01 mm resolution and restrict the relative error to below 0.621% of the throat dimension. The proposed method provides a highly robust and precise solution for dynamic throat ablation monitoring, showcasing significant potential for intelligent defect inspection in aerospace engineering.

title: AI Augmented Mental Health Journal with Sentiment Analysis and Mood Visualization Using NLP and LLM
authors: Irin Mariya Joby, Ansh Mishra, Manoj Kumar Shukla
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Stress, anxiety, and emotional instability are becoming more common today, but getting professional psychological help is still hard because of cost, stigma, and lack of availability. Recent progress in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Large Language Models (LLMs) makes it possible to build smart systems that help people think about their emotions and improve their health. This paper introduces MoodMate AI, a web application for journaling and emotional analysis that uses AI to combine sentiment analysis, fine-grained emotion classification, semantic embeddings, and LLM-driven empathetic response generation. The system employs a hybrid pipeline combining rule-based sentiment analysis (VADER), transformer-based emotion classification, Sentence-BERT (SBERT) embeddings, and a generative LLM for personalized feedback. A scalable web architecture enables real-time interaction, interpretability, and modular extensibility. Experimental evaluation demonstrates effective sentiment detection, promising emotion recognition performance, and low-latency inference, validating the feasibility of deploying emotionally intelligent AI systems for real-world well-being applications.

title: Adaptive Hierarchical Reinforcement and Meta-Learning for Dynamic Load Balancing in 5G-Integrated Smart Grids: A Study Using Real-Time Load Monitoring Data
authors: Vibhor Pal
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The increasing sophistication of contemporary smart grids, compounded by the inclusion of renewable energy sources, and 5G supporting communication networks, requires smart and dynamic load management system. This paper uses the Smart Grid Real-Time Load Monitoring Dataset to create a reinforcement learning (RL)-based model of efficient energy distribution. This methodology utilizes Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) using multi-agent coordination and meta-learning and is compared with Flat RL and Traditional Load Balancing Heuristic methods. The experimental findings prove that HRL has the best performance, with the mean reward of 346.98, the smallest load variation (0.000529), and a maximum electricity cost reduction of 15%, and is stable despite the changing 5G latency and variable renewable generation. HRL displays better adaptability, better load balancing, better utilization of renewable energy and better real-time response compared to Flat RL and heuristic approaches. A scalable, resilient, and practically applicable approach for next-generation energy systems is provided by the research, which emphasizes the need to merge hierarchical control with meta-learning in smart grid operations. These results provide a way forward for smart grid management that is intelligent, dependable, and sustainable, which is essential for making electrical networks more resilient and efficient.

title: Real-Time Adaptive Fault Detection Using Entropy-Based Online Feature Selection and Hybrid Learning
authors: Nirmalya Mallick, Chandan Kumar Shiva, Vedik Basetti, Sachidananda Sen, V. Mukherjee, Shriram S. Rangarajan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: Power system networks require fast and accurate fault detection to maintain stability and prevent damage. An online feature selection is designed, followed by a hybrid learning scheme, to create a model of a real-time adaptive fault detector. The algorithm continuously processes a streaming version of the voltage signal, integrated by adaptive wavelet decomposition, to automatically decrease the window length and frequency of the wavelet basis, as entropy values of signal complexity are computed. The extraction of real-time features is conducted based on energy, entropy, and frequency-domain attributes, and online adaptive selection of features is performed to select the most discriminating features in each processing cycle. An adaptive <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{k}$</tex>-nearest-neighbor classifier is also employed, based on a hybrid Mahalanobis-cosine distance measure that enables the model to be updated online using confidence. The overall classification accuracy of the framework was 96.67%, with a 95% confidence interval of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$[94.58 \%, 98.75 \%]$</tex>, and per-class <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex>-scores were between 0.935 and 1.000. Adaptive learning, high fault discrimination, and computational efficiency justify the use of the implemented entropy-driven framework as a reliable and scalable method for detecting faults in real-time in power transmission systems.

title: Thermal Design and Analysis of Phase Change Material Integrated SiC Based Solid State Circuit Breaker
authors: Mohammad Dehan Rahman, Yannal Nawafleh, Cade Marcinkovich, Han Hu, Xiaoqing Song
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: While traditional electromechanical circuit breakers have a track record of being dependable and efficient for protecting circuits, new developments in power distribution technologies demand better interruption performance characteristics (e.g., faster switching speed). Solid-state circuit breakers (SSCBs) have been the subject of increased research and development due to the necessity for faster switching operation. SSCBs are known to provide better protection due to their arc-free current interruptions and ultra-fast fault clearing performance when compared to traditional mechanical breakers. However, maintaining safe device junction temperature under operating conditions, particularly in overload condition, which intensifies thermal strains on power semiconductor devices is one of the design challenges of SiC-based SSCBs. In this paper, The effect of integrating phase-change material (PCM) into the heatsink is also examined. Results show that PCM reduces the transient temperature rise and delays thermal saturation. Metallic PCMs provide stronger thermal buffering than wax. A prototype SSCB is built to validate the simulations. Experimental data confirm the temperature reduction achieved by PCM integration. These results demonstrate that PCM can improve the thermal stability of high-power SSCBs.

title: Real-Time Intrusion Detection System Using Snort and ML for Edge Devices through Raspberry Pi 5
authors: Nuthan Polineni, Ganesh Pachipala, L. Sujihelen
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Nowadays Cybersecurity has become a vital part of the digital world as well as in our day to day life. Edge devices can process incoming data much faster than traditional cloud environments. But they are exposed to an even greater risk of getting attacked since they are more vulnerable to direct attacks than traditional methods and devices. For example, a person's smart home network or a smallscale business's internal network. Hence, we must defend edge devices to ensure the integrity, confidentiality, and availability of network resources. This paper reflects the need for low-cost and effective network protection. We began by proposing a hybrid IDS that expands on Snort a powerful signature-based intrusion detection system with capabilities that include machine learning's anomaly detection. The main aim is to enhance the network infrastructure and provide network security to smart homes and small organizations such as small-scale businesses or startups. The focus is a lightweight IDS that can be deployed in environments such as the given above example. The proposed design uses the Random Forest algorithm with snort and provides high accuracy as an intrusion detection system as compared to the already existing system.

title: Hypernetwork-Based Adaptive Aggregation for Multimodal Multiple-Instance Learning in Predicting Coronary Calcium Debulking
authors: Kaito Shiku, Ichika Seo, Tetsuya Matoba, Rissei Hino, Yasuhiro Nakano, Ryoma Bise
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: In this paper, we present the first attempt to estimate the necessity of debulking coronary artery calcifications from computed tomography (CT) images. We formulate this task as a Multiple-instance Learning (MIL) problem. The difficulty of this task lies in that physicians adjust their focus and decision criteria for device usage according to tabular data representing each patient's condition. To address this issue, we propose a hypernetwork-based adaptive aggregation transformer (HyperAdAgFormer), which adaptively modifies the feature aggregation strategy for each patient based on tabular data through a hypernetwork.
