Course anomalies are identified through heading angle zoning and channel alignment constraints, enabling the detection of violations such as contraflow navigation or excessive deviation from the designated route. Results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively identifies abnormal ship behavior in bridge waters, providing a technical framework to ensure the safety of both ships and bridges.

title: Unified Multi-Modal Smart Parking Ecosystem
authors: Totti Shaik Taufic Aksa, Divya Sri Moparthi, Mitesh Paliwal, Srinivasa Rao Vankdoth
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: The process of finding a parking space in the metropolitan urban centers today are a huge bottleneck that has led to 30% traffic jam. Current smart parking systems are prone to failure because of individual hardware and software weaknesses where sensors themselves cannot detect highclearance vehicles such as SUVs and overall camera systems are incapable of detecting non-standard (double-row) license plates. The current Smart Parking System (SPS) is designed and implemented in this paper in order to solve these engineering problems. We suggest a three-level solution: (1) An IoT Sensor Node, which, based on Ultra-Wideband (UWB) Magnetic signals, operates an automatic model to adjust and to diverse license plate layouts and (2) A Computer Vision pipeline with YOLOv4 and AlexNet, which can predict parking availability with a new remarkably high accuracy (3) A Cloud-based Predictive model (WA-PSO-WNN), which predicts a new parking availability to a considerable degree. Our implementation system consisted of ESP32 microcontrollers, Node.js back-end and a React native user application. This combined strategy has proven to be a major improvement over the traditional systems as our experimental results in a real world testbed show this strategy is very reliable, latent and user-friendly.

title: Hybrid Diffusion Model for Breast Ultrasound Image Augmentation
authors: Farhan Fuad Abir, Sanjeda Sara Jennifer, Niloofar Yousefi, Laura J. Brattain
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: We propose a hybrid diffusion-based augmentation framework to overcome the critical challenge of ultrasound data augmentation in breast ultrasound (BUS) datasets. Unlike conventional diffusion-based augmentations, our approach improves visual fidelity and preserves ultrasound texture by combining text-to-image generation with image-to-image (img2img) refinement, as well as fine-tuning with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) and textual inversion (TI). Our method generated realistic, class-consistent images on an open-source Kaggle breast ultrasound image dataset (BUSI). Compared to the Stable Diffusion v1.5 baseline, incorporating TI and img2img refinement reduced the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) from 45.97 to 33.29, demonstrating a substantial gain in fidelity while maintaining comparable downstream classification performance. Overall, the proposed framework effectively mitigates the low-fidelity limitations of synthetic ultrasound images and enhances the quality of augmentation for robust diagnostic modeling.

title: Compensation-Winding-Current-Based Constant On-Time Control of Trans-Inductor Voltage Regulators (TLVRs)
authors: Shuyu Zhang, Yicheng Zhu, Nathan Biesterfeld, Robert C. N. Pilawa-Podgurski
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a compensation-winding-current (${i_{{L_{\text{c}}}}}$)-based constant on-time (COT) control strategy for trans-inductor voltage regulators (TLVRs) used in data center applications. Compared with conventional current-mode COT control of TLVRs, which requires high-bandwidth current sensing for each phase, the proposed approach achieves equivalent transient performance by sensing only ${i_{{L_{\text{c}}}}}$, thereby significantly reducing the system complexity. The high-pass filter nature of ${i_{{L_{\text{c}}}}}$-based current sensing and the corresponding preservation of the high-frequency current ripple needed for COT control is derived analytically and verified through circuit simulation. The proposed control strategy is experimentally validated on a six-phase, 500 kHz, 12 V-to-1 V, 240 A TLVR prototype, demonstrating robust load regulation under a 6A/ns load step generated by a custom on-board GaN-based load stepper.

title: Evaluating Segmentation Using Betti-1 Topological Metric: Application to Nasal Cavities in the Context of Airflow Simulation
authors: Nathan Hutin, Thomas Grenier, Sébastien Valette, Hamid Ladjal
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations offer an objective means to analyze nasal airflow. However, to be patient-specific, their accuracy relies on precise CT-based volume segmentation of the nasal cavity. Existing segmentation methods typically prioritize volumetric accuracy (e.g., Dice coefficient) while often overlooking topological fidelity, which is critical for generating anatomically consistent CFD meshes. To address this limitation, we propose an automatic nasal cavity segmentation framework based on nnUNet, augmented with a topology-based evaluation metric that quantifies differences in the number of tunnels between predicted and reference segmentations. Evaluation on the NasalSeg public dataset shows a Dice score of 0.947, comparable to state-of-the-art results, while improving the segmentation consistency by removing tunnels.

title: Strict Quadratically-Constrained Parallel Transmit Design with Unrolled Homeomorphic Projection
authors: Junno Yun, Toygan Kilic, Jürgen Herrler, Patrick Liebig, Kâmil Uğurbil, Mehmet Akçakaya
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Parallel transmission (pTx) has been widely adopted to mitigate transmit field (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{B}_{1}^{+}$</tex>) inhomogeneities in ultrahigh field (UHF) magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). pTx design optimizes subject-specific radiofrequency (RF) pulses under strict quadratic constraints for specific absorption rate (SAR) and power. Deep learning-based pTx design has received attention due to its ultrafast processing times compared to conventional optimization methods, but the incorporation of strict quadratic constratints has remained a challenge. In this work, we present an end-to-end deep learning framework for pTx design that explicitly enforces quadratic SAR constraints through an unrolled homeomorphic projection method. The proposed approach guarantees that neural network outputs strictly comply with RF safety limits, while achieving accurate and homogeneous flip angle maps without any postprocessing, substantially reducing computation time.

title: Posture Correction and Shuttle Metrics Analysis for Badminton using Computer Vision
authors: Keshav Chandar, V R Tanavi, B R Varuni, Zacharias S Edakkara, Shikha Tripathi
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Automated analysis of badminton gameplay from raw video remains challenging due to rapid shuttle motion, occlusions, and the need to jointly model biomechanics and tactical context. This paper presents an end-to-end computer vision framework for posture correction and shuttle metrics analysis using monocular match footage. Using court homography estimation and multi-person pose tracking combined with physics-aware shuttlecock tracking through Kalman filtering techniques, the proposed system introduces an automated Impact Score algorithm, a heuristic metric derived from the product of normalized shuttlecock acceleration and angular inflection to localize hit events without manual annotation. The transformerbased multimodal classifier uses biomechanical/spatial data and shuttlecock motion information to classify shots. A confidencegated tactical model uses the entire rally context to strengthen the confidence level for shot classification. The system provides personalized form correction by comparing user kinematics against professional biomechanical benchmarks, delivering actionable feedback (e.g., “Add Elbow Extension”) when deviations in joint angles are detected.Most existing techniques treat hit detection, shot classification, biomechanical assessment and strategic reasoning individually, frequently using manually annotated data or external sensors as input. An automated, fully integrated approach that automatically analyses all four components with raw footage in real- time is still a challenge. Validated on a diverse dataset of 8,232 stroke samples across 9 classes, combining professional match footage with amateur recordings to ensure cross-domain robustness, the proposed system was validated with accurate hit detection, stable trajectory reconstructions, and dependable shot classifications regardless of recording conditions, confirming its potential use in automated coaching for badminton using video data.

title: Real-Time Driver Stress Monitoring Using Multisignal Fusion and Artificial Intelligence
authors: Sai Harish Yenduri, Praharsha Gurram
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: Real-Time Driver Stress Monitoring Using MultiSignal Fusion and Artificial Intelligence is a suggestion that advances a technically sound method of continuously measuring driver stress with the help of improved sensor fusion and machine learning inference. The suggested system has been developed to combine electrocardiogram (ECG), galvanic skin response (GSR), and continuous facial thermal imaging into a single feature extraction pipeline, unlike the current body of research, which depends on single-modal physiological indicators. The adaptive noise filter, wavelet, artifact and temporal normalization of conditions are executing in real-time through preprocessing modules. Deep convolutional attention network (DCAN) is a neural network that is involved in multimodal feature fusion that encodes correlations between cross-signals and temporal dynamics. The classification of stress is done in a hybrid architecture which involves the utilization of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks alongside a support vector regression (SVR) layer to measure the stress levels in real-time. The device runs on the edge AI platform, which allows the system to realize less inference latency. The evaluation of the performance in custom drive condition cases proves that the framework can identify the onset of stress with low false-positive rates and high accuracy. This publication adds a technical and scalable approach to real-time cognitive state estimation to intelligent vehicles.

title: Leveraging Fourier Domain for Unsupervised Removal of Structured Noise in Fluorescence Microscopy Imaging
authors: Haosen Liu, Edmund Y. Lam
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Due to the practical limitations such as phototoxicity, fluorescence microscopy images are generally with faint signals corrupted by severe and complex noise, necessitating the denoising step to improve image quality for more accurate biological analysis. Recently, unsupervised deep learning techniques have attracted widespread attentions owing to their powerful capability of removing noise without the reliance of collecting clean images. Although existing unsupervised denoising methods such as blind spot networks are effective in suppressing spatially independent noise, developing unsupervised strategies for structured noise is still underexplored. Based on the observation that noise correlated in the spatial domain can exhibit independence in the Fourier domain, this work develops a Fourier-based blind spot strategy to enable effective unsupervised structured noise removal for multi-frame fluorescence microscopy imaging. The evaluation on publicly available real-world microscopy datasets validates its effectiveness in removing structured noise.

title: A 1.5MHz Time-Based Boost Converter with Feedback-PID for AMOLED Displays with Delay-Line-Based Line Feedforward
authors: Paolo Melillo, Mauro Leoncini, Gabriele Magni, Alessandro Bertolini, Alessandro Gasparini, Massimo Ghioni
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: In recent years, time-based compensators have been investigated as a promising alternative to conventional analog control schemes for high-frequency DC-DC converters. As the voltage-mode time-based implementation lacks a PWM ramp generator, implementing a line feedforward is not straightforward. To solve this problem, a different type of compensator, referred to as feedback-PID (F-PID), has been used in this work. This compensator is composed of a proportional-integral forward path and a band-pass-filter feedback path, which can be easily modified to include the feedforward injection from the input voltage. In this paper, the proposed F-PID compensator is presented, and its implementation in a time-based compensator with delay-line-based line-feedforward is discussed. To verify the analysis, a prototype boost converter for AMOLED displays with a 1.5MHz switching frequency is realized in a 180nm BCD process. Measurement results show a 40mV peak-to-peak variation when an input voltage variation of 1V in 50µs is applied, and a duty-cycle RMS jitter of 1.5ns. The proposed controller occupies only 0.23mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: Measuring and Aligning Abstraction in Vision-Language Models with Medical Taxonomies
authors: Ben Schaper, Maxime Di Folco, Bernhard Kainz, Julia A. Schnabel, Cosmin I. Bercea
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) show strong zero-shot performance for chest X-ray classification, but standard flat metrics fail to distinguish between clinically minor and severe errors. This work investigates how to quantify and mitigate abstraction errors by leveraging medical taxonomies. We benchmark several state-of-the-art VLMs using hierarchical metrics and introduce Catastrophic Abstraction Errors to capture cross-branch mistakes. Our results reveal substantial misalignment of VLMs with clinical taxonomies despite high flat performance. To address this, we propose risk-constrained thresholding and taxonomy-aware fine-tuning with radial embeddings, which reduce severe abstraction errors to below 2% while maintaining competitive performance. These findings highlight the importance of hierarchical evaluation and representation-level alignment for safer and more clinically meaningful deployment of VLMs.

title: Ai-Driven Spatial Analysis of Breast Cancer Images for Recurrence Stratification
authors: J. Martínez-Aedo Castro, A. D. Ramos-Guerra, J. E. Ortuño Fisac, I. Carretero-Barrios, C. Ariño Palao, J. Palacios, B. Pérez Mies, M.J. Ledesma-Carbayo
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Breast cancer has the highest incidence among women, making recurrence risk estimation crucial for guiding treatment in early-stage luminal disease. Advances in digital pathology allow quantitative analysis of the tumor microenvironment from routine H&E whole-slide images (WSIs), enabling detailed assessment of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs), a key prognostic factor. This study evaluated topological and spatial descriptors of TILs and their correlation with recurrence risk. 20 features reflecting immune abundance, morphology, proximity to tumor and stroma, and higher-order topological organization were extracted from nuclear segmentation and cell classification, and Machine Learning models were trained in 209 patients and tested in an independent cohort of 53 patients. Results showed several features significantly associated with MammaPrint® risk, with the mean distance between TILs and the nearest tumor cell showing the strongest correlation (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{p}=9.97 \times 10^{-8}$</tex>). Logistic Regression achieved an AUC of 0.765. These findings support that this innovative pipeline based on TILs topology may aid pathologists in genomic risk profiling.

title: TRIDENT: A Secure and Robust Framework for Cloud Data Geolocation Under Byzantine Landmark Attacks
authors: Haider Ali, Megha Gupta, Mohd Izhar, Pinki Nayak
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Cloud customers and auditors often need independent evidence that a cloud endpoint is hosted in a claimed geographic region for residency, sovereignty, or contractual compliance. Landmark-based geolocation offers an external measurement signal, but in adversarial settings it can fail when a subset of landmarks are Byzantine-fabricating delays, biasing probes, selectively responding, or colluding to steer inference. This paper presents TRIDENT, a secure and robust framework for cloud data geolocation under Byzantine landmark attacks. TRIDENT combines (i) landmark vetting and diversity constraints to reduce correlated influence, (ii) signed measurement transcripts to enable auditability and replay, and (iii) a Byzantine-resilient inference rule based on a trimmed-residual score that discards up to f worst-case outliers. We formalize the system and threat model with up to f corrupted landmarks among n selected vantage points, and we provide both deterministic and high-probability correctness conditions under explicit regionseparation assumptions and sub-Gaussian noise. TRIDENT also derives a practical confidence signal from the score gap between the best and runner-up regions. Representative results show mean error of 127 km at f = 0 and $150 / 189 \mathrm{~km}$ at $f=0.2 / 0.3$, compared to $141 / 267 / 412 \mathrm{~km}$ for a non-robust baseline.

title: Structure Preserving Adversarial Diffusion for Unpaired Medical Image Synthesis
authors: K. E Giraldo Paniagua, P.-H. Conze, V. Jaouen, E. Angelini
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Recent works have established diffusion models as viable candidates for unpaired medical image translation, by incorporating supervision through non-diffusive models in an end-to-end fashion.. Existing models typically rely on cycleconsistent, two-sided, non-diffusive modules of relatively high computational cost where structure preservation is not explicit. In this work, we propose SPAD, a novel one-sided approach that explicitly preserves the source domain firstorder (gradient) orientations, while enabling flexible contrast adaptation between source and target domains. Our approach provides direct edge guidance for the diffusive module, leading to better structural preservation. Experiments performed on image synthesis tasks using BraTS, CERMEP, and Gold Atlas datasets demonstrate that our model improved translation fidelity and reduced training time by over 50% compared to SynDiff while keeping the same diffusion architecture. The reduction in computational cost allowed us to train 3D variants, which achieved consistently higher quantitative scores. Code will be publicly shared on GitHub upon acceptance.

title: MTSL-SQL: Multi-Turn Schema Linking for Context-dependent Text-to-SQL Paring with Open-Source LLMs
authors: Taipeng Zhu, Ye Liu, Chenghui Liu, Fangfang Zhou, Yingwei Liang
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Recently, context-dependent text-to-SQL parsing has garnered significant attention, which plays a crucial role in translating natural language into SQL within an interactive process. Existing LLM-based methods leverage context information either from interaction history or from previously predicted SQL queries, but they struggle to utilize both effectively due to mismatches between natural language and database schemas. In this paper, we propose a context-dependent text-to-SQL framework with multi-turn schema linking method, called MTSL-SQL. Specifically, MTSL-SQL integrates historical conversations and current dialogue information for schema linking, and subsequently fine-tunes open-source large language models to use selected schema and dialogue information to generate SQL queries. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among LLM-based approaches on the SParC and CoSQL datasets.

title: Dictionary-Free MR Fingerprinting via Implicit Neural Representation
authors: Chaoguang Gong, Yue Hu, Lixian Zou, Peng Li, Zhilang Qiu, Shuo Zhou, Xingyang Wu, Zhanqi Hu, Xiaoyan Wang, Haifeng Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Magnetic Resonance Fingerprinting (MRF) enables rapid multi-parametric quantitative MRI but traditionally relies on discrete precomputed dictionaries, leading to quantization errors and limited flexibility. In this work, a Dictionary-Free Gradient-based Implicit Neural Representation (DFG-INR) framework is presented to learn direct mappings from spatial coordinates to tissue parameters (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1}, T_{2}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P D$</tex>) without any precomputed dictionary or labeled data. By integrating an INR with a fully differentiable Bloch simulator, DFG-INR enables end-to-end, physics-consistent optimization directly from acquired <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex>-space data. The proposed method is validated using BrainWeb simulations, 3T high-field physical phantom experiments, and 5T ultra-high-field in vivo human brain data. Experimental results demonstrate that DFG-INR achieves superior quantitative accuracy, better anatomical detail preservation, and stronger noise robustness than state-of-the-art model-based MRF methods.

title: Beyond the Monitor: Mixed Reality Visualization and Multimodal AI for Enhanced Digital Pathology Workflow
authors: Jai Prakash Veerla, Partha Sai Guttikonda, Helen H. Shang, Mohammad S. Nasr, Cesar Torres, Jacob M. Luber
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Pathologists diagnose cancer using gigapixel whole-slide images (WSIs), but the current digital workflow is fragmented. These multiscale datasets often exceed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100,000 \times 100,000$</tex> pixels, yet standard 2D monitors restrict the field of view. This disparity forces constant panning and zooming, which increases cognitive load and disrupts diagnostic momentum. We introduce PathVis, a mixed-reality platform for Apple Vision Pro that unifies this ecosystem into a single immersive environment. PathVis replaces indirect mouse navigation with embodied interaction, utilizing eye gaze, natural hand gestures, and voice commands to explore gigapixel data. The system integrates multimodal AI agents to support computer-aided diagnosis: a content-based image retrieval engine spatially displays similar patient cases for side-by-side prognostic comparison, while a conversational assistant provides real-time interpretation. By merging immersive visualization with integrated AI capabilities, PathVis shows promise in streamlining diagnostic workflows and mitigating the burden of context switching. This paper presents the system architecture and a preliminary qualitative evaluation demonstrating the platform's feasibility. The PathVis source code and a demo video are publicly available at: https://github.com/jaiprakash1824/Path_Vis.

title: Real-time Simulation Platform for a Solid-State Transformer with Multilevel Cascaded H-Bridge Dual-Active Bridge Back-to-Back Converters
authors: Zerui Dong, Aditya Ashok, Juan Paez-Alvarez, Satish Kumar Ancha
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: With the vast number of distributed energy resources integrated into the medium voltage distribution system, there is a strong need for flexible grid services to strengthen the grid's stability and reliability. The solid-state transformer (SST) can serve as an energy hub that provides grid services in the distribution network. The three-stage SST has emerged as the most popular SST topology because of its flexibility, controllability, full isolation, and bidirectional power flow. Real-time simulation of an SST is a key step to validate its design and control algorithm before moving to hardware development. However, simulating an SST in real-time is very challenging due to its complex topology with a large number of power electronic switches involved. To address this challenge, a high-fidelity CPU-FPGA-based real-time simulation platform has been developed. Particularly, an FPGA-based model was developed with flexibility and scalability to simulate different SST topologies. To validate the development of the real-time simulation platform, an SST model was developed and tested. This design had a modular, multilevel, cascaded H-bridges and dual-active bridges in a back-to-back topology along with a hierarchical control algorithm. Simulation results show the stable operation of this SST topology for specific setpoint changes, thereby vetting the real-time simulation platform presented.

title: New High Temperature Metallized Film Capacitors Utilizing Low Dissipation Factor Nanolayered Film
authors: Erik Steinmetz, Bryce Lanese, John Tomko, Mike Ponting, Mason A. Wolak
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Current and future capacitors require high performance in demanding applications such as high temperature SiC-based power electronics. New nanolayered capacitor films can be formulated to yield tailored properties resulting in enhanced performance, including but not limited to increased application temperature ranges, energy storage capabilities, reduced volume requirements, etc. Peak Nano’s new nanolayered Nanoplex LDF capacitor film is designed for higher temperature stability (up to 135 Celsius) while maintaining low dissipation factor comparable to/lower than biaxially oriented polypropylene (BOPP). LDF film is manufactured to scale, metallized via standard practices, and fabricated into capacitors with the same manufacturability as industry standard BOPP. This work presents new developments and results from capacitors fabricated with LDF film. The capacitance, dielectric loss, and ESR changes with temperature are lower than 5 percent change up to 145 Celsius. The dielectric breakdown strength of capacitors made of LDF film was found to maintain room temperature properties at 135 Celsius, a significant improvement over BOPP. The leakage current of LDF film-based capacitors is much lower and constant over this temperature range while BOPP increases significantly. The combined high temperature properties of LDF based capacitors make them ideal for use in high temperature environments

title: View-Guided Multi-Task Learning for Fetal Cardiac Segmentation and CHD Classification
authors: Yingyu Yang, Qianye Yang, Can Peng, Yuyuan Liu, J. Alison Noble
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Congenital heart disease (CHD) is a major cause of infant morbidity and mortality, and fetal ultrasound is the standard modality for prenatal screening. The FETUS Challenge (ISBI 2026) focuses on the joint tasks of fetal cardiac structure segmentation and CHD-related multi-label classification across standard cardiac views under a semi-supervised learning (SSL) setting. We present a view-guided framework with separate models for segmentation and classification, both augmented with an auxiliary standard-view prediction head. To further exploit anatomical information, we introduce a view-specific geometric classifier that derives explicit geometric features from segmentation masks to capture cardiac structural relationships. On the validation leaderboard, our method (team CHDkiller) achieved an overall score of 52.54 (F1 44.54, DSC 81.34, NSD 63.08), compared with the baseline score of 40.37 (F1 34.20, DSC 65.48, NSD 45.55). On the hidden test leaderboard, CHDkiller obtained an overall score of 47.62 (F1 25.58, DSC 69.85, NSD 46.18), outperforming the baseline score of 36.05 (F1 17.48, DSC 54.11, NSD 33.97), with an inference time of 55.45 ms. Our team ranked 5th on the validation leaderboard and 4th on the hidden test leaderboard. Code available at https://github.com/YingyuYyy/FetalViewGuidedSegCLS.

title: A Calibrated Lightweight Spiking Neural Network Framework for EEG Seizure Detection
authors: Shreya Yogesh, Shagun Gupta
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Epilepsy affects millions of individuals around the world and is marked by seizures that require precise identification as quickly as possible through the use of electroencephalogram (EEG) signals. Conventional deep learning approaches for EEG signal classification are often computationally intensive, which limits real-time processing and edge deployment. This work proposes an independent calibrated Spiking Neural Network (SNN) approach for automated seizure detection using multichannel EEG signals. To train and evaluate the model, we utilized the scalp EEG CHB-MIT dataset under a Leave-One-Patient-Out (LOPO) cross-validation protocol to ensure patient-independent generalization. Extracted EEG features are converted into spike representations and processed using fully connected layers with Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neurons. Classification is performed by counting the output spikes across the final layer. The proposed work obtains a sensitivity of 97.03%, with a false positive rate of $0.66 \mathrm{FPR} / \mathrm{h}$, illustrating efficient seizure identification while preserving low computational complexity and suitability for low-power neurological monitoring devices.

title: Analysis and Experimental Validation of Inverter Switching Frequency Impact in High-Speed Air-Cooled Motor-Inverter Systems
authors: Kazuma Tomida, Tatsuro Yamada, Naoki Omura, Koki Notake, Takahiko Murayama, Koji Yamaguchi
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This study prototyped and experimentally evaluated a high-speed air-cooled integrated motor-inverter system (6kW power), focusing on the impact of inverter switching frequency on losses and thermal performance. Increasing the switching frequency from 30 kHz to 60 kHz increases the inverter loss, but more than that, it decreases the electromagnetic loss of the motor, resulting in an approximately 20% reduction in the combined inverter and electromagnetic losses, and a notable reduction in rotor temperature was observed, improving the motor’s thermal margin. These findings demonstrate that optimizing the inverter switching frequency effectively enhances both efficiency and thermal stability in high-speed air-cooled motor-inverter systems.

title: SiC Device Based Four Phase Interleaved Bidirectional DC-DC Converter Control with Wide Voltage Range Operation for EV Drive Validation
authors: Murali Manohar S R, Arun Raj Kumar K P, Sreedevi M L, Jinuraj K G, Renji V Chacko
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: The need for advanced test platforms capable of evaluating complete EV systems, drivetrain components, and electric vehicle supply equipment (EVSE) is growing as a result of the rapid developments in electric vehicle (EV) and EV subsystem. Traditional testing methods involving physical energy storage systems are often expensive and time-consuming. This paper presents the design, control, hardware implementation, and testing of a wide voltage range, four-phase interleaved bidirectional DC-DC converter based on silicon carbide (SiC) devices for use in a battery emulator platform. The emulator system, comprising an Active Front End (AFE), Dual Active Bridge, and Bidirectional DC-DC Converter (BDC), enables programmable power delivery for EV subsystem testing. The BDC multi-phase interleaved topology offers benefits including high power density, reduced current ripple, improved thermal distribution and scalability. Integration of SiC devices enhances switching performance, efficiency, and thermal capability, supporting high dynamic load conditions encountered in EV operations. The converter supports bidirectional power flow for both buck (discharge) and boost (charge) modes, enabling realistic simulation of EV scenarios of acceleration, cruising and regenerative braking. This system facilitates cost-effective, high-fidelity testing and validation of EV subsystems under real-world driving profiles. For the bidirectional converter, three nominal voltage levels - 48 V, 72 V, 360 V - are chosen as output voltage requirements in accordance with battery voltage levels of two-, three- and four-wheeler electric vehicles respectively. This converter is designed for a power capability of 30kW. Synchronous buck and synchronous boost mode operations are tested with power level of 17 kW and current capacity up to 100A.

title: Deep Learning for Remaining Useful Life Estimation of Turbofan Aircraft Engines: A Comparative Study
authors: Aicha El Horre, Abdelali El Gourari, Abdelati Reha
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: Within the civil aviation sector, aircraft engine maintenance accounts for a major portion of operational expenditures. Unanticipated component failures can severely compromise both economic efficiency and flight safety. Traditional maintenance strategies often rely on fixed intervals rather than the actual health status of the engine. To address this, the present study investigates a data-driven Deep Learning framework leveraging the NASA C-MAPSS dataset to estimate the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of turbofan engines. Unlike prior studies that often focus on single-condition datasets or compare only against static baselines, this work presents a progressive three-model comparison: a static Random Forest (RF) regressor, a standalone Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, and a Hybrid CNN-LSTM architecture. This ablation-style evaluation is conducted across all four C-MAPSS sub-datasets (FD001–FD004), encompassing both single and multi-operating-condition scenarios. Results demonstrate that sequential models consistently outperform static approaches, and that the CNN-LSTM hybrid provides critical robustness under complex multi-condition settings where the standalone LSTM collapses. Specifically, the CNN-LSTM achieves an RMSE of 15.76 cycles on FD001 and maintains competitive performance across all subsets, while the LSTM degrades to an RMSE of 54.20 on FD002. Prediction error distributions and the NASA asymmetric scoring function are reported to characterise the margin of error of each model. These results highlight the potential of hybrid sequential deep learning to enhance system reliability and optimise maintenance scheduling within the context of the growing North African aerospace MRO sector.

title: Frequency-Aware Spatiotemporal Modeling With Cross-Frequency Attention for Echocardiography Left Ventricle Segmentation
authors: Xiaodi Li, Hongxu Li, Sining Hu, Shuangtong Shao, Chaoguang Gong, Zhaolin Chen, Yue Hu
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate segmentation of the left ventricle (LV) in echocardiography videos is essential for cardiac function assessment but remains challenging due to complex motion patterns, temporal dependencies, and noisy artifacts. In this work, we propose a novel frequency-aware spatiotemporal modeling network that captures both video-level and frame-level frequency characteristics for robust LV segmentation. At the video level, the extracted spatiotemporal feature tensor is decomposed into low-frequency components that capture periodic cardiac dynamics and high-frequency components that retain fine structural details. A cross-frequency attention mechanism enables high-frequency queries and values to interact with low-frequency keys, enhancing boundary learning under the guidance of global motion consistency. At the frame level, we utilize joint spatial-frequency feature extraction in the encoder and adaptively fuse high- and low-frequency components through skip connections in the decoder, enhancing segmentation precision and structural fidelity for each frame. Experiments on the CAMUS dataset demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, effectively leveraging both temporal coherence and frame-level detail for accurate LV segmentation.

title: Multi-Modal Emotion and Personality Analysis for AI-Enabled Recruitment Systems
authors: Hemanta Ghosh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: The increasing adoption of artificial intelligence in recruitment has led to significant efficiency gains; however, most existing systems rely primarily on resume-based screening and overlook critical behavioral and psychological attributes of candidates. This paper proposes a multi-modal emotion and personality analysis framework for AI-enabled recruitment systems, designed to approximate human-like assessment during video-based interviews. The proposed approach integrates visual, acoustic, and textual modalities to capture facial expressions, speech characteristics, and linguistic patterns exhibited by candidates. Deep learning models are employed for modality-specific feature extraction, followed by an intermediate fusion strategy to learn a unified representation. Emotion recognition is formulated as a classification task, while personality assessment is modeled as regression over the Big Five personality traits. Experimental evaluations conducted on benchmark multi-modal interview datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms unimodal and late-fusion baselines in both emotion recognition accuracy and personality prediction reliability. The results indicate that multi-modal intelligence can significantly enhance the robustness, fairness, and interpretability of automated recruitment systems, while emphasizing considerations for responsible AI deployment in recruitment contexts.

title: Hierarchical Dirichlet Modeling of Human and Machine Olfaction for Comparing Odor Recognition Using Monte-Carlo Sensor Simulation
authors: Vincent Gnanaraj T, Jaswanth S, Jayaganesh V, Manikandan S
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Human olfactory perception is highly variable, shaped by cognitive bias, memory association, and subjective interpretation, whereas machine olfaction provides stable chemical sensing through gas-sensor arrays and patternrecognition models. An Odor Confusion Matrix framework is introduced to formally evaluate differences in odorclassification behavior between human subjects and an electronic-nose system. Monte-Carlo sensor simulation, Random Forest classification and a hierarchical Dirichlet Bayesian model are used to estimate confusion probabilities, annotator reliability and posterior accuracy distributions. The machine-olfaction system attains a posterior mean accuracy of 95.6%, while human participants achieve an average of 87.8%. Bayesian comparison indicates a posterior probability of 1.000 that machine olfaction surpasses mean human accuracy, reflecting decisive evidence of superior performance. Confusion-difference heatmaps further reveal consistently lower misclassification across odor classes for the electronicnose system. These outcomes highlight machine olfaction as a reliable solution for real-time applications such as food spoilage detection, environmental monitoring, quality inspection and automated diagnostic systems.

title: Understanding Annotation Error Propagation and Learning an Adaptive Policy for Expert Intervention in Barrett's Video Segmentation
authors: Lokesha Rasanjalee, Jin Lin Tan, Dileepa Pitawela, Rajvinder Singh, Hsiang-Ting Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate annotation of endoscopic videos is essential yet time-consuming, particularly for challenging datasets such as dysplasia in Barrett's esophagus, where the affected regions are irregular and lack clear boundaries. Semi-automatic tools like Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) can ease this process by propagating annotations across frames, but small errors often accumulate and reduce accuracy, requiring expert review and correction. To address this, we systematically study how annotation errors propagate across different prompt types, namely masks, boxes, and points, and propose Learning-to-Re-Prompt (L2RP), a cost-aware framework that learns when and where to seek expert input. By tuning a human-cost parameter, our method balances annotation effort and segmentation accuracy. Experiments on a private Barrett's dysplasia dataset and the public SUN-SEG benchmark demonstrate improved temporal consistency and superior performance over baseline strategies.

title: 3D-LLDM: Label-Guided 3D Latent Diffusion Model for Improving High-Resolution Synthetic MR Imaging in Hepatic Structure Segmentation
authors: Kyeonghun Kim, Jaehyeok Bae, Youngung Han, Joo Young Bae, Seoyoung Ju, Junsu Lim, Gyeongmin Kim, Nam-Joon Kim, Woo Kyoung Jeong, Ken Ying-Kai Liao, Won Jae Lee, Pa Hong, Hyuk-Jae Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Deep learning and generative models are advancing rapidly, with synthetic data increasingly being integrated into training pipelines for downstream analysis tasks. However, in medical imaging, their adoption remains constrained by the scarcity of reliable annotated datasets. To address this limitation, we propose 3D-LLDM, a labelguided 3D latent diffusion model that generates high-quality synthetic magnetic resonance (MR) volumes with corresponding anatomical segmentation masks. Our approach utilizes hepatobiliary phase MR images enhanced with Gd-EOB-DTPA contrast agent to derive structural masks for liver, portal vein, hepatic vein, and hepatocellular carcinoma, which subsequently guide volumetric synthesis through a ControlNet-based architecture. Trained on 720 real clinical hepatobiliary phase MR scans from Samsung Medical Center, 3D-LLDM achieves Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) of 28.31 and a 70.9% improvement over GANs and 26.7% over state-of-the-art diffusion baselines. When used for data augmentation, our synthetic volumes boost hepatocellular carcinoma segmentation by up to 11.153% Dice score across five CNN architectures.

title: A Novel Frequency Control Approach for Wide Power Regulation and Full-Range ZVS in Wireless Battery Charging Systems
authors: Gangwei Zhu, Jiayang Wu, Siew Chong Tan, Shu Yuen Ron Hui
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Frequency control is widely adopted in wireless battery charging (WBC) systems due to its inherent hardware simplicity and straightforward implementation. Nevertheless, conventional frequency control strategies are fundamentally constrained by the frequency splitting (FS) phenomenon, which significantly limits power regulation and efficiency under varying load conditions. In this paper, a novel frequency control approach is proposed to overcome the FS challenge. The underlying FS mechanism is first analyzed, and an analytical expression for the threshold load resistance that triggers FS is derived. Building on this insight, a hybrid control scheme is developed that intelligently combines constant-frequency and variable-frequency operation, enabling seamless transitions across different operating conditions. The proposed method effectively mitigates FS-related issues without imposing restrictive constraints on load conditions or coil coupling, while simultaneously achieving wide-range power regulation and full-range zero-voltage switching (ZVS) over all operating points. Experimental results demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed approach, confirming its practicality and potential for high-efficiency, robust WBC system implementation.

title: Comparison Feature Optimized Machine Learning to Improve Heart Disease Prediction
authors: Mahaveerakannan R, Sudhakar K, N. S Ravi Kumar, V. Meenakshi, R. Dhanalakshmi
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Cardiovascular disease is one of the major causes of deaths across the globe and proper early prediction systems are required. In the paper, a comparative analysis is given on five machine learning algorithms used to predict heart disease, based on the Stat log heart dataset which contains 270 records of patients with with 13 clinical attributes. Our pipeline architecture to perform a systematic preprocessing pipeline of Kalman filtering noise reduction, features selection using variance-based features selection and statistical imputation of missing values. The accuracy, precision, recall and F1score are used to evaluate five classifiers; Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Support Vector machine (SVM), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN). The results of the experiment show that the Decision Tree classifier has the best accuracy and precision of 98.51 and 98.12 respectively, whereas Logistic Regression is the best in the F1score of 0.99. Recall is 0.98 to 0.99 in all the models, which implies a strong sensitivity of heart disease detection. As a basis of automated diagnostic support systems, the comparative analysis proves Decision Trees to be the best classifier to use with this clinical application.

title: Resmscam-FFT Net: Residual Multi-Scale Channel Attention Module with Fast Fourier Transform Network for Cardiac Segmentation
authors: Wenqing Jiang, Yoichi Kato, Hiroyasu Iwata
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases remain the leading cause of global mortality, underscoring the critical need for accurate and timely diagnosis. Echocardiography has been established as the primary non-invasive diagnostic tool, but its reliance on operator expertise leads to significant variability and limits access to timely care. To address these challenges, we introduce ResMSCAM-FFT Net, a deep learning model for precise semantic segmentations and measurements of cardiac chambers in 2D echocardiogram videos. Rooted in the U-Net structure, ResMSCAM-FFT Net incorporates Pyramid Vision Transformer v2 (PVT v2) encoder for efficient multi-scale feature extraction, Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) Block to enforce global topological consistency and mitigate ultrasound artifacts, and Residual Multi-Scale Convolutional Attention Module (ResMSCAM) for content-aware local boundary refinement. Compared with other deep learning-based models, our proposed network achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating robust and highly accurate segmentation masks for automated cardiac function assessment.

title: Ranking-Guided Semi-Supervised Domain Adaptation for Severity Classification
authors: Shota Harada, Ryoma Bise, Kiyohito Tanaka, Seiichi Uchida
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Semi-supervised domain adaptation leverages a few labeled and many unlabeled target samples, making it promising for addressing domain shifts in medical image analysis. However, existing methods struggle with severity classification due to unclear class boundaries. Severity classification involves naturally ordered class labels, complicating adaptation. We propose a novel method that aligns source and target domains using rank scores learned via ranking with class order. Specifically, Cross-Domain Ranking ranks sample pairs across domains, while Continuous Distribution Alignment aligns rank score distributions. Experiments on ulcerative colitis and diabetic retinopathy classification validate the effectiveness of our approach, demonstrating successful alignment of class-specific rank score distributions.

title: Body-Diode Reliability Characterization of Silicon Carbide MOSFETs Toward a Degradation-Insensitive Online Junction Temperature Monitoring
authors: Purushottam Khadka, Zheyu Zhang, Christopher S. Edrington
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Accurately measuring the junction temperature of Silicon Carbide (SiC) power devices during online operation remains a significant challenge. Temperature-Sensitive Electrical Parameters (TSEPs) offer a fast and non-intrusive method for temperature monitoring, but their accuracy can degrade over time due to shifts in the electrothermal calibration curve caused by device aging. To address this limitation, this paper begins with a comprehensive review of reported TSEPs to identify a degradation-insensitive candidate suitable for long-term monitoring. Among the options, the body diode’s forward voltage at low current shows strong potential, as it is relatively insensitive to gate oxide and package degradation. However, the long-term reliability of the SiC body diode itself remains a concern, particularly due to inconsistent findings in literature related to bipolar degradation mechanisms. To investigate these concerns, a dedicated test platform and structured workflow are developed to conduct accelerated stress testing and convert the results into equivalent lifetime metrics under realistic PWM converter operation. This methodology is demonstrated through a case study using Wolfspeed Gen 3 SiC MOSFETs, with experimental results providing insight into the body diode’s reliability under realistic converter conditions along with the qualification of identified degradation-free TSEP.

title: A Point-to-Point Time-Optimal Dynamic Voltage Scaling for High-Performance Processor VRMs
authors: Yijie Qian, Kaifu Pan, Ju Zhou, Shen Xu, Weifeng Sun
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Dynamic voltage scaling (DVS) is essential for improving energy efficiency in processor power supplies, but in server-class VRMs the multi-millifarad output capacitance severely restricts the achievable slew rate. Conventional digital controllers perform DVS by slewing the reference with fixed linear slopes, and to stay within current limits they must use conservative settings that both lengthen the transition and still allow considerable overshoot and undershoot. This paper presents a point-to-point, time-optimal DVS scheme for multiphase buck VRMs supplying high-performance processors. Exploiting the fact that the target voltage is known at the beginning of the transition, a large-signal model is derived that links the output-voltage trajectory to the ON/OFF intervals of all phases. Closed-form expressions are obtained for these intervals in both upward and downward DVS ramps, guaranteeing a soft landing at the target voltage with near-theoretical slew rate and negligible overshoot or undershoot. The same framework is used to estimate the peak inductor current and to derive a simple bound on the maximum single-step DVS amplitude, and larger transitions are automatically divided into multiple time-optimal sub-steps to avoid over-current protection. The scheme is implemented in a 1 MHz eight-phase VRM with 6.648 mF output capacitance. Compared with two state-of-the-art commercial controllers under identical hardware and nominal 30 mV/μs settings, the proposed controller maintains <10 mV fluctuation over 0.6–1.4 V and achieves up to 30 mV/μs effective slew rate, more than doubling the DVS speed.

title: Coordinate-Based Variable-Switching-Frequency PWM Strategy for Parallel Three-Level NPC Converters for Low-Voltage High-Current Applications
authors: Knapoj Chaimanekorn, Ryan Olson, Tianchen Li, Robert Cuzner, Yue Zhao, Feng Guo
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: In this study, a new coordinate-based variable-switching-frequency (VSF) modulation technique is proposed for parallel three-level (3-L) neutral-point-clamped (NPC) converters for low-voltage high-current (LVHC) applications. To achieve a better harmonic profile on the grid side, interleaved PWM is adopted, resulting in a high-frequency circulating current (CC). Hence, to leverage the maximum allowable CCs among parallel-interleaved NPC units, the behavior of the CCs is first predicted based on a sextant reference frame. By doing so, three-phase modulation waves can be determined without zero-sequence voltage (ZSV) injection, and the peak value of three-phase CCs can be determined with less effort and high accuracy. Moreover, the capacitor voltage balance is realized using proprietary pulse patterns and dwell-time regulation. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed strategy effectively spreads the switching frequency component, thus significantly reducing conduction electromagnetic interference (EMI) while controlling the magnitude of CCs.

title: A new system to reduce standby losses in compact GaN-based digital adapter
authors: Angelo Giordano, Marco Torrisi, Sebastiano Messina, Daniele Giovanni Sfilio, Mario Cacciato
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The continuous increase in grid-connected electronic apparatus has made the reduction of standby power consumption mandatory. While achieving low power consumption in electronic systems with analog controllers is relatively straightforward, standby power in digitally controlled systems suffers from higher circuit complexity. The advantages of digital systems are in the advanced control techniques that can often improve the conversion efficiency of a system. This paper presents two methods and related circuits to reduce standby power in a digitally controlled ac-dc converter. The converter consists of a totem-pole ac-dc Power Factor Corrector and a dc-dc resonant LLC stage. Both stages are controlled by a single microcontroller. Test results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method with standby losses below 150 mW, meeting the CoC V5 Tier 2 (2019/1782) standard in digital adapters.

title: Locally Linear Embedding with Adaptive Projection
authors: Letian Zhang, Siyi Qin, Xinru Zhang, Jingyu Wang
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Locally Linear Embedding (LLE) is a classical manifold learning algorithm that effectively preserves the local structural information of data. However, it suffers from several limitations, including sensitivity to neighborhood parameter selection and susceptibility to noise. To address these issues, this paper proposes an enhanced method called Locally Linear Embedding with Adaptive Projection (LLEAP). Built upon the local linear modeling capability of LLE, the proposed method incorporates a subspace-cleaned data matrix and an adaptive projection matrix. It adaptively updates adjacency relationships based on the current embedding during alternating iterations, thereby enabling dynamic optimization of the graph structure. It unifies the construction of the weight matrix, the computation of the projection matrix, and the embedding process into a single optimization framework. Moreover, an ℓ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf>-norm regularization is imposed to enforce sparsity on the weight matrix, effectively balancing the preservation of local structure and the modeling of global structure. An iterative optimization algorithm is developed to solve the proposed model, and its computational complexity is theoretically analyzed. Experiments on several real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method.

title: Hybrid High-Resolution Digital PWM Based on SERDES & Clock-Phase-Switching Methods
authors: Fabian Pöchmüller, Patrick Lampl, Roberto Petrella
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: High-resolution digital pulse-width-modulation (HRDPWM) is essential for fast-switching power electronics applications. This work presents a novel hybrid method that combines the Serializer/Deserializer (SERDES) primitive, operated at double data rate (DDR) with deterministic clock-phase-switching. This achieves sub-nanosecond resolution without the need for asynchronous delay elements, while allowing the counter logic to operate at comparably low frequencies. The architecture is robust to process-voltage-temperature (PVT) variations by design and has been validated on AMD/Xilinx Artix-7 and UltraScale+ devices. A use case demonstrator is considered to prove the effectiveness of the proposed solution, based on a 1MHz synchronous buck converter, being part of a 48V to 1V sigma-converter. Experimental results proved that the measured timing resolution was as low as 227 ps, with good linearity of duty cycle steps (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> > 0.99996) and low jitter. Even though the individual performance factors are not the peak in literature, the focus was high-class performance across all categories for standard implementation architectures, and fulfilling the reasonable needs of current converter topologies.

title: Modeling of N-Level Current Balancing Transformers for Modular High Power Transformer Winding Building Blocks
authors: Minh Ngo, Aakash Kamalapur, Dong Dong
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: High frequency high power transformers will play a critical role in the future hybrid ac/dc grid of the future in order to provide high efficiency galvanic isolation for datacenters, distributed energy resources, and EV charging. Conventional high frequency high power transformers suffer from low cooling surface area and high loss per unit volume, negating their high frequency size reduction benefits. Modular magnetics concepts (modular winding or modular transformers) have been proposed in the literature to evenly scale cooling surface area and power as more modules are connected in series and or parallel to achieve higher power ratings. However, if current is not shared evenly among modules the aforementioned benefits cannot be realized. To address current sharing issues among modules; current balancing transformers are utilized in this work and a modeling and optimization procedure is proposed based on the cofactor matrix. Using the cofactor matrix current sharing with and without current balancing transformers can be predicted. An optimization procedure based on the cofactor matrix is also proposed to determine current balancing transformer inductance parameters and determine where the current balancing transformer connections need to be made given any number of parallel winding or transformers.

title: Quantitative and Qualitative Assessment of Generative AI for Cross-Modal CT-to-MRI Translation in Spine Imaging
authors: Paul Banahan, Edward McDermott, Molly Godson Treacy, Prateek Mathur, David Caldwell, Eimear Kyle, Aonghus Lawlor, Peter MacMahon
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: The application of Generative AI for cross-modal image-to-image translation holds significant potential in emergency care, particularly for spinal pathologies such as Cauda Equina Syndrome, where timely MRI access is often critical yet not always accessible. In such cases, synthetic MRI translated from the more readily available CT imaging could provide an interim solution to support clinical assessment. This study compares five generative methods for CT-to-MRI translation through both quantitative analysis using standard computer vision metrics and expert qualitative evaluation via a blinded randomised survey. The survey, delivered through a custom-designed web application, examined radiologists' perceptions of image quality, anatomical fidelity and diagnostic confidence. The findings reveal a clear disconnect between quantitative performance and clinical opinion, as models achieving the highest numerical scores were not consistently preferred by experts. Models incorporating anatomical guidance were perceived as more plausible, highlighting the need for evaluation frameworks and model objectives that are more closely aligned with clinical interpretation.

title: Impact of combined ceramic and electrolytic output capacitors on the dynamic response of BOOST voltage regulators
authors: Subodh Khandelwal, Vahid Yousefzadeh, Shubham Narula, Montu Doshi
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Two-stage switching converter solutions are widely used in automotive LED driver applications. This architecture consists of a first stage BOOST voltage regulator (CV) with input connected to the battery and output connected to one or multiple second stage BUCK Constant Current (CC) mode LED drivers. The second stage CC-Mode Buck converters behave as a Constant Power Load (CPL) to the first stage BOOST [3]. In industrial or automotive applications, the BOOST converter output capacitor is normally a combination of ceramic and electrolytic capacitors connected in parallel to optimize the size and cost of the system. The presence of the electrolytic capacitor significantly alters the outer voltage loop gain of the system by shifting the pole locations and adding extra poles and zeros to the closed loop gain. This paper provides an analytical understanding of the impact of paralleling ceramic and electrolytic capacitors on the outer voltage loop gain using the extra-element theorem. The application of extra-element theorem takes the electrolytic capacitor as an extra element in the simpler open loop transfer functions instead of taking the entire closed loop system altogether. The proposed altered simplified model is compared with experimental results obtained from the network analyzer.

title: A PPG-Driven Non-Invasive Platform for Comprehensive Health Analysis
authors: C. Radhika, V. Dharshini, K. Gurusaran, A. Haran, S. Kokilapriya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: The analysis of hemoglobin is a crucial part and plays a major role in deciding the health status of the body as a whole. It is a major part of the diagnosis and identification of blood disorders like anemia, polycythemia, and arrhythmia. The conventional hemoglobin analysis device remains the most invasive compared to all the other blood analyzers that need the blood sample for analysis and operation. This impacts the convenience and ability to determine immediate results by the hemoglobin analyzers. This project, therefore, meets the requirements by developing a non-invasive and portable hemoglobin analysis device that incorporates the pulse oximeter technology concept. The device was set to aid in the determination of critical physiological factors such as the SpO2 level, hemoglobin level, and heart rate using the Photoplethysmography sensor and the ECG sensor, respectively. The results would then be confirmed by the reference values in order to establish a significant level of accuracy and dependability in the results and data acquired by the device. The device was also set to capture the machine learning components essential in the interpretation and analysis process that would also aid in the determination of abnormalities and the early diagnosis of anemia, arrhythmia, and polycythemia.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization for Constant-Power DWPT Under Mutual and Self-Inductance Drift
authors: Yihao Wu, Zongyang Zhu, Chenmin Deng, Ziyang Xu, Alex J. Hanson
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Dynamic wireless power transfer (DWPT) enables in-motion charging, reduces reliance on large onboard batteries, and mitigates range anxiety. During operation, longitudinal misalignment and vertical air-gap variation cause drift in both mutual and self-inductances, detuning the resonant network and degrading power regulation and efficiency. This work presents a multi-objective optimization of a detuned LCC-S compensation network to suppress output power fluctuation and total loss while maintaining zero-voltage switching (ZVS) across the coupling range. A unified analytical model characterizing the LCC-S behavior under inductance drift is developed, and a non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm (NSGA-II) is used to obtain compensation parameters balancing CP regulation and loss. A 270 W prototype validates the method: under 2× variation in mutual inductance and 6% variation in self-inductances, the output power remains within ±3.6% of the nominal value and the peak efficiency reaches 92.3%.

title: ResMatching: Noise-Resilient Computational Super-Resolution via Guided Conditional Flow Matching
authors: Anirban Ray, Vera Galinova, Florian Jug
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Computational Super-Resolution (CSR) in fluorescence microscopy has, despite being an ill-posed problem, a long history [1]. At its very core, CSR is about finding a prior that can be used to extrapolate frequencies in a micrograph that have never been imaged by the image-generating microscope. It stands to reason that, with the advent of better data-driven machine learning techniques, stronger prior can be learned and hence CSR can lead to better results. Here, we present ResMatching, a novel CSR method that uses guided conditional flow matching to learn such improved data-priors. We evaluate ResMatching on 4 diverse biological structures from the BioSR dataset [2] and compare its results against 7 baselines. ResMatching consistently achieves competitive results, demonstrating in all cases the best trade-off between data fidelity and perceptual realism [3]. We observe that CSR using ResMatching is particularly effective in cases where a strong prior is hard to learn, e.g. when the given low-resolution images contain a lot of noise. Additionally, we show that ResMatching can be used to sample from an implicitly learned posterior distribution and that this distribution is calibrated [4] for all tested use-cases, enabling our method to deliver a pixel-wise data-uncertainty term that can guide future users to reject uncertain predictions [5]–[7].

title: RF Triangular Wave Pulse Power Supply Circuit for Plasma Etching Equipment
authors: Mizuki Kawazu, Miyu Kobayashi, Yuichi Ishihama, Tomohiro Furusato, Yoichi Ishizuka
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a practical circuit topology and control method for a high-frequency power supply used in plasma etching systems. As semiconductor device dimensions continue to shrink, highly precise etching technology is required, and control of the ion energy distribution has become increasingly important. In particular, the temporal slope of the power-supply voltage waveform significantly affects the ion energy density. Therefore, this study focuses on improving the ion energy density by employing a negative-polarity triangular waveform as the power-supply voltage. Conventional studies use power amplifiers, but these may suffer from low efficiency. Therefore, we propose a switching-power-converter-based topology and control scheme that allows arbitrary adjustment of both the amplitude and frequency of the output voltage waveform. While a transformer-based configuration was introduced in a previous digest, this paper presents a new, simpler, and more efficient topology that does not require a transformer. Using a prototype circuit, we demonstrate that a highly linear triangular-wave output can be achieved at approximately 400 kHz.

title: Comparative Performance Analysis of Terahertz Antennas with Different Substrate Materials based on Graphene Patch Antenna
authors: Rida Biqaa, Asma Khabba, Ibtissam Bahlaouane, Saida Ibnyaich, Hassan Belahrach, Abdelouhab Zeroual
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: This study investigates a rectangular microstrip patch antenna designed for terahertz (THz) applications, specifically targeting biomedical use. To optimize the performance of the proposed structure, we analyze the impact of substrate material selection on the antenna’s properties. Using CST Studio Suite, we examine how variations in relative permittivity (ϵ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf>) influence performance metrics such as reflection coefficient, gain, bandwidth, and radiation patterns. The proposed design is a multi-band microstrip antenna optimized for the THz regime. Among the various substrates tested, Polyamide is highlighted as a primary candidate due to its favorable chemical and mechanical properties, characterized by a relative permittivity of 3.5, a thickness of 10 μm, and a loss tangent of 0.0027. The antenna configuration consists of a 100 × 100 × 10 μm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> substrate with a graphene-based radiating patch (0.01 μm thickness, 64 μm length, and 50 μm width). A full copper ground plane of the same length and width and a thickness of 0.01 μm is employed. Operating at a center frequency of 1.88 THz, the antenna demonstrates a wide bandwidth ranging from 1.6 THz to 3.2 THz. The primary objective of this study is to analyze the performance of the material substrate, for instance, Polyamide (ϵ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 3.5; with loss tangent 0.0027). SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>(ϵ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 4), Gallium Arsenide (with loss tangent of 12.94), Quartz (ϵ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 3.75; with loss tangent of 0.0004), Silicon (ϵ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 11.9; with 0.00004 loss tangent), RO 5880 (ϵ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 2.2; with loss tangent of 0.0009), RO 3003 (ϵ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 3; with 0.001 loss tangent), FR4 (ϵ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 4.3; with loss tangent 0.025), RO 4003C (ϵ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 3.55) and select the materials that can give satisfying results, such as reflection coefficient, gain, bandwidth, and radiation pattern.

title: Optimal Distribution of Pole Area and Its Angular Separation on Braking Performance in Eddy Current Brakes
authors: Sri Krishna Roshan, Sethupathy Subramanian
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Eddy current braking systems provide fast response, minimal wear and tear, and contactless braking. The brake action arising from the electromagnetic force, and it can be directly controlled by the electrical systems in the electric vehicles (EVs). Thus, they provide a direct and fast control of the braking action compared to mechanical braking. In this work, the focus is on arriving at an optimal distribution of pole area and the angular separation between the poles. Given the complex nature of the problem, the investigations are carried out using numerical simulations based on the finite element method. An extensive set of simulation is performed to arrive at correlating factors. The results so far indicate that fewer number of poles might perform better than more number of poles with the same total pole area. When considering angular separation between the poles, the braking torque decreases as the separation between poles increases. The findings here provide guidelines for enhancing braking performance in eddy current brakes.

title: Semi-Supervised Object Detection for Remote Sensing Images with Adaptive Threshold
authors: Tong Zhao, Qiang Fang, Xiao Du, Xin Xu, Yujun Zeng, Yixing Lan
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Semi-supervised object detection has witnessed remarkable advancements in common scenes. However, for remote sensing images, typical methods often rely on fixed confidence thresholds for pseudo-label selection, which will suffer from suboptimal filtering performance and biased pseudo-label generation due to the severe class imbalance in remote sensing scenarios. Therefore, we proposes Sparse Teacher, an adaptive threshold-based method to addresses above challenge. Specifically, a Gaussian distribution-based adaptive threshold strategy is designed to dynamically generate class-specific confidence thresholds, mitigating the bias caused by uniform fixed thresholds and balancing the pseudo-label quality across classes. Additionally, we design an aspect ratio-guided weighted loss function to enhance the detection performance for objects with extreme aspect ratios. Experimental results on the DOTA-v1.5 dataset demonstrate that Sparse Teacher outperforms typical semi-supervised object detection methods. Specifically, under 10%, 20%, and 30% labeled data settings, it achieves 48.84%, 55.06%, and 58.02% mAP, respectively. These results outperform typical semi-supervised object detection approaches, such as Soft Teacher and Dense Teacher, highlighting the effectiveness of our proposed method.

title: Design and Comparative Analysis of Fully Soft-switched and Hard-switched Current Injection Circuit for Unfolding-based AC-DC Converters
authors: Shubhangi Gurudiwan, Aditya Zade, Hongjie Wang, Regan Zane
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a fully soft-switched third-harmonic current injection (CI) circuit for unfolding-based ac-dc converter topologies. The proposed zero-voltage/zero-current (ZVS/ZCS)-assisting circuit enables soft-switching of the CI circuit with minimal additional components. A comparative analysis of the thermal performance and efficiency is presented for a hard-switched CI circuit and two soft-switching methods applied to the CI circuit: high ripple-based ZVS and ZVS/ZCS-assisted operation. The comparative analysis is validated through hardware testing on a 5 kW ac-dc converter prototype comprising a three-phase Unfolder, CI circuit, and a dual active bridge converter with an LCL resonant tank. The observed ZVS range, efficiency, and thermal performance align well with the analysis.

title: Reconstruction Algorithm Based on Multi-Harmonic Feature Fusion and Enhanced U-Net for Low-Concentration Magnetic Particle Imaging
authors: Yi Man, Ziye Ma, Yimeng Li
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: To address weak signals and severe background artifacts in low-concentration magnetic particle imaging, this paper proposes a noise-resilient reconstruction algorithm based on multi-harmonic feature fusion and an enhanced U-Net(MH-U-Net). Our algorithm introduces enhancements in both the signal and image domains to improve the reconstruction quality. In the signal domain, the algorithm performs a weighted fusion of the imaginary parts of the 3rd and 5th harmonics, utilizes filtered back projection to preserve highfrequency edges, and employs a grid search to determine the optimal weights. In the image domain, an optimized U-Net architecture driven by a joint loss function is employed for precise denoising. Experimental results demonstrate that our algorithm achieves high structural similarity on the training set, demonstrating a substantial performance improvement compared to the baseline algorithm provided by the official challenge dataset. It realizes MPI image reconstruction under low tracer concentrations, facilitating the biomedical applications of MPI.

title: A New Hybrid Model-Driven Machine Learning Approach for Cyber Attack Detection in Autonomous E-Mobility Systems
authors: Praneeth Reddy Pesala, Durga Prasad Pilli, Deepak Ronanki, Apparao Dekka
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Autonomous e-mobility systems offer advanced functionalities, including remote monitoring and control, self-driving capability, vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) communication, and vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication. These features enhance the operational autonomy, efficiency, and responsiveness of electric vehicles (EVs). However, these capabilities also introduce cybersecurity vulnerabilities, particularly false data injection (FDI) attacks, which can compromise the reliability and integrity of critical components, such as the electric drive system (EDS). This paper proposes a hybrid cyber attack detection framework that combines model-derived features with a machine learning (ML) classifier to detect FDI-type data integrity attacks in the EDS. The proposed methodology is validated through MATLAB-Simulink under various FDI-type attack scenarios. Furthermore, the key performance characteristics of the proposed method are comprehensively analyzed and demonstrated. The results indicate that the proposed approach achieves high detection accuracy and robustness, thereby improving the cyber-security resilience of autonomous e-mobility systems.

title: Introduction of a Learned Prior to Passive Cavitation Imaging
authors: Célestine Lachambre, Bruno Gilles, Barbara Nicolas, François Varray, Jean-Christophe Béra, Adrian Basarab
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: High-intensity focused ultrasound can induce cavitation, enabling therapeutic applications, such as sonoporation, targeted drug delivery, or histotripsy, which require accurate monitoring. Passive acoustic mapping, known as passive delay-and-sum (DAS), is a common cavitation monitoring technique but suffers from limited axial resolution. Recently, a regularized inverse approach, cross-spectral matrix fitting (CMF), incorporating prior knowledge into the reconstruction process, such as the group sparse characteristics of cavitation power maps (CMF-spTV), was proposed.
